1.synthesis: Paul opens with a triple self-description that establishes both his authority and his subject: servant of Christ Jesus, called to be an apostle, set apart for the gospel of God. Calvin notes the ancient epistolary convention of naming the author first, but finds Paul's three-fold identification theologically loaded — he does not merely name himself but defines his standing and mission. The title doulos (slave) is significant: Ellicott observes that while English "servant" softens the word, Paul means voluntary slavery to Christ, a paradox in which the highest dignity is found in the deepest submission. Wesley catches the internal economy: "called to be an apostle" means made an apostle by that very calling — the divine call is simultaneously the divine authorization. "Set apart for the gospel" echoes Galatians 1:15 and the prophetic tradition of Isaiah: Paul is the servant separated from birth for a specific mission. Barnes observes that the epistle is addressed to the most powerful city of the ancient world, and that Paul's credentials for such an address rest entirely on his divine commission, not on learning, lineage, or social standing. The opening verse thus enacts the epistle's central argument: grace, not human distinction, determines everything.

voices.html: The inscription of the Pauline Epistles is according to the manner of the ancients, both Greeks and Romans, who were wont to prefix their name. But Paul makes his introduction in such a way as to proclaim the doctrine of his Epistle. He calls himself a servant of Christ, and declares that he was set apart for the Gospel — not a self-appointed teacher, but one separated by God for a specific and gracious mission.

Servant — more strictly, here as elsewhere in the New Testament, slave; and yet not wrongly translated "servant," because the compulsory and degrading side of service is not put forward. The idea of service, devotion, and belonging to another is the main thought. Paul's voluntary slavery to Christ is paradoxically the source of his apostolic freedom and authority.

The Epistle to the Romans has been regarded as the most difficult portion of the New Testament. Paul, addressing the most powerful city of the ancient world, grounds his authority entirely on divine commission: servant of Christ Jesus, called to be an apostle, set apart for the gospel. No human credential is offered; the divine appointment is itself the whole warrant.

2.synthesis: The gospel Paul has been set apart for is not a novelty but the fulfillment of ancient promises: God had pledged it beforehand through his prophets in the holy scriptures. Calvin notes this grounds the faith of the gospel in antiquity — Christ came not unexpectedly but as the culmination of a long prophetic tradition. The argument is apologetic as well as historical: the suspicion of novelty always subtracts from a doctrine's credibility, and Paul forestalls it by anchoring the gospel in the Hebrew scriptures. Ellicott draws attention to a fine syntactical detail: the absence of the definite article before "holy scriptures" is not accidental but expresses that the character of the scriptures is in view — they are holy writings, a class of literature set apart. Wesley ties verse 2 back to verse 1: the promise and the accomplishment illuminate each other, the Old Testament promises gaining their full meaning from the fulfillment, and the gospel gaining its authority from the promise. Barnes catalogs the prophetic references: Deuteronomy 18:18, Isaiah 9:6–7, 53:1, 61:1, Jeremiah 23:5 — a sweep of prediction that the apostle regards as unified testimony pointing to Christ.

voices.html: As the suspicion of being new subtracts much from the authority of a doctrine, he confirms the faith of the gospel by antiquity. Christ came not unexpectedly, nor without the testimony of the prophets prepared before him. God promised this gospel beforehand through his prophets, so that the whole tradition of Scripture points toward and is illuminated by the one gospel now proclaimed.

Which he promised before — more correctly, which he promised before by his prophets in holy writ. There is a nicety of meaning expressed by the absence of the article before this last phrase: the reference is not merely to these scriptures as a known collection but to their quality — they are holy writings, prophetic documents of a sacred character that bear divine testimony.

Which he promised before — of old time, frequently, solemnly. And the promise and accomplishment confirm each other. Deuteronomy 18:18; Isaiah 9:6, 7; 53:1; 61:1; Jeremiah 23:5. The gospel does not contradict the law and the prophets; it fulfills them, and in being fulfilled it validates them.

3.synthesis: The gospel is explicitly and centrally about God's Son, and Paul immediately asserts the double nature of that Son: according to the flesh, he was born of the seed of David; according to the Spirit of holiness, he was declared Son of God in power by the resurrection of the dead. The first half of the Christological statement anchors Jesus in the covenant history of Israel — the Davidic promise of 2 Samuel 7. Calvin calls Romans 1:3 "a remarkable passage" establishing that the whole gospel is included in Christ: any move away from him moves away from the gospel itself. Clarke develops the two-nature pattern: the human nature is mentioned first because the divine was prior in his essential being — the incarnation is the condescension, and the resurrection the vindication. Barnes explains the grammar: "concerning his Son" picks up the thread from verse 1 ("gospel of God") and shows the content of that gospel to be the person of the Son in his two states. Wesley notes both natures are mentioned, with the human mentioned first, but that the divine preexisted and underlies both.

voices.html: This is a remarkable passage, by which we are taught that the whole gospel is included in Christ, so that if any man removes one step from Christ, he withdraws himself from the gospel. He who is here called the Son of God has both natures: born of the seed of David according to the flesh, and declared Son of God in power — the same person in two states, the humiliation and the exaltation.

Concerning his Son — that is, the Gospel relates every thing concerning the conception, birth, preaching, miracles, passion, death, resurrection, and ascension of Jesus Christ, who was of the seed of David according to the flesh. Both the natures of our Saviour are here mentioned; but the human is mentioned first because the divine was prior in his essential being — the incarnation is the entry point of the gospel story.

Concerning his Son. This is connected with verse 1: the gospel of God concerning his Son. The design of the gospel was to make a communication relative to his Son. The word "concerning" shows that the Son is the substance and subject of the gospel — the gospel has no content apart from him, and he has no proper meaning apart from the gospel that announces him.

4.synthesis: The Greek word horisthentos — governing "declared" in most translations — generates the chapter's most significant interpretive dispute. Calvin surveys the patristic options ("proved," "demonstrated," "exhibited") and settles on a reading that includes both the proof and the appointment: the resurrection did not merely prove what was already known but constituted a public declaration of what had been true from eternity. RWP explains the verbal root: horizō means to bound, to define, to appoint, and suggests that Jesus was appointed or marked out as Son of God in power — not that he became something he had not been, but that his divine sonship was publicly installed in a new mode of power. Ellicott stresses "with power" as the key addition: Christ was Son of God before the resurrection, but now declared to be so with power — the resurrection is the installation of the Son in his regnant, victorious mode. Barnes catalogues the patristic and modern options and settles on "declared" as preserving the proof dimension. Clarke finds in horisthentos the idea of bounding and defining — the resurrection fixes the identity of Jesus as Son of God with irrefutable clarity. The phrase "Spirit of holiness" (not "Holy Spirit") is typically read as referring either to the Holy Spirit or to Christ's own divine nature — the former is more common among these commentators.

voices.html: Declared, ὁρισθέντος. Some of the ancients have given to this verb the meaning of "proved" or "demonstrated." But the resurrection of Christ is not simply a proof: it was the installation of the Son in his glory. He who was Son from eternity was appointed and declared to be Son of God with power — the resurrection is the public investiture of what had been eternally true.

With power — that is, in a transcendent and superhuman manner. According to the spirit of holiness — in antithesis to "according to the flesh," and therefore coming where we should expect "in his divine nature." The resurrection is the event by which Christ's divine sonship was installed in a new mode of power. He was Son of God before; now he is declared Son of God in power — regnant, victorious, enthroned.

Who was declared (τοῦ ὁρισθέντος). Articular participle, first aorist passive of ὁρίζω, to mark off by boundaries, to appoint, to designate. He was the Son of God in his preincarnate state, but the resurrection marks him out, appoints him publicly as Son of God in power — the same person, but now in his risen and regnant mode.

4.key_tension: Ὁρισθέντος is read as "declared/proven" by most (preserving the public-demonstration sense of the resurrection) or "appointed/defined" by Calvin and RWP (preserving the installative force of the verb); both readings agree Christ is Son of God from eternity, but differ on whether the resurrection proves or publicly installs that identity.

5.synthesis: Through Christ, Paul and his co-workers have received grace and apostleship — for the purpose of bringing about obedience of faith among all the nations for the sake of Christ's name. The linking of "grace and apostleship" is important: Calvin notes that grace here refers to the peculiar qualification for the office, not merely the general favor of God. Without the grace, Paul could not have been an apostle; without the apostleship, the grace would have taken a different form. Wesley reads the phrase collectively: "we" includes the other apostles, who together received the endowment and the commission. Ellicott stresses the target: the obedience of faith that Paul is commissioned to produce is not the obedience of law but the loyal submission that flows from trusting Christ as Lord. RWP identifies "obedience of faith" as a subjective genitive — the obedience which springs from faith — so that faith and obedience are not alternatives but the same act described from different angles. Barnes traces the authority: it was derived not from human appointment or tradition but directly from the Lord Jesus, the risen Son of God just described.

voices.html: Having completed his definition of the gospel, which he introduced for the recommendation of his office, he now returns to speak of his own call. "Grace and apostleship" — the grace is the peculiar influence and qualification the office requires; without it, no man could be an apostle. The obedience of faith among all nations is the declared purpose of the whole apostolic mission.

By whom we have received — I and the other apostles. Grace and apostleship — the favour to be an apostle, and qualifications for it. For obedience to the faith in all nations — that all nations might be brought to the obedience of faith, to believe and obey. For his name — for the sake of Christ, and to his glory. The name is the person and the honor of the risen Son of God.

Unto obedience of faith (εἰς ὑπακοὴν πίστεως). Subjective genitive as in 16:26: the obedience which springs from faith. Faith and obedience are not two things but one thing described from its inner origin (faith) and its outer expression (obedience). This is what Paul's apostleship aims to produce in all the nations.

6.synthesis: Among the Gentile nations who have been called to the obedience of faith, the Romans are included — they too are the called of Jesus Christ. The verse is a brief pivot from the general scope of the apostolic mission (all nations) to the specific addressees. Ellicott notes that "among whom" links smoothly to verse 5's "all the nations" — the Romans are part of this wider Gentile scope. Clarke explains the pastoral significance: "ye Romans are all invited" — the call is not to a racial or national aristocracy but extends to every person in that cosmopolitan city. Wesley is pointed: Paul gives the Romans no preeminence above the other nations; they are called like all the others, by the same gospel and the same grace. Barnes infers from the verse that the Roman church was predominantly Gentile, though Jews were certainly present — the shape of the letter throughout confirms this. RWP notes the genitive construction: "called of Jesus Christ" might be read as a predicate genitive (belonging to Jesus Christ by virtue of the call) or as a genitive of origin (called by Jesus Christ), but both readings point to the same reality: the Romans belong to the risen Lord by his gracious summons.

voices.html: Among whom are ye also. Among these Gentile churches, to which I am specially commissioned, you Romans too are called to the same obedience of faith. The call extends to the heart of the empire; no city, however proud or powerful, lies outside the scope of the apostolic commission or the reach of the grace it offers.

Ye are the called — ye Romans are all invited to believe in Christ Jesus, for the salvation of your souls; and to you, with the rest, my apostolical mission extends. The gospel does not make distinctions of nationality or rank among its recipients: all who are called are called by the same grace, on the same terms, to the same Lord.

Among whom — that is, among the Gentiles who had become obedient to the Christian faith, in accordance with the design of the gospel. This proves that the church at Rome was made up in part, at least, of Gentiles, and was the class to whom Paul regarded himself as specially sent as the apostle of the nations.

7.synthesis: The salutation addresses "all who are in Rome, beloved of God, called to be saints," and the greeting formula — "grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" — is the standard Pauline opening, but each word carries weight. Calvin observes the theological sequence: first God makes us objects of his love (beloved of God), then he calls us (called saints) — the love precedes and produces the call. Clarke explains the social breadth: the letter goes to all who are in Rome without distinction of status; and "saints" does not denote a special category of especially holy persons but is the general term for all Christians, those set apart by God's call. Wesley notes that the recipients are scattered throughout the great city, not yet formed into a single organized church. Barnes stresses that "grace" in Paul's greeting is not a mere pleasantry but a wish for the actual divine favor, and "peace" the comprehensive Hebrew shalom — all well-being and blessedness. Ellicott notes a textual curiosity: one important manuscript omits "in Rome," which has been used (along with a similar omission in verse 15) to argue that the letter originally circulated as a general epistle.

voices.html: By this happy arrangement he sets forth what there is in us worthy of commendation: the Lord through his own kindness first made us the objects of his favor and love, and then called us. The love precedes the call; the call is the means by which the love reaches us. "Called to be saints" — they are saints not by their own achievement but by divine summons and by the sanctification that accompanies it.

Called to be saints — invited to become holy persons, by believing the Gospel and receiving the gifts of the Holy Ghost. The word saint does not designate a spiritual aristocracy but the whole body of Christians: those set apart by the divine call, consecrated to God, and in the process of being made holy by his grace.

To all that be in Rome. That is, to all who bear the Christian name. Grace to you and peace. This is the customary salutation of Paul. "Grace" means favour — the divine goodwill and its practical expression. "Peace" in the Hebrew sense means every kind of blessing. Together they name the whole of what God gives in the gospel: his undeserved favor and its fruit in human life.

8.synthesis: Paul's thanksgiving for the Roman church is the first movement of the letter's body: he thanks God through Jesus Christ for all of them, because their faith is spoken of throughout the whole world. Calvin notes that this is simultaneously a commendation of the Romans and a demonstration of Paul's pastoral love — he would not be so anxious for their welfare if he did not greatly love them. Ellicott emphasizes the mediatorial structure: Paul thanks God through Jesus Christ, because Christ is the medium through whom God has been brought into close relation to man; thanksgiving too must pass through him. Wesley observes that of all spiritual affections Paul expresses in his letters, thankfulness is chief and most consistently expressed — the opening of nearly every letter. Barnes notes the striking claim: the faith of the Roman church has been "spoken of throughout the whole world." Rome was the center of the empire; travelers moved in and out constantly; news of a remarkable Christian community at Rome would naturally spread along all the empire's roads. Clarke remarks that this is part of Paul's strategy of conciliation: by praising their faith publicly, he prepares their receptive disposition for the doctrines he is about to unfold.

voices.html: He proves his love by its effects: had he not greatly loved them, he would not have so anxiously commended them to the Lord, and especially he would not have so ardently desired to promote their welfare. The thanksgiving for their faith is simultaneously a commendation — he honors what God has done among them — and an expression of the pastoral love that motivates the whole letter.

I thank my God through Jesus Christ. How can the Apostle be said to thank God through Jesus Christ? Christ is the medium through whom God has been brought into close relation to man, and through whom man now approaches God. Thanksgiving, like prayer, is offered in his name: he is the way to the Father, and not only the way of salvation but the way of gratitude.

I thank my God. The God whom I worship and serve. The faith of the Romans was spoken of throughout the whole world. Rome was the capital of the empire; travelers were constantly going and coming; the news of a Christian church in that city would naturally spread along every road. The reputation of the Roman faith was itself a form of the gospel's advance in the world.

9.synthesis: Paul appeals to God as his witness that he continually mentions the Romans in his prayers — and the character of the appeal reveals something about Paul's understanding of his own ministry. Ellicott notes that the word for "serve" here (latreuō) is the term for specifically religious service, the devotion paid to God: Paul's apostolic work is itself a form of worship. Clarke develops this: latreuō originally referred to hired service but came to denote the highest form of religious reverence; Paul worships God with every faculty of his soul, not merely his body. Wesley is brief and pointed: "in my spirit — not only with my body, but with my inmost soul." Calvin observes that it is not probable we study the benefit of those whom we are not ready to assist by our labors; Paul's prayer for the Romans shows his love, and his desire to visit them shows his love in a more concrete form. Barnes explains the strength of the oath-like appeal: the reason for so solemn a statement is to prove the depth of his interest in their welfare, an interest that might otherwise seem impossible from one who had never met them.

voices.html: He proves his love by its effects; for had he not greatly loved them, he would not have so anxiously commended them to the Lord, and especially he would not have so ardently desired to promote their welfare by his presence. The oath-like appeal to God as witness carries this weight: Paul stakes his credibility on the sincerity of his prayer for a church he has never visited.

Proof that the Apostle takes this lively interest in the Roman Church is conveyed through a solemn adjuration. Whom I serve — the word for "serve" is strictly used for voluntary service paid to God, especially in sacred rites. Paul's apostolic mission is not employment but worship: he serves God in his spirit, and the service takes the form of preaching the gospel.

God, whom I serve — as an apostle. In my spirit — not only with my body, but with my inmost soul. In the gospel — by preaching it. That I make mention of you always in my prayers — always, not occasionally. The constancy of the prayer is itself the evidence of the love behind it.

10.synthesis: The prayer for the Romans includes a specific petition: that God might grant Paul a prosperous journey to visit them. Calvin notes that the fact of the prayer shows how earnest Paul was to benefit them; he was not merely spiritually moved but practically committed — he prayed for the opportunity and would take it when given. Ellicott draws attention to the cluster of particles in the Greek: ei pōs ēdē pote — "if by any means, now at length" — an accumulation expressing long-deferred longing, the frustration of good desire held back by providence. Wesley echoes this: the accumulation of particles declares the strength of his desire. Barnes offers the practical explanation of the prayer: earnest desire about any matter expresses itself in prayer to God; if Paul truly wanted to go, he would naturally ask the One who controls events. Clarke observes that Paul had long been burdened with this desire — "always" in verse 9 covers a continuous span of prayer — and the word "prosperous" indicates he did not presume on God's timing but submitted the means and the moment to God's direction.

voices.html: As it is not probable that we study the benefit of those whom we are not ready to assist by our labors, he now adds, after having said that he was anxious for their welfare, that he showed by the proof of his effort that he truly desired this — making continual request to God that he might have a prosperous journey to them in the will of God.

If by any means now at length — note this accumulation of particles, denoting the earnestness of his desire. "All this time I have been longing to come to you, and now at last I hope that it may be granted." The repeated deferral has not extinguished the longing; if anything it has intensified it, and the prayer reflects both the desire and the submission to God's ordering of the means.

Making request — it was his earnest desire to see them, and he presented the subject before God. If by any means — this shows the earnest desire which he had to see them, and implies that he did not know when or how it would be possible. The whole phrase expresses the feeling of a man who has long and fervently desired something and wonders whether it will ever come to pass.

11.synthesis: Paul's desire to visit has a specific purpose: he longs to impart some spiritual gift to the Romans that they might be established. Calvin notes that while he might have confirmed their faith by his written doctrine, counsel is better taken from one present — the personal encounter does what the letter cannot. The phrase "spiritual gift" has generated discussion: Ellicott reads it as referring not to the extraordinary charismatic gifts of 1 Corinthians 12–14 but to the natural overflow of blessing from one believer's presence on another — the spiritual enrichment of personal communion with a mature apostle. Clarke tends toward the charismatic reading: the extraordinary gifts of the Spirit, given through the laying on of apostolic hands. Barnes holds both in view: it may refer to spiritual gifts in the technical sense, or to the blessings that come through instruction and mutual encouragement — the context of verse 12 suggests the latter. Wesley observes that the Corinthians, who had enjoyed Paul's presence, were richer in such gifts than the Romans who had not; personal apostolic ministry had a generative effect on the Spirit's work in a congregation.

voices.html: He might, indeed, while absent, have confirmed their faith by his doctrine; but as advice is better taken from one present, he had a desire to be with them. He explains what his object was — not personal satisfaction but the establishment of their faith, the specific purpose that governed his desire and shaped his prayer for the prosperous journey.

That I may impart unto you some spiritual gift — such gifts as would naturally flow to one Christian from the personal presence and warm sympathy of another; in St. Paul's case enriched by the peculiar endowments of his apostleship. The word "some" is modest; he does not presume to specify what gift, but trusts that personal presence will convey what the letter cannot.

For I long to see you — earnestly desire to see you. That I may impart to you — share with you. Some spiritual gift — to the end you may be established. The purpose is confirmatory: he does not come merely to observe or to be honored, but to build up the faith of a church he has never met, by means of whatever grace God will distribute through him.

12.synthesis: Paul immediately qualifies what he has just said in a gracious act of self-correction: it is not merely that he will impart to them, but that they will be mutually comforted and encouraged by each other's faith. Calvin notes that this correction shows Paul's pastoral wisdom — he does not occupy the place of a teacher so exclusively as to exclude being taught and comforted in return. Ellicott calls it "a beautiful touch of true courtesy": having said he will give them something, he hastily draws back and corrects himself, adding that the giving will be mutual. Wesley observes the rhetorical effect: Paul not only associates the Romans with himself but virtually prefers them before himself, a style very different from worldly greatness. Barnes draws the practical application: Paul sought the communion of saints not merely to dispense but to receive — the mutual edification of Christians assembled together was the expected experience of any gathering, even an apostolic one. Clarke reads verse 12 as a fine example of the apostle's address: by suggesting that they could comfort him, he shows he arrogates nothing to himself and treats them as equals in the grace of God.

voices.html: Being not satisfied with his modest statement that he will impart to them, he qualifies it, and shows that he did not so occupy the place of a teacher as to exclude wishing also to learn from them. He thus professes his desire to be mutually comforted by the faith both of them and of himself — an acknowledgment that the apostle is a receiver as well as a giver in the fellowship of the gospel.

That is, that I may be comforted — a beautiful touch of true courtesy. He is anxious to see them, that he may impart to them some spiritual gift. But no! He hastily draws back and corrects himself. He will not merely give; the giving will be mutual. His faith will be a help to them; their faith will be a help to him. The relationship of the apostle and the church is reciprocal, not merely hierarchical.

That I may be comforted together with you — it was not merely to confirm them that Paul wished to come. He sought the communion of saints; he expected to be himself edified and strengthened. The mutual faith that each brings to such an encounter is itself the vehicle of divine encouragement, and no rank in the church exempts one from the need of it.

13.synthesis: Paul does not want them to be unaware that he has often intended to come to them — and has been hindered every time. This is a statement of frustrated purpose combined with a statement of intended fruit: he has planned the visit often precisely because he wants to have some harvest among them as among the other Gentiles. Calvin notes the problem of credibility: the repeated deferral could look like neglect, and Paul explains it by attributing the hindrance to external causes beyond his control, not to lack of desire. Clarke suggests that the Greek ekōlythēn ("I was hindered") indicates providential prevention — God's ordering of events, not a passive impediment. Barnes observes that "among the other Gentiles" confirms the Roman church's predominantly Gentile character and situates Rome within the scope of Paul's universal Gentile mission. Wesley finds pastoral significance in the form of address — "brethren" — a term he calls "frequent, holy, simple, sweet, and yet grand"; Paul's letters prefer this over individual names because it emphasizes belonging to the family of faith. The verse thus functions as both explanation and motivation: the long delay was not willful, and the harvest-desire remains undiminished.

voices.html: What he has hitherto testified — that he continually requested of the Lord that he might visit them — might have appeared vain, and could not have obtained credit, had he neglected to seize the opportunity when offered. He forestalls this objection by noting that he has often intended to come and has been hindered — not by choice but by the ordering of events under God's providence.

That oftentimes I purposed — how often he had purposed this we cannot ascertain. The fact, however, showed his strong desire to see them, and his deep interest in their welfare. Even as among the other Gentiles — this proves the Roman church was largely Gentile. Paul expected the same result of his ministry in Rome that he had elsewhere: a harvest of faith among the nations for the glory of Christ.

Oftentimes I purposed (πολλάκις προεθέμην). Second aorist middle of προτίθημι, old verb to place, to propose to oneself; in N.T. only here, 3:25, and Ephesians 1:9. And was hindered (ἐκωλύθην). First aorist passive of κωλύω, old verb to hinder. No agent is named, but the passive construction points to divine providence as the controlling cause.

14.synthesis: Paul declares himself a debtor — one under obligation — to both Greeks and barbarians, to both wise and foolish. This is the theological basis for his readiness to preach the gospel even in Rome. Calvin explains the dual categorization: "Greeks and barbarians" was the standard Greek division of all humanity (the Greeks used "barbarian" for all non-Greek speakers, including Jews), while "wise and foolish" cuts across the same humanity along the axis of education. Clarke notes the historical specificity: in the apostle's day the Romans were effectively included in the "Greek" category culturally, so the division means all peoples. Wesley reads it as inclusive of all humanity without exception. Barnes provides the crucial clarification: Paul's debt is not one contracted by receiving favors from these groups — they had conferred nothing on him. His debt is the obligation of one entrusted with a message: he is in debt because God has given him the gospel and commissioned him to deliver it. The debtor-creditor image is Paul's way of saying that not preaching would be a theft — withholding what he owes.

voices.html: Those whom he means by the Greeks and the Barbarians he afterwards explains by adding "wise and unlearned." He means all humanity without exception. And the ground of the debt is not what he has received from them but what he has received for them: the gospel entrusted to him for all the nations makes him a debtor to every human being who has not yet heard it.

To the Greeks and the barbarians — he includes the Romans under the Greeks; so that this division comprises all nations. Both to the wise, and the unwise — for there were unwise even among the Greeks, and wise even among the barbarians. The obligation to preach is universal because the gospel is universal; no category of humanity lies outside the debt.

I am debtor. This does not mean that they had conferred any favour on him which bound him to make this return, but that he was under obligation to preach the gospel to all to whom it was sent. God had given him the commission; the commission included all nations. To withhold the message from any group would be to default on the trust.

15.synthesis: The readiness to preach is presented as the personal dimension of the obligation stated in verse 14: "so, as much as in me is, I am ready to preach the gospel to you also who are in Rome." The qualification "as much as in me is" is important: Paul claims readiness only for what depends on his own will, not on the external ordering of events that has repeatedly delayed him. Ellicott makes this explicit — the clause acknowledges that readiness is not the same as the ability to act; Providence must still open the door. Calvin reads to kat' eme prothymon (literally "as to me is readiness") as a strong expression of personal eagerness: the inner disposition is fully set toward this goal, whatever the outward obstacles. Barnes develops the courage implied: Rome was the world's greatest city, the center of power and civilization, and it would take considerable confidence to announce the gospel there as something superior to all that Rome had produced. RWP notes that the Greek construction is awkward — literally "thus the according-to-me affair is ready" — but the meaning is clear: Paul's personal readiness is complete and undivided.

voices.html: As to me is readiness — I am ready, or I am prepared. He expresses his personal eagerness: the inner disposition is fully set toward this goal, whatever the outward obstacles have been and may be. The qualification ("as much as in me is") acknowledges that the timing and the means remain in God's hands, but the will is wholly engaged.

So, as much as in me is, I am ready to preach the gospel, as to the other Gentiles, so also at Rome. The clause "as much as in me is" is significant: it limits the claim to what depends on his own will and not upon the external ruling of events by God. He is ready; he has been hindered; he prays for the opening. The readiness and the Providence are both real.

So, as much as in me is, I am ready. As far as opportunity may be offered and according to my ability. I am prepared to preach among you, and to show the power of the gospel, even in the heart of the empire. The city of Rome might seem to require something more impressive than a simple proclamation; Paul knows otherwise, and his readiness is grounded in the gospel's own power.

16.synthesis: "For I am not ashamed of the gospel" — the great declaration that launches the epistle's argument. Calvin treats it as an anticipatio: Paul forestalls the taunts of the ungodly by declaring in advance that he does not share the world's contempt for the gospel. Ellicott develops the social context: this will be said in the metropolis of the world, the city that prizes power and culture — and the gospel that Paul brings is from a crucified provincial Jew. That Paul is not ashamed is therefore not a casual assertion but a costly one. Wesley points to the reason: to the world, the gospel is folly and weakness; at Rome especially, at the head of imperial power, the apostle who preaches a crucified Savior stands in a position that the world regards as embarrassing. Barnes notes that Paul had been persecuted and driven from city after city; by human accounting he had every reason to be ashamed. Yet he declares himself unashamed. The reason he gives is the power of God unto salvation: the gospel does not merely communicate ideas about salvation — it is the operative means by which God exercises saving power. RWP's note that Paul had seen the "dynamite" of God at work is apt: the gospel is potent in ways that make the world's assessments irrelevant. "To the Jew first" preserves the historical priority of the covenant people without limiting the gospel's scope.

voices.html: This is an anticipation of an objection; for he declares beforehand that he cared not for the taunts of the ungodly. He thus provides a way for himself to pronounce an eulogy on the gospel, which the world regarded as a disgrace. The proud, the powerful, the philosophically sophisticated — all regarded the crucified Christ as a stumbling block or folly. Paul declares himself free from that shame.

The Apostle will not be ashamed of his mission, even in the metropolis of the world. He cannot be ashamed of a scheme so beneficent and so grand. The gospel that he preaches is a mighty agency of God for human salvation — the power of God, not a human opinion or a philosophical school. Against such power the empire's pride is simply irrelevant.

For I am not ashamed. The Jews had cast him off as an apostate; by the wise among the Gentiles he had been persecuted and driven from place to place; and to say that you believed in a crucified man as the Savior of the world was to be regarded as weak and credulous. Yet Paul is not ashamed — because the gospel is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes. Power answers shame.

17.synthesis: The reason the gospel is the power of God for salvation is unpacked in this verse: it is because in it the righteousness of God is revealed from faith to faith — and this is confirmed by the ancient prophetic testimony of Habakkuk 2:4, "the righteous shall live by faith." The verse is perhaps the most theologically consequential in the epistle. Calvin stresses that "righteousness of God" is not God's essential attribute of justice but the righteousness God bestows — the status God grants and which he accepts as the ground of justification. Ellicott develops the revelatory character: the gospel does not merely describe a process of becoming righteous; it reveals it, makes it real. The phrase "from faith to faith" has generated the most discussion: Calvin and Barnes favor reading it as expressing the total domination of faith in the process (from first to last, faith throughout); Wesley suggests a movement from justifying faith to sanctifying faith; Clarke, pressing close to the Greek, reads it as "shown to be by faith, and not by the works of any law." RWP takes it as faith being both the means and the goal — the righteousness is received by faith and produces more faith. The Habakkuk quotation (here and in Galatians 3:11 and Hebrews 10:38) is Paul's scriptural anchor: the principle of faith-righteousness is not Pauline invention but prophetic anticipation.

voices.html: The causative γάρ indicates a connection with the preceding: the gospel is the power of God because by the gospel is revealed the righteousness of God — not his essential justice which condemns sinners, but the righteousness which he gives freely, by which the sinner is made right with God. This is revealed "from faith to faith" — from beginning to end, faith is the only hand that receives it.

The gospel attains its end — the salvation of the believer — by revealing the righteousness of God: the plan designed by God for men to become just or righteous in his sight. The process runs from faith to faith — perhaps "from the faithfulness of God" as its source to "the faith of man" as its reception. At every point the initiative is divine and the mode is faith, not works.

For therein is the righteousness of God revealed. This is the reason for the preceding declaration. In the gospel a plan is revealed by which men may become righteous before God. "From faith to faith" — from the beginning to the end, the gospel is a system of faith; faith is the alpha and the omega of the process by which the righteousness of God reaches the sinner.

17.key_tension: "From faith to faith" is read as a totality-expression — faith from beginning to end (Calvin, Barnes) — or as a movement from God's faithfulness to human faith (Ellicott), or from justifying faith to sanctifying faith (Wesley); all agree that faith, not works, is the mode of receiving God's righteousness.

18.synthesis: With verse 18 the argument of the epistle begins in earnest. The wrath of God is being revealed — a present-tense revelation, not merely a future eschatological event — against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of people who suppress the truth by their unrighteousness. Calvin notes that Paul's first argument establishing the need for the gospel is that all human beings, beginning with the Gentiles, are under condemnation: the law condemns all as unrighteous. Ellicott observes the structural parallel with verse 17: just as the righteousness of God is revealed in the gospel for those who believe, so the wrath of God is revealed against those who suppress the truth. Barnes identifies verse 18 as the commencement of the epistle's central argument: the proposition (salvation by faith in the gospel) requires proof, and the proof begins by demonstrating that all other possible grounds of righteousness have been forfeited. Wesley keeps the theological logic clear: "having laid down his proposition, the apostle now enters upon the proof of it." Clarke notes the significance of "revealed" (apokalyptetai): the wrath is not a hidden threat but an active disclosure, visible in the present condition of humanity — the social, moral, and religious disorder of the world is itself the revelation of divine wrath at work.

voices.html: The connection here is with Romans 1:16. He shows by this argument the absolute need of the Gospel: there is no other way of obtaining life and salvation. His first argument is that the law condemns all men as being unrighteous. The wrath of God is revealed — not as a future threat only but as a present reality, operating now in the condition of a humanity that has suppressed the truth it was given.

As a preliminary stage to the revelation of justification and faith, there is another revelation — which is its opposite: a disclosure of divine wrath. The proof is seen in the present condition of mankind. The parallel with verse 17 is deliberate: the gospel reveals God's righteousness for those who believe; the wrath of God is revealed against all who suppress the truth in unrighteousness. Both revelations are simultaneous and real.

For — this word denotes that the apostle is about to give a reason for what he had just said. This verse commences the argument of the epistle — a demonstration designed to show that all human beings are sinners, and that all need the righteousness which the gospel offers. Without this demonstration, the announcement of a divine righteousness would land without its proper weight.

19.synthesis: The suppression of truth requires that truth was first available; Paul immediately establishes this: what can be known about God is plain to them, because God has shown it to them. This is the foundation of Paul's argument against Gentile excuse-making — the knowledge of God was given, not withheld. Calvin is explicit: God has presented to the minds of all the means of knowing him, manifesting himself through his works so that all can perceive what they would naturally prefer to see. Ellicott explains how the Gentiles came to have such knowledge: it is not a separate revelation but the light of creation itself, the universal endowment that leaves no one entirely in the dark about God's existence and power. Wesley traces the channel: "by the light which enlightens every man that comes into the world" (John 1:9), God has made himself known — a light that is both universal and inward. Barnes develops the apologetic significance: the point is not that all people know everything about God but that they have enough knowledge — the knowledge of God's existence and power — to make their suppression of that knowledge culpable. Clarke summarizes the substance of what is known: God's unity, his perfections, his power — enough to make idolatry inexcusable.

voices.html: He thus designates what it behoves us to know of God — all that appertains to the setting forth of the glory of the Lord, whatever ought to move and excite us to glorify him. God has presented to the minds of all the means of knowing him, having so manifested himself by his works that they must necessarily see what of themselves they seek not to know.

They had it — the knowledge of God — because all the knowledge that mankind generally possesses of God was communicated by God himself. It was not discovered by human reason in a neutral universe; it was shown to them by their maker, who built into the creation the evidence of his own character, power, and divine nature.

Because — the apostle here is showing that it was right to condemn men for their sins. To do this it was needful to show that they had the knowledge of God and the means of knowing him. They are not condemned for ignorance they could not overcome; they are condemned for suppressing a knowledge they genuinely had. The knowledge was given; the suppression was chosen.

20.synthesis: The invisible things of God — his eternal power and divine nature — have been clearly seen since the creation of the world, being understood through the things that have been made. This is Paul's most direct statement of natural theology, and the commentators draw out both its reach and its limits. Calvin notes the remarkable parallel with Aristotle's De Mundo, but insists that the point is not philosophical demonstration but moral culpability: the visibility of God in creation is invoked not to praise the achievement of natural theology but to show that the suppression of that knowledge is without excuse. Ellicott explains that though there are parts of God's being no eye can penetrate, what is perceivable in creation is sufficient: the character of the visible creation bears throughout the stamp of its divine origin. Barnes expands the two qualities mentioned — "eternal power" (the power of one who has no beginning and can have no end) and "divine nature" (theiotēs) — and notes that both are visible in the scale, the order, and the design of the created world. Wesley keeps the comment epistemological: these things are "seen by the eye of the mind" by those who use their understanding — natural knowledge requires attention, not supernatural illumination. RWP explains the rare word theiotēs (divinity) as distinct from theotēs (deity): it is the quality or nature of the divine, not the full being of God.

voices.html: There is a passage from Aristotle in his book De Mundo which remarkably coincides with part of this verse, showing that the natural philosophers were not wholly ignorant of this truth. But Paul cites it not to praise natural philosophy but to establish culpability: God has so clearly displayed himself in the creation that those who failed to know him have no excuse, for the knowledge was available to them.

For, though there were parts of God's being into which the eye could not penetrate, still they were easily to be inferred from the character of his visible creation, which bore throughout the stamp of divine authorship. His eternal power — power that has no beginning and no end — and his divine nature are legible to any mind that reads the creation honestly.

For the invisible things of him — those things which cannot be perceived by the senses but can be inferred by the mind. Since the creation of the world they are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made. The scale, the order, the design, the beauty of creation — all testify to a power and a wisdom that lie behind it and cannot themselves be visible. So that they are without excuse — the visibility of the evidence removes the excuse of ignorance.

21.synthesis: The moral failure is stated precisely: knowing God, they did not honor him as God or give him thanks, but became futile in their thinking and their foolish hearts were darkened. The sequence is important — it is not that the darkness caused the failure of knowledge, but that the moral refusal to honor God produced the intellectual darkness. Calvin is explicit: God has presented to the minds of all the means of knowing him, and they plainly testify that they knew him — the verb "knew" (gnontes) is a second aorist, Calvin and RWP both note, indicating definite past knowledge. Ellicott develops the two specific failures: they did not glorify God (the failure of adoration) and they were not thankful (the failure of gratitude) — the two basic responses owed to a creator. Wesley observes that even the wiser heathens knew there was one supreme God but for base considerations conformed to idolatry; the knowing was real and the suppression was deliberate. Barnes explains the word "futile" (emataiōthēsan): they became vain, empty, purposeless in their thinking — the mind detached from God loses its orientation and spirals into confusion. Clarke identifies the specific failure: they did not proclaim God's unity and perfections to others, nor worship him as the one God.

voices.html: He plainly testifies here that God has presented to the minds of all the means of knowing him. But they plainly showed by their works that they knew God; for it was the knowledge of God which produced this obligation — that they should glorify him as God and give him thanks. The failure is not ignorance but suppression: they turned away from what they knew.

They knew enough of God to know that thanks and praise were due to him; but neither of these did they offer. They put aside the natural instinct of adoration and fell to speculations which only led them further astray. The two specific failures — not glorifying God, and not giving thanks — are the root from which all subsequent moral and intellectual collapse grows.

Because that — the apostle here is showing that it was right to condemn men for their sins. They had the knowledge of God and chose not to act on it. In their reasonings — their speculations about God, the world, and morality — they became vain: the mind divorced from God loses its direction and produces confusion instead of wisdom. And their foolish heart was darkened — not by an external force but by their own refusal to honor what they knew.

22.synthesis: "Claiming to be wise, they became fools" — the paradox of rationalist idolatry is stated sharply. Calvin infers that Paul may be alluding specifically to the philosophers who prided themselves on wisdom above all others, but the observation applies to a general pattern of human religious pretension: those who regard themselves as too sophisticated for simple faith in the creator end in absurdities far worse than what they despised. Ellicott adds a judicial note: they were made fools — the foolishness is not only their natural tendency but a divine infliction, a judgment on their first step of declension (compare the sequence in verses 24, 26, 28 with the threefold "God gave them up"). Barnes traces the cultural-historical background: the ancient philosophers explicitly prided themselves on their wisdom (philosophoi = lovers of wisdom), and their pride was precisely the pretension that verse 22 describes. Clarke observes the word phaskō (professing, claiming) — they made the claim insistently; the claim was the very thing that made the folly visible. RWP gives the grammatical structure: moranō (to make foolish, to show as fools) is the verb, and the sentence is a biting example of the irony that pervades this section of the letter.

voices.html: It is commonly inferred from this passage that Paul alludes to those philosophers who assumed to themselves in a peculiar manner the reputation of wisdom. The pattern is universal: those who claim the highest wisdom in their own right, detached from the God who is the source of all wisdom, end in a folly proportional to their pride. The greater the claim, the more visible the collapse.

They became fools — they were made fools. It is not merely that they exposed their real folly, but that folly is itself judicially inflicted by God as a punishment for the first step of declension. The pretension to wisdom, when it replaces the knowledge of God with human speculation, is itself the beginning of the darkening process, and the "God gave them up" of verses 24, 26, 28 is its continuation.

Professing themselves to be wise. This was the common boast of the philosophers of antiquity. The very word they chose — philosophers, lovers of wisdom — expressed the self-assessment. Paul's verdict is that this highest cultural achievement of the ancient world, when divorced from the knowledge of the creator, issued in the worst degradation. The higher the pretension, the deeper the fall.

23.synthesis: The descent into idolatry follows the logic of the whole passage: having abandoned the knowledge of the incorruptible God, human beings constructed substitute objects of worship drawn from the mortal and material world. The verse quotes and applies Psalm 106:20 and Jeremiah 2:11. The progression Calvin notes is significant: human beings first reduced the divine glory to a human form (anthropomorphism), then to animals, then to reptiles — descending through the hierarchy of creation in inverse proportion to their claim of wisdom. Ellicott observes that the first stage — images of mortal man — applies especially to the Greek and Roman religions, in which the gods were depicted as idealized humans. Clarke notes the specific catalogue: first the form of mortal man, then four-footed beasts, then birds, then creeping things — the full range of Gentile idolatry from the Zeus-Jupiter tradition to Egyptian animal cults. Barnes emphasizes the theological outrage: "the glory of the immortal God" is the divine majesty, the self-disclosure that demands adoration — to exchange it for the image of a reptile is the measure of how thoroughly sin distorts perception. Wesley identifies this as the first of three degrees of ungodliness and punishment in the chapter, a structural note that helps readers follow Paul's argument.

voices.html: Having feigned such a God as they could comprehend according to their carnal reason, they were very far from acknowledging the true God: they devised a fictitious and new god, or rather a phantom. The glory of the incorruptible God was not lost but exchanged — traded for images that their own hands had made and their own minds had conceived. The exchange is the measure of human willfulness in suppression of truth.

They changed the glory — the finest representation of their deities was in the human figure, and on such representative figures the sculptors spent all their skill. But these images were of mortal men, not the immortal God. And from there the descent continued: four-footed beasts (the animal cults of Egypt), birds, and creeping things — the whole of creation pressed into service as objects of worship, while the Creator was ignored.

And changed — they exchanged, in their conceptions and their worship. They produced, not a change in God's being, but a change in their treatment of him: they honored images instead of the living God. The glory of the immortal God — his majesty and self-disclosure — was surrendered for the likeness of a corruptible creature. The exchange is as irrational as it is impious.

24.synthesis: "Therefore God gave them up" — the first of three judicial acts of divine abandonment (repeated in verses 26 and 28). The abandonment is not arbitrary but follows the logic of sin: those who chose to worship the creation rather than the Creator are handed over to the further consequences of that choice. Calvin sees a demonstrative purpose in this: to prove their guilt by a more palpable demonstration, God allowed the fruits of their impiety to become visible in their conduct. Barnes is direct: the verse traces the practical tendency of heathenism — not as innocent error but as the harvest of deliberate moral choice. Ellicott notes the structural parallel: "as they did not like to retain God in their knowledge, God gave them up" — the punishment is shaped by the crime. Clarke draws out the mechanism: their idolatrous system necessarily produced all kinds of moral impurity because the gods they worshipped were themselves adulterers and criminals, and the worship legitimized what the gods were. RWP traces Paul's argumentative logic: the three uses of "God gave them up" (verses 24, 26, 28) form a crescendo, each stage of moral degradation being both the punishment of the previous one and the occasion for the next. The lusts of their hearts and the dishonoring of their bodies are the first installment of what idolatry costs.

voices.html: As impiety is a hidden evil, lest they should still find an evasion, he shows by a more palpable demonstration that they cannot escape: such fruits as these — the dishonoring of the body, the degradation of the natural order — prove that the worship which produced them was not innocent ignorance but deliberate suppression of truth. God gave them up to the harvest of their own choices.

Wherefore — that is, because they were unwilling to retain God in their knowledge and chose to worship idols. Here is traced the practical tendency of heathenism: not as an innocent and harmless error but as a system whose moral consequences were as degraded as its theology was false. God gave them up — a judicial act, not an arbitrary one; the punishment fits the crime precisely.

Wherefore (διό) — Paul's inexorable logic. God gave them up (παρέδωκεν αὐτοὺς ὁ θεός). First aorist active indicative of παραδίδωμι — to hand over, to give up to the consequences of their own choices. This verb appears three times (verses 24, 26, 28), forming a descending spiral: each act of divine abandonment is both punishment for the previous stage and occasion for the next.

25.synthesis: The verse restates in different words what was said in verse 23 — the exchange of the truth of God for a lie — in order to fix it more deeply. Calvin's observation is precise: when the truth of God is turned to a lie, his glory is obliterated, and it is just that he should avenge such a contempt. The phrase "the truth of God" does not mean a proposition about God but the true God himself — as Ellicott explains, they ceased to worship God as he is and worshipped false gods instead. Barnes makes the same point: the phrase is a Hebrew idiom meaning the true God; they exchanged him for an idol that was a lie — a falsehood, a non-entity. Clarke identifies idols as "lies" drawing on Isaiah 44:20, Jeremiah 10:14, and 13:25, showing that the prophetic tradition regularly designated idols as falsehoods. Wesley keeps the two dimensions distinct: "changed the truth" means the true worship of God; "into a lie" means false and abominable idolatry; "worshipped" is the inward devotion and "served" the outward practice — idolatry corrupts both the interior and the exterior of human religious life. The doxology that closes the verse — "who is blessed forever, Amen" — is Paul's instinctive response to speaking of God: even in this dark passage, the mention of the true God calls forth praise.

voices.html: He repeats what he had said before, though in different words, in order to fix it deeper in our minds. When the truth of God is turned to a lie, his glory is obliterated. It is then just that he should avenge such contempt by giving people over to the fruits of the choice they made. And the doxology — blessed be he forever — shows that Paul cannot mention the true God even in the context of his dishonoring without breaking into instinctive praise.

Who changed the truth of God into a lie — they ceased to worship God as he is in his own true essential nature, and worshipped false gods instead. The phrase "into a lie" — literally, with a lie, in exchange for a lie — captures the deceptive character of idolatry: what is presented as divine is in fact nothing. The truth was abandoned; the lie was embraced; the consequences follow.

Who changed the truth of God — this is a repetition of the declaration in Romans 1:23, in another form. The phrase "the truth of God" means the true God. In such a passage the phrase denotes the real and genuine God as opposed to idols, which are falsehoods and vanities. They exchanged the real for the counterfeit, and the apostle's doxology — blessed be he forever — is the instinctive recoil of a worshipping heart.

26.synthesis: The second "God gave them up" concerns the degradation of sexuality: God handed them over to dishonorable passions, and the specific example is same-sex relations among women. Calvin notes that he returns to the judgment of God stated in verse 24, bringing the first example of the dreadful crimes that unnatural desire produces. The argument of the passage is that the moral disorder mirrors the theological disorder: those who exchanged the Creator for the creature find their most intimate relationships exchanging natural for unnatural. Clarke is direct about the historical application: the Roman emperors themselves were notorious for these practices, and no community was more implicated in the pattern Paul describes. Barnes observes that the verse states the first of two related examples (verse 27 completes it with the male parallel), both establishing that Paul regards these practices as morally serious precisely because they violate the natural order that creation itself discloses. Wesley is brief but pointed: God gave them up to these vile affections — the Roman world, and none more than its emperors, was then abandoned to such practices. Ellicott reads the verse with the judicial logic intact: God's giving-up is not approval but abandonment to consequences, the wrath that consists in allowing people to proceed in the direction they have chosen.

voices.html: After having introduced as it were an intervening clause, he returns to what he had before stated respecting the judgment of God: and he brings, as the first example, the dreadful crime of unnatural lust. Their idolatry produced this as its harvest — the degradation of the body mirrors the degradation of the mind that chose images over the Creator.

For this cause God gave them up — their system of idolatry necessarily produced all kinds of impurity. The gods they worshipped were themselves represented as guilty of the very crimes their worshippers practiced. And the Roman world of Paul's day was, as history records, abandoned to exactly these degradations — at every level of society and most visibly at the highest.

For this cause — on account of what had just been specified: that they did not glorify God, that they became polytheists and idolaters. In the previous verses Paul had been describing their intellectual degradation; now he turns to the moral. God gave them up — a judicial act corresponding exactly to the theological choice they had made: they exchanged natural for unnatural in religion; the same exchange pervades their moral lives.

27.synthesis: The parallel example is the male version of the same pattern: men burned in their lust for one another, committing shameless acts and receiving in themselves the due penalty for their error. Calvin begins with the justice of this: they deserved to be blinded, to forget themselves and not see anything befitting them, since they had willfully closed their eyes against the light God gave them. Ellicott specifies the "due recompense" or "penalty": the "error" referred to is the turning from God to idols, and the penalty received "in themselves" — upon their own persons — is the physical and social consequences of the practices described. Clarke brings the physiological observation of his era: the practices described enervated bodies, caused disorders, and shortened lives — the penalty was built into the conduct itself. Barnes develops the historical claim: this particular vice was so prevalent in the ancient world that it hardly needs documentation; Paul's description is historically accurate. Wesley states the theological sequence with precision: their idolatry was punished with this unnatural lust, which was as great a dishonor to the body as the idolatry was to God — the punishment fits the crime exactly. RWP notes the verb exekauthēsan (burned out, consumed with passion) — a causative form, indicating not just passion but passion inflamed beyond natural restraint.

voices.html: They indeed deserved to be blinded, so as to forget themselves and not see anything befitting them — they who through their own malignity had closed their eyes against the light offered them by God. The recompense received in themselves is the penalty that the nature of the sin itself carries: the dishonoring of the body is proportional to the dishonoring of God.

In themselves — upon themselves, upon their own persons thus shamefully dishonoured. That recompense of their error which was meet — the "error" is the turning from God to idols. The "recompense" is not an external imposition but a consequence inherent in the conduct itself: the penalty is written into the transgression, as moral disorder always carries within it its own disruption.

And likewise the men — the vice here specified, called sodomy from the infamous city of Sodom, was so common in the ancient world that it barely requires documentation. The apostle declares that this was the recompense of their theological error — their abandonment of God was met by God's abandonment of them to what they had chosen, and what they had chosen worked its own destruction in them.

28.synthesis: The third and final "God gave them up" concerns the reprobate mind — a mind incapable of sound moral judgment. The verse states the symmetry with notable precision: "as they did not see fit to acknowledge God, God gave them up to a mind that is not fit." Calvin identifies the evident comparison: they chose not to continue in the knowledge of God, so God gave them a mind that has no judgment; the punishment corresponds to the crime in exact proportions. Ellicott reads "even as" not as "because" but as expressing proportion: the degree of God's abandonment matched exactly the degree of their rejection of him. Barnes makes the moral psychology clear: the choice was active — they did not choose (did not see fit, did not approve) to retain God in their knowledge. They tested God and rejected him; God gave them a mind that similarly fails every moral test. RWP's note is concise: the heathen examined God and found him wanting; God in turn gave them a mind (noun) that does not pass examination — a mind that fails every moral test. The result is a catalogue of sins (verses 29–31) that illustrates what a mind detached from God produces when left to itself.

voices.html: There is an evident comparison to be observed in these words, by which is strikingly set forth the just relation between sin and punishment. As they chose not to continue in the knowledge of God, who had been made known to them, so God gave them a mind destitute of judgment: since they chose to be fools in religious matters, God delivered them over to be fools in all the affairs of life.

Even as — rightly translated, not as "because" but as expressing proportion: just in like proportion as they rejected God, God gave them over to a reprobate mind. The degree of the punishment corresponded exactly to the degree of the offense. They found God unworthy of acknowledgment; God gave them a mind that is unworthy of any acknowledgment as a moral guide.

And even as they did not like — this was the true source of their crimes. They did not choose to acknowledge God. It was not that they could not, but that they were displeased with him and preferred their own objects of worship. God gave them up to a reprobate mind — a mind incapable of sound moral judgment, that would approve what ought to be condemned and condemn what ought to be approved.

29.synthesis: Verses 29–31 form a vice catalogue — a literary form common in both Jewish and Hellenistic moral writing — listing the specific forms of unrighteousness that characterize the God-abandoned mind. Paul opens with four major categories (unrighteousness, evil, covetousness, malice) and then adds a series of more specific vices. Calvin notes the philological distinction between adikia (unrighteousness, violation of justice in human relations) and ponēria (a disposition of active malice). Clarke provides a detailed lexical analysis of each term, showing that Paul has chosen Greek words that map precisely onto specific forms of social and moral disorder. Wesley identifies covetousness with fornication as the two encompassing categories, following his reading of the structure. Barnes observes that the verse's opening claim — "being filled with all" — indicates these were not marginal phenomena but pervasive patterns, the normal texture of pagan social life at its worst. RWP lists the four leading terms as a tight cluster in the associative instrumental case, noting that the list is both comprehensive and precise: Paul is not ranting but cataloguing specific moral conditions that his readers would recognize from daily observation in Rome and throughout the empire.

voices.html: Understand by the violation of justice among men, by not rendering to each his due, the unrighteousness that heads the list. Ammonius teaches that ponēros, the wicked, denotes one addicted to mischief-making; so ponēria here is an active malice that delights in causing harm. Paul has chosen his terms to cover the full range of what a mind abandoned to itself will produce in social life.

Being filled — that is, these things were common, they abounded among them. This is a strong phrase, denoting that these things were so often practised as to characterize the age and nation. Unrighteousness — the violation of right in all its forms. Evil — active malice. Covetousness — the insatiable desire for more. Malice — the settled disposition of ill-will toward others. These four open the catalogue that the remaining terms will specify.

Being filled with (πεπληρωμένους). Perfect passive participle of the common verb πληρόω, state of completion, "filled to the brim with" — four vices in the associative instrumental case: ἀδικία (unrighteousness), πονηρίᾳ (active wickedness), πλεονεξίᾳ (covetousness), κακίᾳ (malice). The list is precise, not rhetorical padding.

30.synthesis: The catalogue continues with a second cluster of twelve vices, including the much-discussed theostygeis. Calvin reads the word as active — "haters of God" — a designation for those who, regardless of their formal religiosity, are at enmity with the God they claim to honor; the rest of the list shows this is the summary of the whole moral failure. Ellicott argues for the passive reading — "hated by God" — citing the absence of classical examples of the active sense and the common Greek passive-adjectival formation. Barnes surveys both options without settling definitively, noting that both readings make theological sense in the context. Clarke resolves it practically by noting that those who consistently do the things listed are in fact hated by God, and by God's own testimony in Scripture such conduct places a person in that position. Wesley gives a clear enough paraphrase: rebels against God, deniers of his providence, accusers of his justice. RWP follows Calvin in the active sense and places it as the pivot of the whole catalogue. The remaining terms — insolent, haughty, boastful, inventors of evil, disobedient to parents — continue the social portrait of a mind that has severed itself from the acknowledgment of God.

voices.html: The word theostygeis means haters of God — there is no reason to take it in a passive sense (hated of God), since Paul here proves men to be guilty by manifest vices. Those designated are not those against whom God has any private grudge, but those whose works declare their active enmity: they are such as have divested themselves of all fear of God and of all reverence for his majesty.

Haters of God — rather, perhaps, hated by God. There seem to be no examples of the active sense in classical Greek. The Apostle apparently throws in one emphatic word summing up the catalogue as far as it has gone — and whether it is taken as active (hating God) or passive (hated by God), the point is the same: the moral catalogue describes persons who stand in radical opposition to the God they were made to worship.

Haters of God — or hated by God. Whichever reading is adopted, the term marks the theological ground of the whole catalogue: these vices are not random moral failures but the systematic expression of a life that has rejected its creator. Insolent, haughty, boastful, inventors of evil, disobedient to parents — the list shows how thoroughgoing the moral disintegration is when God is removed from the center.

30.key_tension: Calvin reads theostygeis as active (haters of God), which fits the moral catalogue that proves human guilt; Ellicott argues for the passive (hated by God) based on classical usage and Greek word formation; both readings are theologically coherent in the context.

31.synthesis: The catalogue concludes with four more vices, each built with the a- prefix of negation: without understanding, covenant-breakers, without natural affection, unmerciful. RWP notes the wordplay with verse 28's "reprobate mind" (adokimon noun): "without understanding" (asunetous) returns to the same root, forming a bracket. Calvin identifies covenant-breaking as one of the most severe: those who do not hesitate to violate the most solemn engagements have abandoned the foundation of civil society. Ellicott applies "without understanding" to moral rather than intellectual capacity — the incapacity to discriminate between right and wrong. Barnes explains "without natural affection" (astorgous): the term specifically refers to natural family love — the parental instinct, the child's love for parents — the most basic of human bonds; its absence signals how thorough the moral dissolution is. Clarke and Wesley both observe that the Romans as a nation were notorious covenant-breakers; the historical specificity is not incidental — Paul is describing the world his readers know. Wesley notes that "unmerciful" is placed last, as the final collapse of the human heart: unmercifulness is the summary of what a life lived without God produces.

voices.html: They are those who have put off the first affections of nature towards their own relations. The want of natural affection is evidence that human nature is depraved; and Augustine, arguing against the Stoics, uses this passage to show that the natural impulses of love are not simply to be suppressed but to be directed and sanctified. Covenant-breakers and unmerciful stand as the final indictments of a social order that has lost God.

Without understanding — without moral or spiritual understanding; incapable of discriminating between right and wrong, expedient and inexpedient. The word picks up the "reprobate mind" of verse 28. Covenant-breakers — faithless to their most solemn engagements. Without natural affection — the most fundamental human bonds dissolved. Unmerciful — the catalogue ends at its most desolate point.

Without understanding — inconsiderate, morally blind. Covenant-breakers — perfidious, false to their contracts. Without natural affection — this expression denotes the want of affectionate regard for children, parents, siblings: the bonds of nature dissolved. Unmerciful — without compassion for the suffering of others. Together these four terms describe the final stage of moral collapse: a human being who is intelligent enough to construct systems but incapable of basic human solidarity.

32.synthesis: The catalogue's final verse adds the most damning element: these people not only do such things but give hearty approval to those who practice them. Paul has been describing a society in moral collapse; now he shows that the collapse is not merely behavioral but evaluative. The Gentiles know the divine decree — the moral law written into creation and conscience — that those who practice such things deserve death; and yet they not only do them but applaud others who do them. Calvin reads this as expressing the deepest degree of moral ruin: a person may be swept into sin by passion while still retaining some internal protest, but the one who approves, celebrates, and encourages such sins in others has abandoned even that last foothold. Ellicott reads "knowing" (epignontes) as full and thorough knowledge — they are not ignorant of the moral standard but are in rebellion against it with open eyes. Wesley states the escalation plainly: it is the highest degree of wickedness to take pleasure in the evil-doing of others. Barnes observes the double crime: it is bad enough to do wrong; it compounds the guilt enormously to approve of wrong in others, since approval encourages and multiplies the evil. RWP cites the Greek term for approval — suneudokousinon — as a term of hearty, enthusiastic endorsement.

voices.html: Though this passage is variously explained, the following interpretation appears to me the correctest: men learn that the things they commit are worthy of death, and they commit them still. But much more damnable is this — that they not only commit them but give their approval to others who commit them. The one who sins against conscience is less culpable than the one who has made peace with sin and celebrates it in others.

Knowing — again the word for full or thorough knowledge. With full knowledge of the sentence of eternal death which is in store for them, they show that it is no mere momentary yielding to the force of passion. They not only do the same themselves but have pleasure in those that practise them — the approval of others' sins is the final and most deliberate expression of a will set against God.

Who knowing — the Gentiles had a moral sense and were capable of knowing the will of God in this case; Romans 2:14–15 makes this clear. Not only do the same — which would have been bad enough — but have pleasure in those who practise them. This is the deepest degradation: it is bad to do wrong, but it immensely aggravates the evil to approve, defend, and encourage wrong in others. The approver multiplies the harm beyond his own act.

1.synthesis: Romans 4 opens with the test case that Paul's entire argument has been building toward: Abraham, the father of the Jewish race. If justification by faith is true, it must hold for Abraham — the supreme exemplar of Old Testament righteousness. Calvin calls this "a confirmation by example; a very strong one," because Abraham is not a peripheral figure but the pattern to whom all the faithful must conform. The rhetorical question — "What then shall we say that Abraham, our forefather according to the flesh, found?" — sets up the argument. RWP notes that "forefather" (propator) appears only here in the New Testament and that the phrase "according to the flesh" qualifies "our forefather," not the verb "found": Abraham is our physical ancestor; the question is whether his physical lineage gives him a different mode of justification than the one Paul has described. Barnes observes that the argument is addressed particularly to a Jew, to show that no new doctrine is being advanced. The entire chapter is a demonstration that the gospel of justification by faith is not a Pauline novelty but the ancient pattern of the covenant.
