voices.html: This is a confirmation by example; and it is a very strong one, since all things are alike with regard to the subject and the person; for he was the father of the faithful, to whom we ought all to be conformed; and there is also but one way and not many ways by which righteousness may be obtained by all. As in the person of Abraham there was exhibited a mirror and pattern of righteousness which belongs in common to the whole Church, Paul rightly applies what belongs to him to all the members.

The words "according to the flesh" appear to be determined by their position in the sentence. According to the best MSS. they are distinctly separated from "hath found" and joined with "our father." They therefore mean simply "our father according to the flesh," i.e., by natural descent. The question is whether Abraham as our physical ancestor found a privileged mode of justification — or whether the one gospel applies to him as to all.

The main object of this chapter is to show that the doctrine of justification by faith which the apostle was defending was found in the Old Testament. The argument is to be regarded as addressed particularly to a Jew, to show him that no new doctrine was advanced. The argument is derived first from the fact that Abraham was so justified, and secondly from the fact that the same thing is declared by David.

2.synthesis: The structure of the argument is what Calvin identifies as an epicheiréma — an abbreviated syllogism: if Abraham were justified by works, he would have grounds for glorying before men; but the Scripture denies that he has grounds for glorying before God; therefore he was not justified by works. Ellicott renders the logic directly: had Abraham's justification been earned, it would have been something to be proud of before God as well as men — he could have pled his own merit. But God's own record denies this. Barnes sharpens the distinction: the question is not whether Abraham did good works (he did), but whether those works were the ground of his acceptance before God. Wesley captures the form of Paul's reasoning precisely: "If Abraham had been justified by works, he would have had room to glory. But he had not room to glory. Therefore he was not justified by works." RWP notes that the first clause is a conditional of the first class — assumed true for the sake of argument though false in fact — a common Pauline rhetorical device that grants the premise temporarily in order to show where it leads. The whole verse sets up the Scripture citation in verse 3 as the decisive answer.

voices.html: This is an incomplete argument, an attempted but an unfinished process of reasoning. The word for "glorying" here, kauchēma, means reason, ground, or cause for glorying — whereby he may hope for praise. If Abraham were justified by works, he had such a cause; but before God he did not. Therefore the justification was not by works. The Scriptural record in verse 3 is the proof that confirms the syllogism.

We know that he obtained justification. If that justification had been earned by his own works it would then have been something to be proud of; it would be a pride that he might fairly hold both towards men and towards God. But he has not this merit. His justification was not earned, but bestowed upon him, not for the sake of his works, but of his faith. This is the express statement of Scripture.

The meaning is: If Abraham had been justified by works, he would have had room to glory. But he had not room to glory. Therefore he was not justified by works. David is also introduced: Abraham was the most illustrious pattern of piety among the Jewish patriarchs; David was the most eminent of their kings. If neither was justified by his own obedience, if both obtained acceptance only by faith, then faith — not works — is the way of salvation.

3.synthesis: The quotation from Genesis 15:6 — "Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness" — is the hinge of the chapter. Paul treats it as decisive: the Scripture itself said this, and the Scripture cannot be wrong. Calvin observes that if Abraham was justified because he embraced by faith the mercy of God, then he brought nothing of his own except a confession of his misery. RWP explains the verb elogisthē as accounting language: it was set down on the credit side of the ledger. What was set down? His believing — the act of trust, not any work of merit. Barnes notes that the faith in view was Abraham's trust that his posterity would be as the stars of heaven in number, a promise made when he had no child. Wesley extends the reference: Abraham believed both the promise about a numerous seed and the promise concerning Christ through whom all nations should be blessed. Ellicott draws out what imputation means: "His righteousness was not real, but imputed. His faith was treated as if it had been equivalent to a righteousness of works." But then the next verse immediately clarifies: faith carries no idea of merit — it is simply the hand that receives the free gift of God.

voices.html: This is a proof of the minor proposition: if Abraham was justified because he embraced by faith the bountiful mercy of God, it follows that he had nothing to glory in; for he brought nothing of his own, except a confession of his misery, which is a solicitation for mercy. He takes it as granted that the righteousness of faith is the refuge and asylum of the sinner who is destitute of works.

It was reckoned unto him for righteousness (elogisthē eis dikaiosynēn). First aorist passive indicative of logizomai, old and common verb to set down accounts, literally or metaphorically. It was set down on the credit side of the ledger "for" (eis as often) righteousness. What was set down? His believing God (episteusen tō theō).

Abraham believed God — in the Hebrew, "Abraham believed Jehovah." The faith which Abraham exercised was that his posterity should be like the stars of heaven in number. This promise was made to him when he had no child, and of course all natural probability was against it. Yet he believed. And this implicit trust in the divine word, this confidence in God's power and faithfulness, was what God counted as righteousness.

4.synthesis: The contrast between works and faith is here expressed in economic terms: wages versus gift. Calvin is careful about who is meant by "the worker": it is not the person who does good works (all believers ought to do those), but the person who seeks to merit something by those works and demands God's acceptance as a right. Barnes expresses the same: the passage does not mean that anyone actually has so worked as to earn salvation, but it states a general principle — where a man fulfills the conditions of a contract, the reward is owed, not given as charity. Ellicott makes the theological inference clear: Abraham's justification fell into the gift category, not the wages category. He was not owed righteousness; he received it freely. Wesley identifies Abraham and David as the two supreme test cases: "the most illustrious pattern of piety among the Jewish patriarchs" and "the most eminent of their kings" — if neither was justified by obedience, the pattern is settled. RWP reinforces the distinction linguistically: the contrast is between kata opheileima (according to debt, as wages owed) and kata charin (according to grace, as a gift bestowed). The two categories are mutually exclusive: if one applies, the other does not.

voices.html: It is not he whom he calls a worker who is given to good works — to which all the children of God ought to attend — but the person who seeks to merit something by his works. He would not have the faithful to be idle; but he only forbids them to be mercenaries, so as to demand anything from God as though it were justly their due. The question is not here how we are to regulate our life, but how we are to be saved.

Those who base their claim on works have a right to their reward. It is not conceded to them by any sort of imputation, but is their desert. On the other hand those who rely only upon faith, even though ungodly themselves, have righteousness imputed to them. This latter was Abraham's case, and not the former. The contrast of debt and gift is the contrast of two systems that are mutually exclusive.

This passage is not to be understood as affirming that any actually have worked out their salvation by conformity to the law so as to be saved by their own merits; but it expresses a general truth in regard to works. On that plan, if a man were justified by his works, it would be a matter due to him — not of grace but of right. This general principle then is applied to Abraham: he was not paid righteousness as wages; he received it as a gift.

5.synthesis: "To the one who does not work but believes in him who justifies the ungodly" — the phrase is one of the most striking in the Pauline corpus. Calvin notes that some have stumbled at this expression and misapplied it, as though faith were itself a meritorious act rather than the means of attaining righteousness. The syntax is dense: faith is counted as righteousness not because faith is itself the righteousness, but because it lays hold of the God who freely bestows righteousness on the ungodly. RWP offers what Denney called the definitive summary: "The whole Pauline gospel could be summed up in this one word — God who justifies the ungodly." Ellicott notes the deliberate boldness of "the ungodly" — a stronger word than merely "the unrighteous" — and acknowledges that faith and positive impiety would hardly be found together in practice. But the phrase preserves the theological principle in its sharpest form: justification is not the reward of the pious man's piety, but the gift of God to the one who, recognizing his own ungodliness, trusts in the God who can deal with it. Barnes makes the pastoral application: the reference is specifically to the Christian plan of justification, where the person who does not rely on works of law, but believes, finds God imputing righteousness as a free act of grace.

voices.html: This is a very important sentence, in which he expresses the substance and nature both of faith and of righteousness. He clearly shows that faith brings us righteousness, not because it is a meritorious act, but because it obtains for us the favor of God. Some have stumbled at this sentence — "his faith is counted for righteousness" — misapplying it as though faith were in itself the cause of righteousness and hence a meritorious act, rather than the way and means of attaining it.

That justifieth the ungodly (ton dikaiounta ton asebē). The impious, irreverent man. See 1:25. A forensic figure (Shedd). The man is taken as he is and pardoned. "The whole Pauline gospel could be summed up in this one word — God who justifies the ungodly" (Denney).

But to him that worketh not — who does not rely on his conformity to the law for his justification; who seeks to be justified in some other way. But believeth on him that justifieth the ungodly — this is a very important expression. It implies that men are undeserving and wicked, and that God pardons them not for any merit of their own but by an act of pure mercy through the intervention of the Lord Jesus Christ.

6.synthesis: Having established Abraham's case from the Pentateuch, Paul now introduces a second witness: David. The move is apologetically significant — Calvin observes that anyone could try to limit the Abrahamic argument to the patriarchal era before the law, but David testifies to the same principle under the full Mosaic dispensation, destroying any such evasion. Wesley notes that David is fitly introduced after Abraham because he also received and delivered down the promise, and he is the most eminent of the kings. Barnes explains the specific phrase "to whom God imputeth righteousness without works": it means God treats as righteous, or as entitled to his favor, in a way not grounded on their own obedience. Ellicott pauses on the verb: to "describeth" or better, to "pronounces blessed" — David felicitates or congratulates the person who is in this state. The felicitation has forensic content: to be pronounced blessed is to be declared in a favorable standing before God. RWP gives the Greek noun (makariamos) as a rare form meaning felicitation or congratulation, used only here and in verse 9. The citation of Psalm 32 that follows (vv. 7–8) gives the concrete content: the blessed person is the one whose transgressions are forgiven.

voices.html: We hence see the sheer sophistry of those who limit the works of the law to ceremonies; for he now simply calls those works, without anything added, which he had before called the works of the law. No one can deny that a simple and unrestricted mode of speaking ought to be understood of every work without difference. There is nothing less reasonable than to remove from ceremonies only the power of justifying, since Paul excludes all works indefinitely.

So David also — David is fitly introduced after Abraham, because he also received and delivered down the promise. Affirmeth — a man is justified by faith alone, and not by works. Without works — that is, without regard to any former good works supposed to have been done by him. The same doctrine attested by the law and by the prophets, by the first patriarch and by the greatest of the kings.

Even as David — the apostle, having adduced the example of Abraham to show that the doctrine was not new, proceeds to adduce the case of David also, and to show that he understood the same doctrine of justification without works. Describeth the blessedness — speaks of the happy condition. Unto whom God imputeth righteousness without works — whom God treats as righteous in a way not founded on their own conformity to the law.

7.synthesis: The quotation from Psalm 32:1–2 gives specific content to David's testimony about the blessedness of forgiven sin. Paul reads the Psalm as a description of the same justification-by-faith that Genesis 15:6 describes; the psalmist's language of forgiveness, covering, and non-imputation of sin is the subjective experience of what imputed righteousness is. Ellicott focuses on the key word: "forgiven" — "whose sins are not abolished, but forgiven; not annihilated, but covered up, removed from sight, hidden by the absolving grace of God." Clarke develops the vocabulary: hai anomiai (transgressions of the law) are forgiven; hai hamartiai (innumerable deviations from the strict rule of righteousness) are covered. Wesley draws the experiential application: "Happy are they whose sins are covered with the veil of divine mercy. If there be indeed such a thing as happiness on earth, it is the portion of that man whose iniquities are forgiven." RWP notes that Paul cites both verse 1 and verse 2 of Psalm 32 together as David's testimony, and that the verb "covered" (epikalyphthēsan) is a first aorist passive, expressing a decisive act of divine concealment. Barnes connects the verse back to the argument: to have sins covered is to be treated as though the sin had not been committed — an act of non-imputation.

voices.html: Forgiven — the stress is upon this word: "whose sins are not abolished, but forgiven; not annihilated, but covered up, removed from sight, hidden by the absolving grace of God." The covering is not the destruction of the sin but its removal from God's sight — a forensic act of mercy by which what is real in guilt is treated as absent in judgment.

Blessed are they whose iniquities are forgiven — that is, the man is truly happy whose iniquities, hai anomiai, whose transgressions of the law are forgiven; for by these he was exposed to the most grievous punishment. Whose sins, hai hamartiai, his innumerable deviations from the strict rule of truth and righteousness, are covered — entirely removed out of sight, and thrown into oblivion.

Happy are they whose sins are covered — with the veil of divine mercy. If there be indeed such a thing as happiness on earth, it is the portion of that man whose iniquities are forgiven, and who enjoys the manifestation of that pardon. Well may he endure all the afflictions of life with cheerfulness, and look upon death with comfort. O let us not contend against it, but earnestly pray that this blessedness may be ours.

8.synthesis: The second verse of the psalm quotation introduces the concept of non-imputation of sin: "Blessed is the man against whom the Lord will not count his sin." This is the negative expression of what verse 7 stated positively. Clarke reads the verse as applying to those who are redeemed from the curse of the law and the consequences of their ungodly life, their sins freely forgiven through the mercy of God — they alone are truly happy. Barnes explains what non-imputation means: it is the refusal to charge, to reckon, or to regard a person as guilty — to treat them, though personally undeserving, as if the sin had not been committed. This is, he notes, the same word (logizomai) used in verse 3 of Abraham's faith being counted as righteousness: the two uses bracket the concept. If faith is reckoned as righteousness (positive imputation), it follows equally that sin is not reckoned against the believer (negative non-imputation). RWP notes that the best manuscripts read hou ("whose sin") rather than hō ("to whom"), following the LXX, and that the strong double negative (ou mē logisthēi) emphasizes the absolute permanence of the non-imputation.

voices.html: Blessed is the man to whom the Lord will not impute sin — that man is truly happy to whose charge God does not reckon sin; that is, they alone are happy who are redeemed from the curse of the law and the consequence of their ungodly life, by having their sins freely forgiven through the mercy of God.

Will not impute sin — on whom the Lord will not charge his sins; who shall not be reckoned or regarded as guilty. This shows clearly what the apostle meant by imputing faith without works. It is to pardon sin, and to treat with favour; not to reckon or charge a man's sin to him; but to treat him, though personally undeserving and ungodly, as though the sin had not been committed. The word "impute" here is used in its natural and appropriate sense, as denoting to charge to one's account.

To whom (hō). But the best MSS. read hou like the LXX, "whose sin." Will not reckon (ou mē logisthētai). Strong negation by double negative and aorist middle subjunctive. The same accounting verb as in verse 3 — here in its negative form: God refuses to enter the sin on the debit side of the ledger.

9.synthesis: Having established that the blessedness of forgiven sin and non-imputed guilt exists (from both Abraham and David), Paul now asks whether this blessedness belongs to the circumcised only — the Jews — or whether it extends to the uncircumcised as well. Clarke notes the rhetorical force of the question: if this pardon is essential to happiness, and David says it is, can it really be the exclusive privilege of Jews? The apostle's own answer follows from the Genesis 15:6 citation: it was Abraham's faith that was counted as righteousness. Barnes explains the structure: Paul has now shown that Abraham was justified by faith; the remaining question is whether this was before or after circumcision. If after, the Jew could argue the circumcision was necessary; if before, the circumcision was irrelevant to the justification. Wesley states the implication plainly: this blessedness is "fully consistent with our being justified — pardoned and accepted by God upon our believing, for the sake of what Christ has done and suffered." Ellicott reconstructs the argument: the felicitation of Psalm 32 belongs to the uncircumcised as well as the circumcised, because the act of faith and the justification consequent upon it were prior to the institution of circumcision — a fact established in verse 10.

voices.html: Cometh this blessedness upon the circumcision only? The apostle's question is very nervous: If this pardon granted in this way be essential to happiness — and David says it is — then is it the privilege of the Jews exclusively? This cannot be; for as it is by the mere mercy of God that any sin is forgiven at all, it must apply as readily to Gentiles as to Jews, since all are alike guilty before God.

It follows from the language of David that the blessedness thus predicated belongs to the uncircumcised as well as to the circumcised, for — then comes the first premise of the argument. It was the act of faith which was the cause of Abraham's justification. But both the act of faith and the justification consequent upon it were prior to the institution of the rite of circumcision. The narrative of Genesis establishes this decisively.

Cometh this blessedness — the apostle has now prepared the way for an examination of the inquiry whether this came in consequence of obedience to the law? Having shown that Abraham was justified by faith in accordance with the doctrine he was defending, the only remaining inquiry was whether it was after he was circumcised or before — whether in consequence of his circumcision or not. The answer to that question decides everything.

10.synthesis: The answer to the question of verse 9 is direct and decisive: Abraham's faith was counted as righteousness while he was in a state of uncircumcision, not circumcision. Wesley establishes the chronology: Genesis 15 (where the counting happened) precedes Genesis 17 (where circumcision was instituted) by approximately fourteen to fifteen years. Clarke confirms: Genesis 15:6 precedes the circumcision of Genesis 17:1 by that interval; Ishmael was thirteen years old at the time of circumcision, and he had not yet been born when faith was counted as righteousness. Barnes draws out the significance for the argument: if the Jew claims that Abraham's faith was acceptable because he was circumcised, this chronology destroys the claim entirely. The imputation of righteousness and the institution of circumcision were not only separate acts but separated by over a decade. RWP makes the grammatical point: Paul's question — "in circumcision or in uncircumcision?" — is a pertinent inquiry "that the average Jew had not noticed," and the answer, once established from Genesis, is unanswerable. Ellicott summarizes: Abraham was justified in uncircumcision — a fact that opens the Abrahamic covenant to all who believe, regardless of their circumcision status.

voices.html: Not in circumcision — not after he was circumcised; for he was justified before Ishmael was born, Genesis 15:1–21; but he was not circumcised till Ishmael was thirteen years old, Genesis 17:25. The chronological gap of some fourteen or fifteen years between the justification and the circumcision is decisive: circumcision could not have been the ground or condition of the righteousness already received.

How was it then reckoned? In what circumstances was Abraham when this blessing was bestowed upon him? Not in circumcision, but in uncircumcision. Faith was reckoned to Abraham for justification, as we read in Genesis 15:6; but circumcision was not instituted till about fourteen or fifteen years after, Genesis 17:1. For faith was reckoned to Abraham for righteousness before he was circumcised, before the law of circumcision existed.

When he was in circumcision or in uncircumcision? This was the very point of the inquiry. For if he was justified by faith after he was circumcised, the Jew might pretend that it was in virtue of his circumcision. But if it was before he was circumcised, this plea could not be set up; and the argument of the apostle was confirmed by the case of Abraham, the great father of the Jewish people himself.

11.synthesis: Verse 11 establishes the proper theological function of circumcision: it was a sign and seal of the righteousness that Abraham had by faith while still uncircumcised. This is the classic Pauline statement of sacramental theology — the rite confirms, ratifies, and visibly marks what faith has already received, but does not confer what faith receives. Calvin notes that circumcision was not unprofitable or superfluous — it had a very remarkable use as a seal — but that "it was not the cause of righteousness; it indeed tended to confirm the righteousness of faith, and that already obtained in uncircumcision." RWP explains the Greek: the sign of circumcision is a genitive of apposition (circumcision being the sign); the seal of righteousness is what it confirms. Ellicott distinguishes the two terms: a sign is any token that represents something; a seal is the formal legal confirmation that ratifies a document. Clarke draws the pastoral conclusion: Abraham received justification before circumcision, then received circumcision as the outward seal of what he already had. The practical corollary follows immediately: Abraham is thus the father of all who believe in uncircumcision — the Gentiles — who receive the same righteousness by the same faith without the rite.

voices.html: He shows that circumcision was not unprofitable and superfluous, though it could not justify; but it had another very remarkable use — it had the office of sealing, and as it were of ratifying the righteousness of faith. And yet he intimates at the same time, by stating what its object was, that it was not the cause of righteousness; it indeed tended to confirm the righteousness of faith, and that already obtained in uncircumcision. He then derogates or takes away nothing from it.

The sign of circumcision — circumcision as a sign. The expression is an instance of the genitive of apposition. The Apostle here puts forth his view of the real import of circumcision. It was not the cause of the righteousness, but the seal — the formal legal confirmation — of a righteousness already possessed by faith. Abraham is therefore the father both of faithful uncircumcised and of faithful circumcised: in both cases faith is what qualifies.

The sign of circumcision (sēmeion peritomēs). It is the genitive of apposition — circumcision being the sign. A seal of the righteousness of the faith (sphragida tēs dikaiosynēs tēs pisteōs). Sphragis is the stamp made by the seal, that by which anything is confirmed. The circumcision was the confirming seal of a righteousness received by faith in uncircumcision — confirming what was already there, not conferring what was absent.

12.synthesis: The corollary for Jewish believers: Abraham is also the father of the circumcision — but only of those among the circumcised who walk in the steps of his faith. Calvin notes the precision required here: he touches the carnal descendants of Abraham who, having nothing but outward circumcision, confidently gloried in it. The chief matter — the faith of Abraham by which alone he obtained salvation — they had neglected. Ellicott states it directly: mere performance of the rite was no guarantee of justification unless attended by faith like Abraham's. Barnes develops the pastoral nuance: though all believers are saved in the same way Abraham was, yet the Jews have a special proprietorship in Abraham as their physical ancestor. Paul does not deny this privilege — he affirms it — but insists that the privilege is only realized by those who also share his faith. Wesley is pointed: "And the father of the circumcision — of those who are circumcised, and believe as Abraham did. To those who believe not, Abraham is not a father, neither are they his seed." RWP wrestles with a grammatical difficulty in the Greek (tois stoichousin) but the sense is clear: the true heirs of Abraham are those who walk in his footsteps, specifically in the faith that preceded and governs the circumcision.

voices.html: He touches the carnal descendants of Abraham, who, having nothing but outward circumcision, confidently gloried in it. The other thing, which was the chief matter, they neglected; for the faith of Abraham, by which alone he obtained salvation, they did not imitate. It hence appears how carefully he distinguished between faith and the sacrament — that no one might be satisfied with the one without the other as though it were sufficient for justifying.

And on the other hand, the mere performance of the rite was no guarantee for justification, unless it was attended with a faith like Abraham's. Of the two things — faith itself, and circumcision the sign of faith — the first only was essential, and the second was useless without it. The rite without the faith was empty; the faith without the rite was fully effective, as Abraham's own case demonstrates.

And the father of the circumcision — of those who are circumcised, and believe as Abraham did. To those who believe not, Abraham is not a father, neither are they his seed. The physical descent is not the determinative thing: belief is. Abraham's true children are those who share his faith, whether circumcised or uncircumcised, Jew or Gentile.

13.synthesis: Paul now broadens the argument: it was not through the law but through the righteousness of faith that Abraham received the promise of being heir of the world. Calvin observes that this more clearly sets the law and faith in opposition to each other: if faith borrows nothing from the law in order to justify, then it has respect to nothing else but the mercy of God. The scope of the promise is vast: "heir of the world" — Ellicott notes that the Jews interpreted this as the universal sovereignty of the Messiah; Wesley and Barnes read it as the promise that all nations would be blessed through Abraham's seed. Clarke explains: Abraham is represented as having all the world given to him as his inheritance because in him all nations of the earth are blessed through Christ, and in him all mankind have their representative head of the covenant of grace. RWP points out that no single verse in Genesis says exactly "heir of the world" — it is Paul's synthesis of the whole chain of promises about Abraham's seed, his descendants as the stars and dust, and the blessing to the world through him. The argumentative point is tight: that promise was made when there was no law of Moses and no circumcision requirement. Therefore it was always a promise through faith.

voices.html: He now more clearly sets the law and faith in opposition the one to the other, which he had before in some measure done; and this ought to be carefully observed: for if faith borrows nothing from the law in order to justify, we hence understand that it has respect to nothing else but the mercy of God. The romance of those who would have this to have been said of ceremonies may be easily disproved; for if works contributed anything towards justification, it ought not to have been said through the written law but rather through works.

Abraham was the father of all who walk in his steps. For this "all" is not limited by the Law any more than it is limited by circumcision. The promise of that world-wide inheritance was not given through the agency of the Law — which at that time did not exist — but as an effect of the righteousness which proceeds from faith. Heir of the world — this promise was explained by the Jews of the universal sovereignty of the Messiah.

For the promise that he should be heir of the world — to show that the faith of Abraham, on which his justification depended, was not by the law, the apostle proceeds to show that the promise concerning which his faith was so remarkably evinced was before the law was given. An heir is one who succeeds, or is to succeed, to an estate. The promise here refers to the succession to be given to all the earth — to the universal blessing of all nations through him.

14.synthesis: The logical impossibility of law-based inheritance is stated sharply: if those who are of the law are heirs, then faith is emptied and the promise is nullified. Calvin's argument from what is impossible or absurd: if the condition of inheritance were legal perfection, no one could ever feel confident of belonging; the inheritance would be perpetually in doubt. Barnes makes the logical point: if men are justified by law, they cannot be by faith, and faith is useless in that scheme. Wesley focuses on the promise: a promise looks to the future; its design is to excite trust in the one who made it. But law-based inheritance removes the promise from the picture — it substitutes a contract for a covenant, wages for a gift. Ellicott notes the perfect tenses: faith "has been made void" (kekenōtai) and the promise "has been permanently rendered idle" (katērgētai) — these would be the permanent consequences if law were the basis. RWP cites both verbs as perfect passive indicatives expressing permanent state: if law is the ground, both faith and promise are locked into permanent uselessness. The force of the argument is not merely logical but redemptive: if the inheritance depends on law, no sinner can receive it — but all are sinners, so no one can receive it at all.

voices.html: He takes his argument from what is impossible or absurd: the favor which Abraham obtained from God was not promised to him through any legal agreement or through any regard to works; for if this condition had been interposed — that God would favor those only with adoption who deserved or who performed the law — no one could have dared to feel confident that it belonged to him. Void then would faith be, and no promise could stand.

Is made void — literally, emptied of its meaning, becomes an empty name, and the promise is rendered nugatory. There is nothing left for either to do, if the votaries of law, simply as such, are to be the inheritors of the Messianic kingdom. The two perfect passive indicatives express the permanent state that would obtain if law were the basis — faith permanently voided, promise permanently annulled.

For if they which are of the law are heirs — who seek for justification and acceptance by the law. Faith is made void — faith would have no place in the scheme. And the promise is of no effect — a promise looks to the future and its design is to excite trust. But if the law is the basis, there is no room for promise, since law deals in contracts and penalties, not in free covenants of grace.

15.synthesis: The reason law cannot serve as the ground of inheritance: it produces wrath, not righteousness. Calvin states the principle precisely: the law can indeed show to the good and the perfect the way of life, but as it prescribes to the sinful and corrupt what they ought to do without supplying any power for doing, it exhibits them as guilty before God's tribunal. Barnes develops the generalization: not just the Mosaic law but all law, considered as a moral standard, produces this effect on fallen humanity. Law demands; it does not enable; it condemns those who fail. The second clause is equally important: "where there is no law, there is no transgression." Ellicott explains: transgression (parabasis) is by definition the breach of an existing law; in the absence of explicit law, the specific character of transgression does not exist. Wesley qualifies this carefully: the clause refers not to the age before all moral awareness, but to the observation that law and transgression are correlative — you cannot have one without the other. RWP is terse: "There is no responsibility for the violation of a non-existent law." The implication for the promise: if the inheritance rested on law, it would produce only wrath and transgression; the only way the promise can be sure is if it rests on grace and faith, not law.

voices.html: This is a confirmation of the last verse, derived from the contrary effect of the law; for as the law generates nothing but vengeance, it cannot bring grace. It can indeed show to the good and the perfect the way of life; but as it prescribes to the sinful and corrupt what they ought to do, and supplies them with no power for doing, it exhibits them as guilty before the tribunal of God. For such is the viciousness of our nature that the more we are taught what is right and just, the more openly is our iniquity discovered.

But in reality the Law is unable to admit them to the inheritance. It has an entirely contrary function — namely, to call down punishment upon the offences that it reveals. The Law and faith mutually exclude each other, and faith is left to be the sole arbiter of salvation. Where no law is — transgression is by definition the transgression or breach of law, and therefore has no existence in the absence of explicit legal prohibition.

Because the law — all law. It is the tendency of law. Worketh wrath — produces or causes wrath. While man is fallen and a sinner, its tendency, so far from justifying him and producing peace, is just the reverse. It condemns, denounces wrath, and produces suffering. Where there is no law — there is no transgression. There is no responsibility for the violation of a non-existent law; and where there is no transgression there can be no punishment.

16.synthesis: The conclusion of the argument: it is of faith so that it might be of grace, in order that the promise might be sure to all the seed. The three-fold logic is tight and elegant. Calvin summarizes: faith is the instrument, grace is the character of the gift, and certainty is the result. If the inheritance depended on law-keeping, its security would depend on human performance — which always fails. RWP explains the grammatical structure: "of faith" (ek pisteōs) names the source; "according to grace" (kata charin) names the pattern or character; "to the end that the promise might be sure" states the purpose. The security of the promise is grounded in the divine character, not in human achievement. Barnes develops the pastoral force: if men were justified by law, it would be by their own merits, and those merits are always uncertain and inadequate; now it is of unmerited favor and therefore sure. Ellicott extends the scope: "to all the seed" — not only to that part of humanity belonging to the dispensation of the law, but also to that which is in a spiritual sense descended from Abraham by imitating his faith. Wesley states the corollary: God's free promise, grounded in grace and received through faith, includes all who believe, Jew and Gentile alike.

voices.html: This is the winding up of the argument. You may summarily include the whole of it in this statement: the heirship of salvation comes to us by faith, so that its stability being based on the goodness of God alone may be secured. See how the Apostle, regarding faith as a thing firm and certain, considers hesitancy and doubt as unbelief, by which faith is abolished and the promise abolished with it. The certainty of the promise rests on the character of the Promiser, not on the performance of the recipient.

Therefore it is of faith — the words "it is" have to be supplied. "It" stands for the Messianic inheritance, or, in common phrase, salvation. Faith on man's part is correlative with grace on the part of God, and salvation being thus dependent upon grace is as wide and universal as grace itself. It knows no restriction of law — not only to that part of the human race which belongs to the dispensation of the Law, but also to that which is in a spiritual sense descended from Abraham by imitating his faith.

Therefore it is of faith — in view of the course of reasoning which has been pursued. That it might be by grace — as a matter of mere undeserved mercy. If men were justified by law, it would be by their own merits; now it is of mere unmerited favour. To the end that the promise might be sure — stable, firm, established. The promise resting on God's faithfulness rather than man's achievement is as secure as God himself is faithful.

17.synthesis: The citation of Genesis 17:5 — "I have made you the father of many nations" — grounds the argument in the divine act of naming. Paul notes that Abraham stood before a God who gives life to the dead and calls into existence things that do not exist. Calvin explains the circuitous form: by his example an opening is made for the Gentiles; Abraham had to believe in a wonderful way, since there was no visible token of the promise. A seed was promised as though he were in vigor and strength, but he was as it were dead — he had therefore to raise his thoughts to the power of God by which the dead are quickened. Wesley captures the theological height of the divine speech: "The dead are not dead to God, and even the things that are not yet are before God. And calling the things that are not — summoning them to rise into being and appear before him." The seed of Abraham did not then exist; God said "So shall thy seed be" as though they already were. Ellicott notes the key phrase: Abraham is regarded as confronting the Almighty, face to face, as he had done when the promise was first given. The God before whom he stands is the one who both raises the dead and speaks new realities into existence — which is exactly the divine power required for the fulfilment of the promise.

voices.html: In this circuitous form is expressed the very substance of Abraham's faith, that by his example an opening might be made for the Gentiles. He had indeed to attain, in a wonderful way, the promise which he had heard from the Lord's mouth, since there was then no token of it. A seed was promised to him as though he was in vigor and strength; but he was as it were dead. It was hence necessary for him to raise up his thoughts to the power of God, by which the dead are quickened.

Before him — in the presence of. These words are to be connected closely with those which precede the parenthesis: "Who stands as the father of us all in the presence of that God in whom he believed." Abraham is regarded as confronting the Almighty, as he had done when the promise was first given to him. Who quickeneth — who gives life to that which is dead, and issues his fiat to that which is not as though it were.

Before God — though before men nothing of this appeared, those nations being then unborn. As quickening the dead — the dead are not dead to him, and even the things that are not, are before God. And calling the things that are not — summoning them to rise into being and appear before him. The seed of Abraham did not then exist; yet God said, "So shall thy seed be." A man can say to his servant "Do this" and it is done; God says to that which is not "Be" and it is.

18.synthesis: "Against hope he believed in hope" — the oxymoron is precise. RWP translates: "Past hope in (upon) hope he trusted" — a graphic picture of faith that exceeds its objective grounds. Calvin develops the point: nothing is more injurious to faith than to fasten our minds to our eyes and seek from what we see a reason for our hope. Faith flies upward on celestial wings and looks down on the perceptions of the flesh as on things of little worth. Ellicott notes the grammatical distinction: the relative pronoun at the start of verse 18 refers to Abraham (not to God as in verse 17), and the phrase "believed in hope" or better "grounded his faith upon hope" indicates that the internal subjective hope within Abraham was strong even when there were no objective external grounds for hoping. Barnes states the natural impossibility: the reference is to the prospect of posterity when Abraham had no child and both he and Sarah were past natural capacity. Wesley links the two dimensions of the hope: by faith Abraham grasped what God had spoken as true; by hope he anticipated as good what God had promised as his. The content of the hope is expressed by the Genesis citation: "So shall your offspring be" — as numerous as the stars, though as yet there was not one.

voices.html: If we read "beyond hope," the sense is that when there was no probable reason, yea, when all things were against him, he yet continued to believe. There is nothing more injurious to faith than to fasten our minds to our eyes, that we may from what we see seek a reason for our hope. We may also read "above hope" — perhaps more suitably; as though he had said that by his faith he far surpassed all that he could conceive; for except faith flies upward on celestial wings, the perceptions of the flesh will soon overcome it.

Who — it must be noticed that the relative here refers to Abraham, whereas in the previous verse it referred to God. Believed in hope — the force of the preposition gives rather to the sentence the meaning of "grounded his faith upon hope" — that internal subjective hope that was strong within him, though there were no objective grounds for hoping. That he might become — so as by exercise of faith to carry out God's purpose.

In hope believed against hope (par' elpida ep' elpidi episteusen). "Past hope in (upon) hope he trusted." Graphic picture. The first phrase (par' elpida) states the objective impossibility; the second (ep' elpidi) states the subjective ground upon which faith rested. Faith does not deny the natural impossibility; it transcends it by fixing on the divine promise.

19.synthesis: The textual tradition divides on a single word in verse 19, and that division produces two significantly different readings of Abraham's faith. The older manuscripts (supported by Ellicott and reflected in many modern translations) omit the negative: Abraham, being not weak in faith, considered the deadness of his own body — he fully took stock of the natural impossibility — and yet did not doubt. This reading presents Abraham's faith as courageous engagement with reality: he looked the impediment in the face and believed through it. The majority text tradition includes the negative: he was not weak in faith, not considering his own body's deadness — he ignored the obstacle and simply trusted the promise. Calvin follows the reading that omits the negative, interpreting it as Abraham withdrawing his mind from the sense of his own weakness to fix on the power of God. Barnes reads in the more common sense of not permitting the impediment to influence his faith: "He did not suffer that fact to influence him." Clarke captures the energy of the faith: "God cannot lie; Abraham can believe." Ellicott makes the textual point explicitly: the negative should be omitted per the best manuscripts, giving a reading where Abraham's faith is strong precisely because it does not flinch from the facts but overcomes them.

voices.html: Considered not — the negative should, in accordance with the evidence of the best MSS., be omitted. "Who, because he was not weak in faith, considered indeed — took full account of — the natural impediments to the fulfilment of the promise, and yet did not doubt." This reading presents Abraham's faith as stronger, not weaker, than the alternative: he saw the impossibility clearly and believed through it.

He indeed shows now more fully what might have hindered, yea, and wholly turned Abraham aside from receiving the promise. A seed from Sarah was promised to him at a time when he was not by nature fit for generating, nor Sarah for conceiving. Whatever he could see as to himself was opposed to the accomplishment of the promise. Hence, that he might yield to the truth of God, he withdrew his mind from those things which presented themselves to his own view, and turned it to the power of God.

He considered not — he did not regard the fact that his body was now dead as any obstacle to the fulfilment of the promise. He did not suffer that fact to influence him or to produce any doubt about the fulfilment. Faith looks to the strength of God, not to second causes or to difficulties that may appear formidable to man. Now dead — aged; dead as to the purpose under consideration. He was too old, in the course of nature, to have children.

19.key_tension: The best manuscripts omit the negative, giving "he considered the deadness of his body and yet did not doubt" (Ellicott); the majority text tradition includes the negative, giving "he did not consider the deadness of his body" — two different portraits of Abraham's faith, both emphasizing his trust in the divine promise over natural impossibility.

20.synthesis: "He did not waver in unbelief but grew strong in faith, giving glory to God" — the positive statement of what verse 19 denied. The verb RWP identifies is diekrinē: "he was not divided in his mind by unbelief" (instrumental case). Wavering or staggering (diekrithē) is the internal division of a mind pulled between trust in God and trust in appearances — Abraham had no such division. Calvin observes that while all men acknowledge God's power in theory, experience proves that nothing is more difficult than to give that acknowledgment its full weight in practice. There is no obstacle, however small, by which the flesh imagines the hand of God is restrained from working; in the slightest trials, the promises of God slip away. Abraham's faith was precisely the exception to this pattern: he gave God's omnipotence its full honor. Ellicott's gloss on "giving glory to God" is careful: it does not necessarily mean a verbal ascription of praise but anything that tends to God's glory — here it is the frank recognition of God's omnipotence involved in Abraham's trust. Barnes develops the commendation: faith that believes where everything seems to deny the promise is the highest form of honor to God, bearing testimony that he is worthy of implicit confidence.

voices.html: As all men acknowledge God's power, Paul seems to say nothing very extraordinary of the faith of Abraham; but experience proves that nothing is more uncommon or more difficult than to ascribe to God's power the honor which it deserves. There is indeed no obstacle, however small and insignificant, by which the flesh imagines the hand of God is restrained from working. Hence it is that in the slightest trials, the promises of God slide away from us. Abraham's faith gave God's omnipotence its full and undivided honor.

In faith — better, through or by faith, corresponding to "through unbelief" in the preceding clause. Unbelief did not make him doubt, but faith made him confident and strong. Giving glory to God — this phrase does not necessarily refer to a verbal ascription of praise, but to anything which tends to God's glory, whether in thought, word, or deed; here it is applied to the frank recognition of God's omnipotence involved in Abraham's trust in the promise.

He wavered not through unbelief (ou diekrithē tēi apistiai). First aorist passive indicative of diakrinō, to separate, to decide between, to be divided in one's own mind. "He was not divided in his mind by unbelief" (instrumental case). Waxed strong through faith (enedynamōthē tēi pistei). First aorist passive of endynamoō. Faith was the instrument, not the inherent source, of the strength: God empowered him through the exercise of trust.

21.synthesis: "Being fully assured that God was able to do what he had promised" — the content of Abraham's faith is here specified as certainty about divine omnipotence applied to a specific promise. RWP explains the verb: plērophorētheis means "full measure," fully settled or fully assured — not a general conviction about God's power in the abstract but the settled persuasion that this particular promise would be kept. Calvin remarks that the difficulty is not in confessing that God is omnipotent as a theoretical proposition — all men do that — but in believing it in the specific circumstance where natural appearances argue against it. Abraham's faith was not general theism but personal, applied trust: God has promised this; God is able; therefore this will be. Barnes notes the parallel with the faith exercised at the sacrifice of Isaac (Hebrews 11:19): the same implicit confidence that what God had promised he would fulfill, even if it required raising the dead. Clarke traces the Greek precisely: plērophorētheis comes from plērophorō, to bring full; his soul was full of confidence that the truth of God bound him to fulfill his promise and his power enabled him to do it. Wesley is brief and definitive: "being fully persuaded that he who had promised was also able to perform."

voices.html: As all men acknowledge God's power, Paul seems to say nothing very extraordinary of the faith of Abraham; but experience proves that nothing is more uncommon than to ascribe to God's power the honor which it deserves. There is no obstacle, however small, by which the flesh does not imagine the hand of God to be restrained. Abraham's being fully persuaded was not general theism but the specific, applied trust that this promise — about a seed when he was as good as dead — would be kept.

And being fully persuaded — plērophorētheis, his measure was full: his soul was full of confidence that the truth of God bound him to fulfill his promise and his power enabled him to do it. The word comes from plērophorō, to bring full measure. Abraham was not moderately confident or generally hopeful; he was filled to capacity with assurance that the specific promise would be kept.

And being fully persuaded — thoroughly or entirely convinced. He was able — this was not the only time in which Abraham evinced this confidence. His faith was equally implicit and strong when he was commanded to sacrifice his promised son, Hebrews 11:19. The willingness to offer Isaac is the supreme demonstration that Abraham's persuasion of God's ability extended even to the resurrection of the dead.

22.synthesis: "Therefore his faith was counted to him as righteousness" — the conclusion of the Abraham argument returns to Genesis 15:6 and establishes that the faith described in verses 17–21 is precisely what warranted the imputation. Calvin notes that the verse lacks a nominative in the Greek (as frequently in Hebrew and Greek, where the person is included in the verb itself) and observes that "it becomes now more clear why his faith was thus counted." The content of the faith — trust in God's power to keep his specific promise against all natural impossibility — is what was counted. Barnes develops the point: such unwavering, fully-persuaded trust was a demonstration that Abraham was the firm friend of God, supremely attached to him, willing to obey and serve him; this was reckoned as a full proof of friendship, and he was treated accordingly. Clarke paraphrases Dr. Taylor: "God was graciously pleased to place his faith to his account; and to allow his fiducial reliance upon the Divine goodness, power, and faithfulness for a title to the Divine blessing." The verse thus closes the loop from verse 3: the faith that was counted as righteousness in Genesis 15:6 was not a generic assent but the specific, costly, against-all-odds trust in the God who keeps promises.

voices.html: It becomes now more clear why his faith was thus counted: for he who, by laying aside all consideration of his own weakness, relies only on the power of God, thereby honors him as he deserves, and this Paul calls giving glory to God. Abraham, by his faith, gave God his due honor; and for this reason his faith was imputed to him for righteousness. The imputation follows from and responds to the trust.

And therefore it was imputed to him for righteousness — the verse is thus paraphrased by Dr. Taylor: "God was graciously pleased to place his faith to his account; and to allow his fiducial reliance upon the Divine goodness, power, and faithfulness for a title to the Divine blessing, which otherwise, having been an idolater, he had no right to." Abraham's strong faith in the promise of the coming Savior was essentially this: trust in Christ, the seed in whom all nations should be blessed.

And therefore — this faith was so implicit and so unwavering that it was a demonstration that he was the firm friend of God. He was tried, and he had such confidence in God that he showed that he was supremely attached to him and would obey and serve him. This was reckoned as a full proof of friendship; and he was recognized and treated as righteous — that is, as the friend of God. The imputation of righteousness is not the reward of merit but the recognition of genuine trust.

23.synthesis: The application begins: the record of Abraham's justification by faith was not written for Abraham's sake alone. Calvin reminds us that a proof from example is not always valid — "lest this should be questioned, Paul expressly affirms that in the person of Abraham was exhibited an example of a common righteousness, which belongs equally to all." The history of Abraham is not merely biography; it is didactic theology, written for the instruction of the whole Church. Barnes makes the same point: the record was not made on Abraham's behalf alone but to show the way in which men may be regarded and treated as righteous by God. If Abraham was so regarded, then on the same principle all others may be — God has but one mode of justifying men. Wesley is brief: "On his account only — to do personal honour to him" — that was not the purpose; the record has a much more extensive design. Clarke echoes: the fact of Abraham's believing and receiving salvation through faith is not recorded as a mere circumstance in the patriarch's life intended to do him honor. The Scriptural record functions as universal instruction: what God did for Abraham he will do for all who share Abraham's faith.

voices.html: A proof from example is not always valid; lest this should be questioned, Paul expressly affirms that in the person of Abraham was exhibited an example of a common righteousness which belongs equally to all. We are by this passage reminded of the duty of seeking profit from the examples recorded in Scripture. History is the teacher of what life ought to be; but Scripture alone justly claims the prerogative of teaching us what righteousness is before God.

Now it was not written for his sake alone — the fact of Abraham's believing and receiving salvation through that faith is not recorded as a mere circumstance in the patriarch's life intended to do him honor: see Romans 4:24. The mention of this circumstance has a much more extensive design than merely to honor Abraham. It is recorded as the model according to which God will save both Jews and Gentiles.

Now it was not written — the record of this extraordinary faith was not made on his account only. It was made to show the way in which men may be regarded and treated as righteous by God. If Abraham was so regarded and treated, then on the same principle all others may be. God has but one mode of justifying men. Imputed — reckoned; accounted. He was regarded and treated as the friend of God.

24.synthesis: "But for us also, who believe in him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead" — the application is now made explicit. Calvin explains the periphrastic expression: Paul introduced it "that he might, according to what the passages may require, describe in various ways the real character of faith — of which the resurrection of Christ is not the smallest part; for it is the ground of our hope as to eternal life." The resurrection is the Christian's Genesis 15:6: what Abraham trusted the promise of, we trust the fulfillment of. Ellicott draws the association of ideas: the birth of Isaac resembled the resurrection of Christ in that it involved the exercise of omnipotence, and in that omnipotence Abraham believed and we are to believe. Barnes develops the parallel: Abraham believed that God could bring life from a dead womb; we believe that God raised Jesus from the dead — both are trust in divine life-giving power applied to a specific promise. Wesley underscores the anti-reductionist point: Paul's argument answers those who would narrow "justification by faith" to mean only the adoption of Christianity as a religious system. Abraham could not be justified by "Christianity" in that sense; what he had was genuine trust in the God who keeps promises; and what we have is precisely that same trust, now directed at the fulfilled promise — the risen Lord.

voices.html: I have already reminded you of the design of those periphrastic expressions: Paul introduced them that he might, according to what the passages may require, describe in various ways the real character of faith — of which the resurrection of Christ is not the smallest part; for it is the ground of our hope as to eternal life. Had he said only that we believe in God, it could not have been so readily learnt how this could serve to obtain righteousness; but when Christ comes forth and presents to us in his own resurrection a sure pledge of life, faith then clearly shows its power.

That raised up Jesus — it is an association of ideas which leads the Apostle up to this point. The birth of Isaac resembles the resurrection of Christ in that it involved the exercise of Omnipotence, and in that Omnipotence Abraham believed and we are to believe. The Apostle is further led to allude to the Resurrection because of the place which it held in his theory of the gospel — it is the decisive act by which God vindicates his Son and ratifies the righteousness that is received by faith.

But on ours also — to establish us in seeking justification by faith and not by works; and to afford a full answer to those who say that "to be justified by works means only by Judaism; to be justified by faith means by embracing Christianity as a system of doctrines." Abraham could not in this sense be justified either by faith or by works; and equally sure it is that our justification is of the same kind as his — genuine trust in the God who keeps his promises.

25.synthesis: The chapter ends with a dense two-clause formula: Jesus "was delivered up for our trespasses and raised for our justification." Calvin notes the two senses of dia: the first introduces the cause (he was given over because of our offenses), and the second introduces the purpose (he was raised with a view to our justification). RWP refines this: the first dia is probably retrospective — because of our transgressions, to atone for them — while the second is clearly prospective — with a view to our justification, that it might take effect in us. Ellicott makes the theological clarification: the death of Christ is the proper cause of justification, the means of atonement; the resurrection of Christ is the mediate or secondary cause — it is the evidence and ratification of the completed atonement. Clarke states the pastoral logic: Christ was raised that we might have the fullest assurance that his death had accomplished the end for which it took place — our reconciliation to God, and the grounding of our title to eternal life. Barnes understands the verse as using "justification" in a large sense: not merely the formal act of pardon, but the full acceptance with God, including the completion of the work that makes that acceptance permanent. The resurrection is Paul's guarantee that the atonement has been accepted and the promise sealed.

voices.html: The preposition dia has here clearly two meanings: the first signifies the why and the second, the for which. He was delivered for our offenses — because of them, to deal with them. He was raised for our justification — with a view to it, that it might be realized in us. The resurrection does not produce the justification as its direct cause; rather it is the evidence and seal that the work of dealing with our offenses has been accepted by the Father.

For our offences (dia ta paraptōmata). From Isaiah 53:12. The first dia with paraptōmata is probably retrospective, though it will make sense as prospective (to make atonement for our transgressions). The second dia is quite clearly prospective, with a view to our justification. Paul does not mean to separate the resurrection from the death as the means of our justification, but simply shows that the resurrection is the capstone of the work.

Who was delivered to death — for our offences, on account of our crimes, in order that he might make expiation for our sins. And was raised again — from the dead. For our justification — in order that we may be justified. The word justification here seems to be used in a large sense, to denote acceptance with God; including not merely the formal act by which God pardons sins, but also the completion of the work that grounds and secures the pardon.

1.synthesis: Paul opens chapter 3 with the question a Jew would inevitably ask after the argument of chapter 2: if circumcision confers no advantage when the law is disobeyed, what is the point of being Jewish at all? The question is reasonable, not hostile, and Paul takes it seriously rather than dismissing it. Barnes notes that it flows naturally from the preceding argument — Paul had just contended that bare possession of the law without obedience gives no standing before God. Calvin's answer establishes the key distinction: any advantage the Jews possessed came not from their own merit or worthiness, but from God's free mercy in choosing them and sealing that choice with the sign of circumcision. The advantage was real — but it was theirs as stewards, not as proprietors.

voices.html: Though Paul has clearly proved that bare circumcision brought nothing to the Jews, yet since he could not deny but that there was some difference between the Gentiles and the Jews, which by that symbol was sealed to them by the Lord, it remained for him to assign to it its own praise, and to show what fruit they had obtained from it.

The design of the first part of this chapter is to answer some of the objections which might be offered by a Jew to the statements in the last chapter. A Jew would naturally ask, if the view which the apostle had given were correct, what peculiar benefit could the Jew derive from his religion? The objection was plausible, and it was important that it should be answered.

Literally, "What then is the overplus of the Jew?" What does the Jew have over and above the Gentile? It is a pertinent question after the stinging indictment of the Jew in chapter 2. The profit (ἡ ὠφελία) — the help. Old word, only here in the N.T.

2.synthesis: Paul's answer is emphatic: the advantage is great, and in every dimension. But he singles out one privilege above all others — the Jews were entrusted with the oracles of God, meaning the inspired Scriptures of the Old Testament. Wesley identifies this as the foundation for all other prerogatives, which Paul will list further in Romans 9:4-5. Ellicott notes the careful wording: the Scriptures were not merely given to them but entrusted to them, implying a divine confidence in them as custodians of revelation. Barnes observes that no people in history preserved a sacred text more faithfully than the Jews preserved these Scriptures. Calvin, while praising the privilege, is careful to insist that this trustworthiness was not owing to any merit of the Jews themselves but entirely to the free mercy of God. The oracles are a gift held on behalf of the world, not a trophy earned by Israel.

voices.html: He begins here to give the sacrament its own praise; but he concedes not, that on this account the Jews ought to have been proud; for when he teaches that they were sealed by the symbol of circumcision, he does not allow that they became superior to others through any merit or worthiness of their own, but through the free mercy of God.

Oracles. — A good translation; the Scriptures of the Old Testament as containing a revelation of God. Unto them were committed. — This is paraphrastic. "Oracle" is the object, not the subject. They were entrusted with these — implying a divine confidence placed in them as guardians of revelation.

Chiefly in that they were intrusted with the oracles of God — the scriptures, in which are so great and precious promises. Other prerogatives will follow, Romans 9:4–5. St. Paul here singles out this by which, after removing the objection, he will convict them so much the more.

The main benefit of being a Jew is to possess the sacred Scriptures, and their instructions. The word translated "were committed" is commonly employed to express confidence — it implied confidence in them on the part of God in entrusting his oracles to them; a confidence which was not misplaced, for no people ever preserved a sacred book with more fidelity than the Jews preserved the Old Testament.

3.synthesis: The next objection addresses the apparent failure of many Jews: if some did not believe, has their unbelief cancelled God's faithfulness to his covenant promises? Paul frames the question carefully — it is "some," not all, who failed, and the issue is whether human faithlessness can annul divine faithfulness. The answer, developed in verse 4, is no. Ellicott emphasizes that reducing the scope to "some" is itself an important move: it prevents the Jewish objector from claiming that Paul had condemned the entire nation. Calvin's contribution is structural — he shows that the virtue of the sign (circumcision) and the promises bound to it are not destroyed by Israel's inconstancy; divine faithfulness stands independent of human faithfulness. Clarke reads the objection from the Jewish side: if God has promised Abraham an everlasting covenant, surely the wickedness of some cannot abrogate the promise to the nation.

voices.html: As before, while regarding the Jews as exulting in the naked sign, he allowed them no spark of glory; so now, while considering the nature of the sign, he testifies that its virtue is not destroyed, no, not even by their inconstancy. He now, anticipating an objection, again asks what opinion was to be formed of the sign itself.

The Apostle considers an objection that might be brought against his argument. He first reduces the objection to its proper limits; it was not all, but some, who rejected. He then shows that even this partial rejection could not defeat the faithfulness of God, which stands or falls on its own divine ground.

If some of the Jewish nation have abused their privileges, and acted contrary to their obligations, shall their wickedness annul the Promise which God made to Abraham, that he would, by an everlasting covenant, be a God to him and to his seed after him? Shall God falsify his promise to the nation, because some of the Jews are bad men?

4.synthesis: Paul's reply is decisive: God forbid — μὴ γένοιτο, his characteristic expression of vehement denial. God is true even if every human being proves a liar. Paul then reaches for Psalm 51:4 — David's confession that even his own sin served to vindicate the justice of God when God judged him. Ellicott reads this boldly: let God be vindicated even before some imaginary higher tribunal. RWP notes that γινέσθω ὁ θεὸς ἀληθής is a present middle imperative — "let God continue to be true" — asserting ongoing divine faithfulness as a non-negotiable axiom. Barnes stresses that the veracity of God is the great fundamental principle on which all religion rests; once questioned, no religious conviction is stable. Calvin frames it as a logical necessity: human falsehood and divine truth always stand together — the fall of man does not and cannot nullify God's promises.

voices.html: For since these two things stand together, yea, necessarily accord — that God is true and that man is false — it follows that the truth of God is not nullified by the falsehood of men. And hence, no one can infer from the defection of some that the covenant of God was made void.

Impossible! Rather let God be seen to be true though all mankind should be proved false, even as the Psalmist looked upon his own sin as serving to enhance the triumph of God's justice. Speaking of that justice for the moment as if arraigned before a higher tribunal, he asserts its absolute and complete acquittal.

The veracity of God was a great fundamental principle; to admit any question of it would shake the foundations of all religion. God should be esteemed true to all his promises, threatening, and declarations; and rather than admit that any word of his had failed, we should regard every man as false and erring.

"Let God continue to be true" (present middle imperative). The contrast means, "though every man be found a liar." Psalm 51:6 — "That thou mightest be justified" — Paul uses the LXX closely, where David's confession of sin becomes the occasion for God's righteousness to be publicly displayed.

5.synthesis: Paul anticipates a deeper objection born of the same logic: if human unrighteousness commends — actually serves to show up — the righteousness of God, is God unjust to inflict wrath on the very people whose sins glorified him? Paul flags the line of reasoning as human weakness: "I speak as a man." Calvin sees this as a necessary digression — the opponents were primed to use even the testimony of David as cover for the argument that sinners should not be punished if their sin glorifies God. Ellicott admires the intellectual integrity of raising this question at all; Paul's keen mind will not let a genuine logical problem pass unanswered. The force of the objection, as Barnes identifies it, is this: if God's righteousness was actually enhanced by Israel's sin, was not that sin in some sense necessary and therefore excusable? Paul refuses to let this stand but handles it carefully, aware that it is a question a serious person would ask.

voices.html: Though this is a digression from the main subject, it was yet necessary for the Apostle to introduce it, lest he should seem to give to the ill-disposed an occasion to speak evil. For since they were watching for every opportunity to defame the gospel, they had, in the testimony of David, what they might have taken for the purpose of founding a calumny.

A new and profound question suggests itself to the mind of the Apostle, and his keen intellect will not let it go: "If the sin of man only tends to vindicate the righteousness of God, why should that sin be punished?" The mere raising of such a question requires an apology; it is only as a man might speak about man that he dares to utter such a thought.

The force of the objection is this: God threatened to punish the guilty. But the very guilt of the people caused his character to shine out more illustriously than it otherwise would. The question then was whether, if his character was promoted by sin, God could be just in punishing those whose sins had been so serviceable to his own glory.

6.synthesis: Paul's answer to verse 5 is brief and devastating: God forbid — by no means. He does not reason through the objection directly but instead poses a counter-question that exposes its absurdity. If God cannot punish sin that serves to illustrate his righteousness, then he cannot judge the world at all — and everyone assumed he could and would. Ellicott notes that Paul considers this counter-question a sufficient answer on its own; both Paul and his readers shared the assumption of a future universal judgment, making the objection self-refuting. Calvin argues that expressing abhorrence of the logic is more weighty than offering a simple denial: the reasoning was so impious it deserved to be heard with horror. Wesley captures the reductio ad absurdum most cleanly: if it is unjust to punish unrighteousness subservient to God's glory, then since all human unrighteousness glorifies God somehow, no one could ever be condemned — which makes judgment impossible.

voices.html: In checking this blasphemy he gives not a direct reply to the objection, but begins with expressing his abhorrence of it, lest the Christian religion should even appear to include absurdities so great. And this is more weighty than if he adopted a simple denial; for he implies, that this impious expression deserved to be regarded with horror, and not to be heard.

St. Paul considers it a sufficient answer merely to propound this question. He and those to whom he was writing all assumed that there must be a future judgment. If that assumption is granted, the objection in verse 5 collapses of itself: a God who cannot punish sin cannot judge.

God forbid — By no means. If it were unjust in God to punish that unrighteousness which is subservient to his own glory, how should God judge the world — since all the unrighteousness in the world will then commend the righteousness of God.

7.synthesis: Paul returns once more to the same objection, now stated in the first person: if my falsehood makes God's truth abound to his glory, why am I still judged as a sinner? The repetition is deliberate — the apostle is letting the objection run its course before cutting it off. RWP notes that Paul uses the first person "from motives of delicacy" (Sanday and Headlam), putting the objection in his own mouth rather than attributing it to a specific party. Ellicott explains the key word: "my lie" is not mere falsehood but the moral deflection that follows from unbelief — the lie of living as though God's truth could be safely ignored. Wesley sharpens the structure: the objector argues that since God's truth shines the more brightly by contrast with our moral failure, our failure must be not merely pardonable but arguably praiseworthy. Paul refuses to dignify this with a direct answer, cutting it off in verse 8 instead.

voices.html: My lie — suggested as an antithesis to the word "truth" just used, has also a moral signification. It is the moral deflection that follows upon unbelief. The Apostle puts his supposed case in the first person; the objector argues, if my moral failure only made God's truth shine more brilliantly, how can that failure be treated as sin?

But, may the objector reply, if the truth of God hath abounded — has been more abundantly shown — through my lie — if my practice contrary to truth conduces to the glory of God, by making his truth shine with superior advantage — why am I still judged as a sinner? Can this be said to be any sin at all?

Paul "uses the first person from motives of delicacy" (Sanday and Headlam) in this supposable case for argument's sake. So here he "transfers by a fiction" (Field) to himself the objection — letting the question surface under his own name rather than leaving it at the door of any specific opponent.

8.synthesis: Paul ends the diatribe of verses 5-7 not with a formal refutation but by exposing where the logic leads. If sin that glorifies God excuses the sinner, then the right course of action would be to sin as much as possible. This is the conclusion some were already slanderously attributing to Paul's own preaching of free grace. He refuses to answer that slander directly — the sheer monstrousness of the conclusion is refutation enough. Calvin notes that the answer to the antinomian argument is the logical absurdity: the pretense would justify total moral license in the name of promoting God's glory. Barnes makes the pastoral point: if carried out to its conclusion, this reasoning would require doing all possible evil in order to advance divine glory — a result so shocking that exposing it is sufficient. Wesley is most direct: Paul absolutely denies the lawfulness of "doing evil" — any evil — that good may come.

voices.html: The Apostle deigns not to answer the slander; yet we may check it by the most solid reason. The pretense is, "Let us sin to advance God's glory." The answer is, that the pretense would justify any conceivable wickedness — and a pretext so manifestly wicked overthrows itself.

He meets the objection by showing its tendency if carried out. "If the glory of God is to be promoted by sin, and if a man is not therefore to be condemned for it, why not carry the doctrine out, and do all the evil we can, in order to promote his glory?" This was the fair consequence — and yet a result so shocking and monstrous that all that was necessary was to make it plain.
Whose condemnation is just — the condemnation of all who either speak or act in this manner. So the apostle absolutely denies the lawfulness of "doing evil," any evil, "that good may come."
