9.synthesis: Having addressed the objections, Paul returns to his main argument. Are Jews, then, better off than Gentiles in the matter of justification and standing before God? The Greek verb is genuinely ambiguous — it can mean "do we excel" or "are we excelled" — but the context requires Paul's answer: Not at all. Wesley captures the force simply: both Jews and Gentiles are under sin, each by the law peculiar to them — the Jews by the written law, the Gentiles by the law of nature. Calvin notes the apparent tension with verse 2: Paul had just said the Jews had great advantages. The resolution is that the advantage of possessing Scripture is real, but it is an advantage in means and privileges, not in moral standing; on that ground all are level. Ellicott's contribution is syntactical: there is no real contradiction between "much in every way" (v. 2) and the denial here, because the two statements address different questions — the first is about privilege, the second about character and standing before God.

voices.html: He returns from his digression to his subject. For lest the Jews should object that they were deprived of their right, he now at length replies to the question — in what respect they excelled the Gentiles. His answer seems paradoxical, because he had said previously that the privilege of the Jews was great. But this is the resolution: he had spoken before of the gift of God; he speaks now of what they merited by their own conduct.

"Can we claim a preference?" There is no real opposition between the "by no means" of the reply and the "much every way" of verse 2. The earlier statement concerned privilege — external advantages given by God. This statement concerns character and moral standing, on which ground Jew and Gentile are on precisely the same footing.

Under sin — under the guilt and power of it: the Jews, by transgressing the written law; the gentiles, by transgressing the law of nature. All are under sin — not just some, not just the scandalously wicked, but the whole of humanity without distinction of birth or privilege.

10.synthesis: To prove his assertion Paul turns to the Scriptures themselves — a catena of Old Testament quotations that will run through verse 18. The first citation is from Psalms 14 and 53: "There is none righteous, no, not one." Calvin notes the argumentative significance of moving from reasoning to authority; for those who receive Scripture, this is the most forceful kind of proof. RWP identifies Paul's method as the use of a Testimonia — a chain of quotations that early Christians and Jews assembled to prove theological points. Wesley adds the important clarification that David and Isaiah spoke primarily of their own age, but expressed what God sees when he "looks down from heaven" — the divine view of humanity in its natural, unredeemed state. Barnes stresses the solemnity of the divine gaze: God himself conducted the investigation, which gives the verdict peculiar weight.

voices.html: He has hitherto used proofs or arguments to convince men of their iniquity; he now begins to reason from authority — and it is to Christians the strongest kind of proof, when authority is derived from the only true God. And hence let ecclesiastical teachers learn what their office is, since Paul asserts here no truth but what he confirms by the testimony of Scripture.

As it is written — that all men are under sin appears from the vices which have raged in all ages. St. Paul rightly cites David and Isaiah, though they spoke primarily of their own age, and expressed what manner of men God sees when he "looks down from heaven." There is none righteous — this is the general proposition; the particulars follow.

The apostle is reasoning with Jews; and he proceeds to show, from their own Scriptures, that what he had affirmed was true. In the Hebrew, God is represented as looking down from heaven to see whether there were any that understood or sought after him. This circumstance gives not only high poetic beauty, but deep solemnity — God, the Searcher of hearts, making investigation on this very point.

11.synthesis: The catena continues with two parallel negative statements about human understanding and moral seeking. There is none that understands — the things of God; there is none that seeks after God. Calvin presses the depth of the first: empty is the man who lacks knowledge of God, however learned he may be in other things. The arts and sciences, good in themselves, are hollow without God as their foundation and end. Wesley is brief but sharp: "the things of God" are what is not understood. Barnes adds detail drawn from the Hebrew of Psalm 14: God is depicted as looking down from heaven to make investigation — the framing of divine searching adds solemn weight to the finding that none sought him. RWP explains the Greek: συνιών = "to send together, to grasp, to comprehend" — both the intellectual and volitional failures are captured in the one word, as the same participle construction covers both understanding and seeking.

voices.html: The first effect of universal sinfulness is that there is none that understands; and then this ignorance is immediately proved — empty is the man in whom there is not the knowledge of God, whatever other learning he may possess. The sciences and the arts, which in themselves are good, are empty things when they are without this groundwork.

God is represented as looking down from heaven to see — to make investigation — whether there were any that understood or sought after him. This circumstance gives not only high poetic beauty to the passage, but deep solemnity. God, the Searcher of hearts, made investigation on this very point — and found none.

That understandeth (συνιών). Present active participle of συνίημι, to send together, to grasp, to comprehend. The verb covers both the intellectual failure to grasp divine truth and the volitional failure to seek after God — joined here as twin symptoms of the same spiritual disorder.

12.synthesis: The catena continues: all have turned aside, together become worthless, and none does good — not even one. The verse amplifies the universality of v. 10 with three parallel clauses. Ellicott notes that "together" means not merely simultaneously but unanimously — the whole mass of humanity has turned aside as one body. Clarke unpacks the apostasy on both sides: the Jews forsaking the law and the prophets, the Gentiles acting contrary to the conscience God gave them. Wesley's contribution is that "become unprofitable" means helpless and impotent — unable to profit themselves or others. Barnes draws out the social dimension: they are united in their departure, all declining from the same true path of piety and virtue. The repetition of "none" — which has now appeared three times in vv. 10-12 — is Paul's rhetorical hammer: there are no exceptions, no moral remnant who can bypass the need for the righteousness about to be announced in v. 21.

voices.html: They are together become unprofitable. — Here the adjective is used to express a state of moral corruption and depravity. "Together" means "altogether" — the whole mass of mankind, with one consent, has fallen to ruin. Not a partial corruption leaving some sector of humanity intact.

They have all diverged from the right way — they have either abandoned or corrupted the worship of God: the Jews, in forsaking the law and the prophets; and the Gentiles, in acting contrary to the law written in their hearts, and the dictates of their own consciences. Together they have become unprofitable — useless both to God and man.

They have all turned aside — from the good way. They are become unprofitable — helpless, impotent, unable to profit either themselves or others. Not a single exception: "not even one" — the absolute universality of the verdict is Paul's entire point.

13.synthesis: The catena now turns from the fact of universal moral failure to its specific expression in corrupt speech, drawn from Psalms 5:9 and 140:3. Three images stack upon each other: the throat as an open sepulcher, deceit used by the tongue, and the poison of asps under the lips. Wesley observes the structural movement: evil discourse proceeds from the heart outward — through throat, tongue, lips — until the whole mouth is filled with it. Ellicott presses the image of the open grave: it is not merely a pit into which someone falls, but a pestiferous chasm ravening after victims, spreading corruption wherever its breath reaches. RWP notes that the Greek for "they have used deceit" is an imperfect tense — a continued, habitual action, not a single episode. Barnes connects the open-grave image to slander: as the grave ever-readies itself to swallow the living, so the slanderer's mouth is ever ready to swallow up the peace and reputation of others.

voices.html: Their throat is an open sepulchre — their speech is at once corrupt and corrupting. It is compared to a "yawning grave" — not merely to a pit into which a man may fall, but to a sort of pestiferous chasm yawning and ravening, as it were, after its prey. Under their lips — as the poison-bag of the serpent is directly under the tooth by which its venom is discharged.

Their throat — is noisome and dangerous as an open sepulchre. Observe the progress of evil discourse, proceeding out of the heart, through the throat, tongue, lips, till the whole mouth is filled therewith. The poison of asps — infectious, deadly backbiting, tale-bearing, evil-speaking — is under their lips.

The image may mean that as the grave is ever open to receive all into it — into destruction — so the mouth of the slanderer is ever open to swallow up the peace and happiness of all. Or: as from an open sepulchre there proceeds an offensive and pestilential vapour, so from them proceed corrupt and pestilential words.

14.synthesis: From Psalm 10:7 Paul adds the image of a mouth full of cursing and bitterness — two moral vices that together describe speech weaponized against both God and neighbor. Wesley distinguishes them precisely: "cursing" is directed against God; "bitterness" is directed against one's neighbor. Ellicott traces the etymology of "bitterness": it derives from the idea of venom contained in the gall, making it semantically related to the "poison of asps" just named in verse 13. Calvin notes that Paul modified the LXX slightly, rendering the Hebrew מרמות as "bitterness" — which in context suggests mischievous deceit as well as the emotional disposition of malice. Barnes reminds the reader that Paul is not quoting with strict literalism but giving the sense — the purpose of Psalm 10 was to describe bitter enemies of righteousness, and Paul appropriates the vocabulary to paint the universal portrait of unrestrained human speech.

voices.html: Cursing — against God. Bitterness — against their neighbour. Psalm 10:7. The two together complete the corruption of speech — no relationship is spared: the mouth armed against heaven above and against the neighbor beside.

Bitterness. — Malignity; from the notion that venom was contained in the gall. The word connects this verse with the "poison of asps" of verse 13 — both images belong to the same field of deadly, venomous corruption of speech and spirit.

The apostle has not quoted this literally, but has given the sense. David in the psalm is describing his bitter enemies. The apostle has selected these expressions and applied them to describe the universal character of mankind by nature — appropriating the specific to prove the general.

15.synthesis: Paul now shifts the indictment from speech to action, drawing from Isaiah 59:7-8: their feet are swift to shed blood. The movement from vv. 13-14 (the mouth) to vv. 15-17 (the feet and paths) traces the spread of wickedness from the internal to the external, from words to deeds. Barnes notes the image of eagerness: the wicked do not merely commit violence when forced to it, but hasten toward it, thirsting for the blood of innocence. Clarke extends the scope beyond physical killing: they destroy reputation and life by every means at their disposal. Calvin (commenting on this section from Isaiah) describes ferociousness above measure barbarous, producing waste and solitude by destroying everything wherever it prevails. RWP keeps the Greek focus: ἐκχέαι (to pour out) is an aorist infinitive emphasizing the active, decisive character of the violence — blood shed, not merely threatened.

voices.html: The expression "their feet are swift" denotes the eagerness of the wicked to commit crime, particularly deeds of injustice and cruelty. They thirsted for the blood of innocence and hasted to shed it, to gratify their malice. The language is from Isaiah 59:7-8 — a description of the national character in the time of the prophet, which the apostle applies as a general indictment.

Their feet are swift to shed blood — they make use of every means in their power to destroy the reputation and lives of the innocent. The violence is not passive or reluctant; it is pursued actively, eagerly, as the image of swift feet conveys.

Romans 3:15, 16, and 17 are taken from Isaiah 59:7, 8. It is a representation of ferociousness above measure barbarous, which produces solitude and waste by destroying every thing wherever it prevails — a picture of what sinful humanity does when left to its own impulses.

16.synthesis: The consequence of those swift, blood-hungry feet is destruction and misery wherever they go. This verse, still drawn from Isaiah 59, shifts from the eagerness of the wicked to the desolation they leave behind. Clarke is precise about the two terms: destruction is the wicked person's work — what they produce — while misery is the double consequence that falls both on the victims and eventually on the perpetrators themselves. Barnes broadens the application beyond the immediate historical context: this is a description not only of the wicked of that era but of all times. Wherever the ungodly press their path, the ruin of reputation, happiness, and peace follows in their wake. Calvin's comment on this section from Isaiah frames it as the portrait of a social order from which God's governance has been withdrawn: things fall apart, solitude and waste replace what was human community.

voices.html: Destruction and misery are in their ways — destruction is their work; and misery, to themselves and to the objects of their malice, is the consequence of their impious and murderous conduct. They cannot traffic in violence without becoming themselves its ultimate victims.

They cause the destruction or the ruin of the reputation, happiness, and peace of others. Misery — calamity, ruin. In their ways — wherever they go. This is a striking description not only of the wicked then, but of all times. The tendency of their conduct is to destroy the virtue, happiness, and peace of all with whom they come in contact.

Very striking is the sentence that is added from Isaiah — a representation of ferociousness above measure barbarous, which produces solitude and waste by destroying every thing wherever it prevails: it is the same as the description which gives of the effects produced by the ungodly wherever they dominate.

17.synthesis: The culminating line of the Isaiah section: the way of peace they have not known. Where they should know peace — both in themselves and toward others — there is only ignorance, disruption, and the endless friction of enmity. Wesley's comment is brief but theologically exact: peace can only spring from righteousness, and those who have rejected righteousness have by that very fact cut themselves off from peace. Calvin makes the point in terms of habit: they are so habituated to plunder, violence, and cruelty that they have lost the capacity for courtesy and kindness — the social graces that make peace possible. RWP quotes Denney's classic summary: wherever they go they leave a trail of woe and destruction. Barnes extends the diagnosis: intent on their plans of evil, they do not know or value what promotes the welfare of themselves or others — a condition that is at once willful and self-destructive.

voices.html: Of peace — which can only spring from righteousness. Those who have turned from righteousness find themselves strangers to the peace that righteousness alone can generate; and having no peace themselves, they spread its absence wherever they go.

They are so habituated to plunders, acts of violence and wrong, to savageness and cruelty, that they know not how to act kindly and courteously. The way of peace has become alien to them — a road they cannot walk because they have never been upon it.
The way of peace (ὁδὸν εἰρήνης). Wherever they go they leave a trail of woe and destruction (Denney). Peace — both inward and social — is the one thing their feet do not know how to find or follow.

18.synthesis: The catena closes with a verse from Psalm 36:1: there is no fear of God before their eyes. Having catalogued the vices of speech (vv. 13-14) and action (vv. 15-17), Paul identifies their root: the absence of the fear of God. Without reverence for God, no external restraint can hold — every vice listed above flows from this foundational irreverence. Ellicott makes a precise observation: the fear of God, which is properly an inward disposition, is here "projected" — treated as an external rule of life; its absence means the loss of both the inward check and the external standard. Clarke describes the result as practical atheism: they fear not God's judgments although his eye is upon them. Wesley goes further: no fear of God means no love of God either — the highest positive disposition absent along with the most basic reverential one. Barnes: reverence for God's character and authority is the one thing that restrains human beings from crime; without it, all the preceding vices of the catena are inevitable.

voices.html: The fear of God, which is properly a subjective feeling, is here projected, as it were, and regarded as an external rule of life. Its absence means there is no inward reverence and no external standard — the double vacancy that explains all the particular vices Paul has just catalogued.

This completes the bad character — they are downright atheists, at least practically such. They fear not God's judgments, although his eye is upon them in their evil ways. The Psalm verse summarizes what the preceding catalogues have been showing detail by detail.

The word "fear" here denotes reverence, awe, veneration. There is no such regard or reverence for the character, authority, and honour of God as to restrain them from crime. Their conduct shows that they act as if they had no such fear — as if there were no God who would call them to account.

19.synthesis: Paul draws the conclusion from the catena. The law speaks to those who are under it — primarily to the Jews, since the Scriptures Paul has just quoted are their own. Therefore every Jewish mouth is stopped; they cannot claim these texts describe only pagans. The purpose is that the whole world — Jews included — may be held accountable to God. Calvin identifies the rhetorical strategy: Paul had the harder task with Jews, who covered themselves with the covenant as a cloak of exemption. By showing that Israel's own Scriptures indicted them, he strips away that cloak. Wesley makes the application precise: "every mouth" — full of bitterness (v. 14) and yet also of boasting (v. 27) — becomes guilty, fully convicted, and liable to just condemnation. Ellicott notes the two different Greek verbs for "saith" in this verse: one applies to the content of the utterance, the other to the outward act of speaking — together they emphasize the directness of Scripture's address to its primary audience.

voices.html: Leaving the Gentiles, he distinctly addresses his words to the Jews; for he had a much more difficult work in subduing them, because they, though no less destitute of true righteousness than the Gentiles, yet covered themselves with the cloak of God's covenant, as though it was sufficient holiness to have been separated from the rest of the world by divine election.

In order to bring home this testimony of Scripture more directly to the Jews, and to prevent any subterfuge by which they might attempt to shift the reference from themselves on to the Gentiles, the Apostle calls attention to the fact that the Law speaks especially to those to whom it was given.

Whatsoever the law — the Old Testament — saith, it saith to them that are under the law — that is, to those who own its authority; to the Jews. Every mouth — full of bitterness (verse 14) and yet of boasting (verse 27) — may become guilty, fully convicted, and apparently liable to most just condemnation.

20.synthesis: The climactic conclusion of chapters 1–3: by works of law no flesh will be justified before God. The law cannot save — it can only expose. Ellicott insists that "because" (not "therefore") is the correct reading: the reason no one is justified by works is not merely that all have sinned but that law, by its very nature, can only illuminate the problem, not resolve it. Calvin engages the old debate about whether "works of the law" refers to the whole law or only the ceremonial portions, and settles for the whole moral law — the context of the preceding chapters demands it. Wesley specifies that Paul means chiefly the moral law; the moral law is precisely what is not abolished (v. 31) and yet cannot justify. The second clause — "through the law comes knowledge of sin" — is the positive function: RWP quotes Paul's own parallel in Galatians 3:19-22 and notes that the effect of law is rebellion (1 Cor. 15:56). The law diagnoses; it was never designed to cure.

voices.html: Therefore — rather, because. All mankind alike owe the penalty for their sins. Because not even the Law can protect its votaries — it has no power to justify. All it can do is to expose in its true colours the sinfulness of sin. The proposition is thrown into a general form: not by works of the Jewish Law only, but by "works of law" of any kind.

It is a matter of doubt, even among the learned, what the works of the law mean. Some extend them to the observance of the whole law, while others confine them to the ceremonies alone. But the context of Paul's argument — in which the moral condition of all humanity has been the subject — demands that the whole moral law is in view.

The law was not given to justify, but to show what conduct is required and to reveal how far we come short. By showing what is right, it shows also how far we have departed from it. "By the law is the knowledge of sin" — not the forgiveness, not the cure, but the diagnosis.

"Out of works of law" (ἐξ ἔργων νόμου) — Mosaic law and any law as the source of being set right with God. Paul quotes Psalm 43:2 as he did in Galatians 2:16 to prove his point. The knowledge of sin (ἐπίγνωσις ἁμαρτίας) — the effect of law universally is rebellion (1 Cor. 15:56).

21.synthesis: "But now" — the great pivot of Paul's argument. Having established that neither Jew nor Gentile can stand before God on the basis of law-keeping, Paul announces the gospel: a righteousness of God has been manifested apart from law. The "but now" is both logical and historical — it marks a new epoch, what Ellicott describes as the shift from the period of the Law to the period of the Gospel. This righteousness is not law-independent in the sense of contradicting Scripture: the Law and the Prophets bear witness to it, as Paul will demonstrate through Abraham in chapter 4. Calvin observes that the phrase "righteousness of God" has a double sense — it is both what God accepts as righteous and what he graciously confers on the sinner — and insists both meanings are suitable and neither should be ruled out. Wesley specifies that "apart from law" means without the previous obedience that law demands. Barnes: the closing of all law-paths in vv. 1-20 was preparation for this announcement.

voices.html: But now — in these latter days. The Apostle conceives of the history of the world as divided into periods; the period of the Gospel succeeds that of the Law, and to it the Apostle and his readers belong. The God-kind of righteousness stands permanently manifested (πεφανέρωται, perfect passive), though attested by Law and Prophets throughout the earlier period.

It is not certain for what distinct reason he calls it the righteousness of God — whether because it can alone stand before God, or because the Lord in his mercy confers it on us. As both interpretations are suitable, we contend for neither. This righteousness which God communicates to man and accepts alone has been revealed apart from the law — meaning apart from works; for the teaching of the law is not excluded.

But now the righteousness of God — that is, the manner of becoming righteous which God hath appointed. Without the law — without that previous obedience which the law requires; without reference to the law, or dependence on it. Is manifested in the gospel. Being attested by the Law itself, and by the Prophets — by all the promises in the Old Testament.

22.synthesis: The righteousness of God is further defined: it comes through faith in Jesus Christ and extends to all who believe, without distinction of race. Two clarifications are required. First, the genitive "faith of Jesus Christ" is objective — it means faith directed toward Christ as its object, not Christ's own faithfulness, as RWP notes against Haussleiter's alternative reading. Second, the phrase "to all who believe" erases the ethnic boundary: Wesley distinguishes "to all" (the Jews) and "upon all" (the Gentiles) as complementary in scope, insisting there is no difference as to the need of justification or the manner of it. Ellicott defines faith as Paul uses it: it has an intellectual basis (belief in Christ's existence and attributes) but is moral in its operation — intensive attachment and devotion, not merely mental assent. Clarke adds the missionary scope: this righteousness is unto all mankind in its intention and offer, not restrained to any particular people as the law's privileges were.

voices.html: The words "through faith in Jesus Christ" mean not the faith which is his, but the faith of which he is the object. They ought to be rendered "through faith in Jesus Christ." The genitive case of the object is a Hebraism. Cf. "I live by faith in the Son of God" (Gal. 2:20) — the same construction, requiring the same objective reading.

A further definition of this righteousness: it is a righteousness that has its root in faith and is coextensive with faith, being present in every believer. "Faith" in Paul's writings implies an intense attachment and devotion — it has an intellectual basis necessarily, but it is moral in its operation and total in its claim on the person.

To all — the Jews; and upon all — the Gentiles — that believe: for there is no difference — either as to the need of justification, or the manner of it. The gospel does not merely open salvation to all; it applies the same righteousness to all, on the same terms, without ethnic preference.

23.synthesis: The brief verse that grounds the universality of verse 22: all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God. Two verb tenses carry theological weight here. Ellicott observes that "have sinned" (aorist) looks back to a decisive past act — Paul is thinking of the sin of Adam and the whole race's participation in it — while "fall short" (present) describes the continuing state that results from it. RWP confirms: the aorist is a constative tense, gathering the whole race into a single statement; the present is a continuous fall. Wesley unpacks the content of "the glory of God": it means the supreme end of man — the image of God on earth and the enjoyment of God in heaven — of which humanity has fallen catastrophically short. Clarke notes the egalitarian implication: since all are equally guilty, there is no reason for God to prefer one people over another if he should choose to show mercy at all.

voices.html: All have sinned and come short. — Strictly, all sinned — the Apostle looking back upon an act done in past time under the old legal dispensation. He then goes on to say that the result of that act continues into the present: mankind, as he now sees them, in a body, still fall short of the glory they were created to reflect.

For all have sinned — in Adam, and in their own persons; by a sinful nature, sinful tempers, and sinful actions. And are fallen short of the glory of God — the supreme end of man; short of his image on earth, and the enjoyment of him in heaven.

Sinned (ἥμαρτον). Constative second aorist active indicative — this tense gathers up the whole race into one statement (a timeless aorist). And fall short (ὑστεροῦνται). Present middle indicative — continued action, still falling short. The two tenses together capture both the historical act and the ongoing condition.

24.synthesis: The gospel response to the universal diagnosis of verse 23: all who believe are justified freely, by grace, through the redemption in Christ Jesus. Three grounding terms stack upon each other. "Freely" (δωρεάν) means without cost to the recipient — as a pure gift. "By his grace" names the source — God's unmerited favor. "Through redemption" names the mechanism — the price paid to secure release. Wesley remarks that Paul doubles the assertion ("freely by his grace") not as redundancy but to give the fullest conviction of the truth. Clarke identifies ἀπολύτρωσις as the ransom price, the sum paid for release from captivity. Barnes: this is the essence of the doctrine of justification — being treated as righteous by mere favour, not by right. RWP notes that the participle δικαιούμενοι is present passive, indicating repeated, ongoing action — each believer being set right individually as the gospel is received.

voices.html: And are justified — pardoned and accepted. Freely — without any merit of their own. By his grace — not their own righteousness or works. Through the redemption — the price Christ has paid. Freely by his grace — one of these expressions might have served to convey the apostle's meaning; but he doubles his assertion, in order to give us the fullest conviction of the truth.

Being justified freely by his grace — so far from being able to attain the glory of God by their obedience, they are all guilty; and, to be saved, must be freely pardoned by God's grace; which is shown to them who believe, through the redemption, ἀπολυτρώσεως, the ransom price, which is in the sacrifice of Christ Jesus.

Being treated as if righteous; being regarded and treated as if they had kept the law. The apostle has shown that they could not be so regarded by any merit of their own, or by personal obedience to the law. This is the essence of the doctrine of justification — not earned, not deserved, but given as the act of sovereign grace.

Being justified (δικαιούμενοι). Present passive participle — repeated action in each case, each being set right individually. Freely (δωρεάν). As in Galatians 2:21. By his grace (τῇ αὐτοῦ χάριτι) — instrumental case of the word that so richly expresses Paul's idea of salvation as God's free gift.

25.synthesis: God set forth Christ as a propitiation through faith in his blood — the most explicit atonement statement in Paul's letters. The word ἱλαστήριον carries enormous weight. Ellicott renders it as "that which renders God propitious" and acknowledges that the mechanism of how Christ's death accomplished this is only partially explained in Scripture, but the fact that it did is not in doubt. Clarke identifies the term with the mercy-seat (the lid of the ark of the covenant where blood was sprinkled) — Christ being the antitype toward whom the whole Levitical system pointed. Wesley argues forcefully that the concept of propitiation requires that God was genuinely offended by sin; if he was not, Christ's death was pointless. Calvin opens two possible translations of the verb ("appointed beforehand" vs. "set forth publicly") and finds value in both: either emphasizes the sovereignty of God in designing the atonement, or the publicity of its display before the world. Barnes chooses the public exhibition meaning, seeing in the cross a display intended for all to see.

voices.html: A propitiation. — The Greek word properly means "that which renders propitious." Here, "that which renders God propitious." In some way, which this passage does not fully explain, the death of Christ did act so as to render God propitious toward men. The death on the cross is the one, unrepeatable, publicly exhibited act.

Whom God hath set forth — appointed and published to be a propitiation, ἱλαστήριον, the mercy-seat, or place of atonement; because the blood of the sacrifice was sprinkled on and before it, in order to obtain remission of sin. The mercy-seat was the lid of the ark of the covenant, where God was manifest between the cherubim — Christ is the antitype of all this.

Whom God hath set forth — before angels and men. A propitiation — to appease an offended God. But if, as some teach, God never was offended, there was no need of this propitiation. And, if so, Christ died in vain. To declare his righteousness — to demonstrate not only his clemency, but his vindictive justice whose essential office is to punish sin.

The word properly means, to place in public view — to exhibit in a conspicuous situation, as goods are exhibited for sale, or premiums are displayed at the games. God has publicly exhibited Jesus Christ as the atoning sacrifice — the display was intended to be universal, seen by all.

26.synthesis: Verse 26 names the dual purpose of the atonement: to demonstrate divine righteousness, and to make it possible for God to be both just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus. RWP identifies this as the key phrase that establishes the connection between the righteousness of God as an attribute and the righteousness that comes by faith — both are vindicated simultaneously in the cross. Barnes insists that "just" here means the integrity of God as moral Governor, not merely his benevolence: God showed a due regard for his law and for the penalty of the law; to forgive without atonement would sacrifice justice. Wesley sees the harmony of all divine attributes in the atonement: justice was shown on the Son, mercy was shown to the believer, and the two do not conflict but reinforce each other. Ellicott adds the crucial temporal dimension: sins committed in previous ages were "passed over" in God's forbearance, not wiped away; the cross retroactively vindicates God's patience in not punishing them immediately.

voices.html: "Just and the justifier" (δίκαιον καὶ δικαιοῦντα) — "This is the key phrase which establishes the connexion between the δικαιοσύνη θεοῦ and the δικαιοσύνη ἐκ πίστεως" (Sanday and Headlam). Nowhere has Paul put the problem of God more acutely or profoundly than here.

For a demonstration of his righteousness — both of his justice and mercy. That he might be just — showing his justice on his own Son. And yet the merciful justifier of every one that believeth in Jesus. Justice is preserved inviolate, and inviolate it is preserved if there was a real infliction of punishment on our Saviour. On this plan all the divine attributes harmonize.

This verse contains the substance of the gospel. "Just" here refers to the fact that God retained the integrity of his character as a moral Governor — that he showed due regard to his law, and to the penalty of the law, in his plan of salvation. Should he forgive sinners without an atonement, justice would be sacrificed and abandoned.

27.synthesis: Paul draws the practical consequence with something like triumph: boasting is completely shut out. The perfect passive ἐξεκλείσθη — "it was excluded" — refers to the moment the gospel law of faith came in, as Ellicott notes. On what principle is it excluded? Not on the basis of a law of works — that would leave room for the achiever to boast. But on the basis of a law of faith, which requires all, without distinction, to approach God as guilty and helpless sinners. Wesley captures the logic precisely: faith, as the condition of justification, strips away every basis for human self-congratulation because it requires presenting oneself as having nothing. Calvin adds the pastoral weight: the Holy Spirit must thunder loudly on this subject — a gentle explanation of the doctrine is not sufficient to lay prostrate human loftiness. Barnes notes the social target: the Jews in particular were addicted to boasting of their privileges, and this passage addresses them directly.

voices.html: The Apostle, after having, with reasons abundantly strong, cast down men from their confidence in works, now triumphs over their folly. This exulting conclusion was necessary; for on this subject, to teach would not have been enough — it was necessary that the Holy Spirit should loudly thunder, in order to lay prostrate our loftiness.

It is excluded. — Strictly, It was excluded — at the moment when the law of faith — the gospel — was brought in. By what law? — Properly, By what kind of law? Is this law which gets rid of boasting one that calls for works; or one that calls for faith? The gospel is called "the law of faith" — another name for the principle of grace.

Where is the boasting then of the Jew against the gentile? It is excluded. By what law? of works? Nay — this would have left room for boasting. But by the law of faith — since this requires all, without distinction, to apply as guilty and helpless sinners, to the free mercy of God in Christ.

28.synthesis: Paul states the thesis in the clearest possible terms: a man is justified by faith apart from works of law. Calvin describes this as the main proposition, incontrovertible, drawn from the preceding argument — and notes that adversaries in his own day labored hard to blend faith with the merits of works, allowing that man is partly justified by faith while reserving some role for works. Wesley adds a refinement that goes to the heart of the matter: faith justifies not as a work but as that which receives Christ — it is the instrument, not the ground. This is what makes faith essentially different from works: it is the empty hand, not the contributing hand. Ellicott, with a text-critical note, prefers "for" to "therefore" in this verse, reading v. 28 as the foundational reason why boasting is excluded (v. 27) rather than as its consequence. Barnes: there is no other way — the whole argument has been building to this conclusion.

voices.html: He now draws the main proposition, as one that is incontrovertible, and adds an explanation. Justification by faith is indeed made very clear, while works are expressly excluded. Hence, in nothing do our adversaries labor more in the present day than in attempts to blend faith with the merits of works — allowing that man is justified by faith, but not by faith alone.

We conclude then that a man is justified by faith — and even by this, not as it is a work, but as it receives Christ; and, consequently, has something essentially different from all our works whatsoever. Faith is the instrument, not the ground — the receiving, not the contributing.

That boasting is excluded is much rather the consequence than the cause of the principle that man is justified by faith. This principle the Apostle regards as a fundamental proposition, not as something he is still deducing but as something he confidently and finally asserts.

As the result of the previous train of argument. That a man is justified by faith — there is no other way; the apostle intends not that Christianity makes obedience to the law unnecessary, but that it is not the ground of acceptance with God.

29.synthesis: The theological argument for universal justification by faith: is God the God of Jews only? The question answers itself. Barnes notes that the Jews typically assumed that God's saving purposes applied to their nation uniquely, and that Gentiles could at best be admitted to the margins of God's favor. Paul exposes this assumption as incompatible with monotheism: there is only one God, which means there can only be one mode of justification, and if that mode is available at all, it cannot be restricted to one ethnic group. Ellicott: any man, simply as man — without regard to race or lineage — is capable of justification. Calvin sees the strategic importance of this move for the gospel's global reach: it was crucial to establish that the kingdom of Christ had free passage through the whole world, not just through the corridors of Jewish privilege. RWP adds the wry note: Jews overlooked this then, and some Christians do now.

voices.html: It was a great matter that this point should be urged, in order that a free passage might be made for the kingdom of Christ through the whole world. He does not ask whether God was the Creator of the Gentiles, which was admitted without dispute; but whether he designed to manifest himself as Savior also to them.

The Jews supposed that God was the God of their nation only, that they alone were to be admitted to his favour. Paul showed that as all had alike sinned — Jews and Gentiles — and as the plan of salvation by faith was adapted to sinners without special reference to Jews, so God could show favour to all, and all might be admitted on the same terms.

Of Gentiles also (καὶ ἐθνῶν). Jews overlooked it then and some Christians do now. The parenthesis Paul inserts — "yes, of Gentiles also" — is brief because it needs no argument; it follows necessarily from the oneness of God.

30.synthesis: The logical argument is stated formally: if God is one — and he is — then he must justify both Jew and Gentile by the same means: faith. The rhetorical form ("if, as we are sure is the case") is what RWP calls polite certainty — the conditional framing assumes rather than doubts the premise. Ellicott makes the argument crisp: if there were any distinction between Jew and Gentile, it would require either multiple gods or different laws — both impossible. Clarke notices Paul varies his prepositions (ἐκ πίστεως and διὰ πίστεως — "out of faith" and "through faith") but insists the sense is the same. RWP explores the nuance: ἐκ denotes source (justification springing from faith), διά denotes intermediate agency — but both make faith the operative principle. Calvin suggests the variation may touch on Jewish imaginations of a different standing before God, but finally treats both as synonymous.

voices.html: The argument is strictly logical. If there is to be any distinction between Jew and Gentile, this can only be upon the assumption either that there are more gods than one, or that they will be justified by some different law in some different way. But neither is the case. Therefore it follows that there is no distinction.

It is one and the same God who made both Jews and Gentiles, who shall justify the circumcision — the believing Jews — by faith; and the uncircumcision — the believing Gentiles — by the same faith; as there is but one Savior and one atonement provided for the whole. It is fanciful to suppose any real difference in the two prepositions Paul uses.

By faith (ἐκ πίστεως) — "out of faith," denoting source. Through faith (διὰ τῆς πίστεως) — "by means of the faith" just mentioned. Ἐκ denotes source, διά intermediate agency or attendant circumstance — both make faith the operative ground.

31.synthesis: Paul closes the chapter by anticipating the objection that justification by faith abolishes the law. He denies it emphatically — "by no means" — and asserts the opposite: faith establishes the law. The commentators vary in how they understand "law" here. Wesley takes it comprehensively: faith establishes the law's authority, purity, and end — by pointing to Christ as its fulfillment and by showing how it may be kept in its purity through the Spirit. Clarke distinguishes three senses: the Mosaic ceremonial law (accomplished in Christ's passion), the moral law (not annulled by faith but given new inward power), and the law and prophets (whose testimony the gospel vindicates). Ellicott takes the verse as an isolated anticipatory claim, to be worked out in chapters 6ff. — faith meets the charge of antinomianism not by argument here but by the demonstration of holy living that follows. RWP adds the practical corollary: apart from Christ and the help of the Holy Spirit, no one can keep God's law — so faith, far from abolishing the law, is the only means by which it can ever be truly fulfilled.

voices.html: We establish the law — both the authority, purity, and the end of it; by defending that which the law attests; by pointing out Christ, the end of it; and by showing how it may be fulfilled in its purity. Faith does not make the law void but gives it its only real possibility of being kept.

By law here we may understand three things: (1) the whole of the Mosaic law — accomplished in Christ's passion; (2) the moral law — faith does not abrogate it but empowers its fulfilment; (3) the law and prophets — all of whose testimony the gospel vindicates and confirms. Faith establishes all three.

It seems right to take this as an isolated statement to be worked out afterwards (Romans 6:1 et seq.) more fully. The Apostle deals with two objections to justification by faith: that there ought to be a different rule for Jew and Gentile (v. 29), and that the law is thereby abolished. He meets the second by flat contradiction — it is not abolished, but confirmed.

Paul hinted at this in verse 21 and will show in chapter 4 how Abraham illustrates the very point. Besides, apart from Christ and the help of the Holy Spirit no one can keep God's law. The Mosaic law is only workable by faith in Christ — so faith, far from voiding it, is the prerequisite for its genuine fulfilment.

31.key_tension: The voices agree that faith does not void the law, but differ on which sense of 'law' is primarily in view: Wesley emphasizes the moral law's authority and purity, Clarke distinguishes three senses (Mosaic, moral, prophetic), and Ellicott takes the verse as a forward reference to be demonstrated through the argument of chapters 6 onward.

1.synthesis:
Paul opens chapter 2 by pivoting from the catalog of Gentile wickedness to confront the moral self-assessor — whether Jew or Gentile — who sits in judgment on others while practicing the same things. The word therefore links the sections: the very person who condemns the vices Paul has just described is inexcusable when he commits them himself. Ellicott notes that by judging another you deliver your own verdict, since you declare those acts criminal which you yourself commit. Calvin identifies the target as saintlings — outwardly respectable hypocrites who could not be included in the grosser vices of chapter one, yet whose self-complacency before God is equally groundless.

Wesley sharpens the application: knowledge without practice only increases guilt. The rhetorical shift to second person keeps the accusation from being deflected; no category of moral respectability exempts one from the judgment Paul announces here.

voices.html: This reproof is directed against hypocrites, who dazzle the eyes of men by displays of outward sanctity, and even think themselves to be accepted before God, as though they had given him full satisfaction. Hence Paul, after having stated the grosser vices, now attacks saintlings of this kind, who could not have been included in the first catalogue.

The description just given of the state of one section of the human race contains implicitly the condemnation of the other; for it is equally applicable to both. By the very act of sitting in judgment upon your fellow-man, you pass sentence upon yourself. You declare those acts to be criminal of which you are yourself guilty.

Thou art inexcusable — seeing knowledge without practice only increases guilt. O man — having before spoken of the gentile in the third person, he addresses the Jew in the second person. But he calls him by a common appellation, as not acknowledging him to be a Jew. Whosoever thou art that judgest — for in that thou judgest the other, thou condemnest thyself; for thou doest the same things.

The design of this and the following chapter is to show that the Jews were no less guilty than the Gentiles, and that they needed the benefit of the same salvation. This the apostle does by showing that they had greater light than the Gentiles, and yet that they did the same things. Still they were in the habit of accusing and condemning the Gentiles as wicked and abandoned.

2.synthesis:
Paul appeals to a principle his readers will recognize as settled: God's judgment falls according to truth — in strict accordance with reality, without the partiality or self-exception that human courts allow. Ellicott observes that Paul assumes this as a shared axiom, known to both Jew and Gentile, which makes it the more pointed against those who presumed that divine judgment applied to others alone. Calvin notes Paul's strategy: he summons the hypocrite to the tribunal of God, to whom even darkness is not hidden — no human jury's verdict of innocent can stand before that bar.

Wesley adds that this judgment is according to truth in that it reaches the heart as well as the life, not merely the outward conduct. Barnes emphasizes that the Jews knew this principle thoroughly from their own scriptures, and it was the acknowledged doctrine of the entire nation.

voices.html: The design of Paul is to shake off from hypocrites their self-complacencies, that they may not think that they can really gain any thing, though they be applauded by the world, and though they regard themselves guiltless; for a far different trial awaits them in heaven. He summons them to the tribunal of God, to whom darkness itself is not hid.

St. Paul assumes that this will be acknowledged as a general principle by his readers, whether Jew or Gentile. There is still a strong under-current of allusion to the way in which the Jew was apt to fall back upon his privileges. The Jews, it seems, had an idea that the Gentiles only would be judged, while they would be able to claim admission into the Messianic kingdom as theirs by right of birth.
For we know — without thy teaching — that the judgment of God is according to truth: is just, making no exception, and reaches the heart as well as the life.

It is the common and admitted sentiment of mankind that God will punish such crimes. It is known and believed by men generally. It is implied in this declaration that this was known to the Jews; they knew it because it was everywhere taught in the Old Testament, and it was the acknowledged doctrine of the nation.

3.synthesis:
Paul presses the logical collapse of the self-exempting judge: if God judges according to truth those who do such things, and you do the same things, how can you think you will escape his judgment? The argument is direct — not a complex syllogism but an appeal to moral consistency. Ellicott underscores the emphatic thrust: Are you — because you are a Jew — to be the only exception to this rule? The force of the question is that such an exception would be self-evidently absurd.

Calvin notes the unusual rhetoric: Paul does not argue before men but appeals to the tribunal of conscience, treating the charge as proven because no one can honestly deny their own iniquity when they submit to God's scrutiny. Barnes frames it as an appeal to deep instinctive convictions of justice — if imperfect human judges condemn such things, a holy God most certainly will.

voices.html: Paul may seem to some to have acted unwisely for having passed so severe a censure when he had not yet proved the accusation. But the fact is otherwise; for he adduced not his accusation before men, but appealed to the judgment of conscience; and thus he deemed that proved which he had in view — that they could not deny their iniquity, if they examined themselves and submitted to the scrutiny of God's tribunal.
That thou shalt escape — emphatic. Are you — because you are a Jew — to be the only exception to this rule?

This is an appeal to their common sense, to their deep and instinctive conviction of what was right. If they condemned those who practised these things; if, imperfect and obscure as their sense of justice was; if, unholy as they were, they yet condemned those guilty of these offences — would not a holy and just God be far more likely to pronounce judgment?

4.synthesis:
The apostle anticipates a subtle temptation: that present prosperity and God's patience might be read as tokens of divine approval. Calvin identifies this as the hypocrite's characteristic error — being transported with prosperity as if earthly blessing proved God's favor. Clarke names three distinct gifts being abused: God's goodness (undeserved benefit), his forbearance (toleration of past sins), and his longsuffering (continued patience under repeated provocation). All three exist for one purpose — to lead to repentance — but the self-righteous inverts their meaning, reading them as indulgence rather than as gracious summons.

Wesley captures the escalation: the sinner moves from hoping to escape God's wrath to actively abusing God's love. Barnes is precise: the abuse lies not in explicit contempt but in the practical inference that God's continued blessing means he regards you as innocent and safe — a perversion the apostle now exposes.

voices.html: As hypocrites are commonly transported with prosperity, as though they had merited the Lord's kindness by their good deeds, and become thus more hardened in their contempt of God, the Apostle anticipates their arrogance, and proves by an argument taken from a reason of an opposite kind, that there is no ground for them to think that God, on account of their outward prosperity, is propitious to them.

Or despisest thou the riches of his goodness — wilt thou render of none effect that marked benevolence of God towards thee which has given so many superior advantages, and that forbearance which has tolerated thy many miscarriages, and that long-suffering which, after repeated provocations, still continues to bear with thee? Not acknowledging that this goodness of God leadeth thee to repentance — was designed to accomplish this blessed end.

Or despisest thou — dost thou go farther still, from hoping to escape his wrath, to the abuse of his love? The riches — the abundance — of his goodness, forbearance, and longsuffering — seeing thou both hast sinned, dost sin, and wilt sin. All these are afterwards comprised in the single word goodness. Leadeth thee — that is, is designed of God to lead or encourage thee to repentance.

This word properly means to contemn or to treat with neglect. It does not mean here that they professedly treated God's goodness with contempt; but they perverted and abused it. They did not regard it as fitted to lead them to repentance; but they derived a practical impression that because God had not cut them off, he did not regard them as sinners, or that they were innocent and safe.

5.synthesis:
The hardened heart does not remain neutral — it actively accumulates divine wrath. Wesley's image is arresting: the one who thinks himself treasuring up all good things is in fact laying up wrath against the day of judgment, each unrepented sin adding to the store. Calvin sees impenitence as the inevitable consequence of hardening oneself against God's admonitions — a trajectory ending in open provocation of the Lord. Clarke connects the Greek term (ametanoēton) to the idea of a heart that has become incapable of repenting rather than merely unwilling — the hardness has become structural.

Ellicott captures the severity in the contrast between accumulation and discharge: guilt is laid up gradually, little by little, but the punishment will come in one overwhelming tide. Barnes grounds the diagnosis anthropologically: hardness is a state of mind where no motive makes any impression, where every appeal God sends simply bounces off an insensible surface.

voices.html: When we become hardened against the admonitions of the Lord, impenitence follows; and they who are not anxious about repentance openly provoke the Lord. It is an impenitable rather than an impenitent heart — that is, a heart incapable of repenting.

The one condition upon which the goodness of God will come into operation, you directly contravene. Instead of being penitent, you are impenitent, and therefore the load of wrath which you have been accumulating against yourself remains unremoved. It is only waiting for the day of judgment to discharge itself upon you. The guilt of man is accumulated little by little; the punishment will be discharged upon him all at once, in one overwhelming tide.

Treasurest up wrath — although thou thinkest thou art treasuring up all good things. O what a treasure may a man lay up either way, in this short day of life! To thyself — not to him whom thou judgest. In the day of wrath, and revelation, and righteous judgment of God — just opposite to the goodness and forbearance and longsuffering of God.

The word hardness is used to denote insensibility of mind. It properly means that which is insensible to the touch, or on which no impression is made by contact. Hence it is applied to the mind to denote a state where no motives make an impression; which is insensible to all the appeals made to it — a state of mind where the goodness and forbearance of God makes no impression.

6.synthesis:
The declaration that God will render to each one according to his works is one of the most carefully placed sentences in Romans, arriving before Paul establishes universal guilt. Ellicott states its doctrinal force plainly: the consistent teaching of Scripture is that works — the moral tenor of a person's life — will be the standard by which each is judged at the last day. He then acknowledges the apparent tension with justification by faith and invites the reader to hold both as consistent. Calvin reads the verse differently in emphasis: Paul is pointing to genuine righteousness of works as God actually defines it, stripping away every false confidence in religious performance or outward piety that substitutes hollow observance for actual obedience.

Barnes establishes the governing principle as pure justice: God as righteous judge renders to each as each deserves — a standard that levels Jew and Gentile before the same bar.

voices.html: As he had to do with blind saintlings, who thought that the wickedness of their hearts was well covered, provided it was spread over with some disguises of empty works, he pointed out the true character of the righteousness of works — even that which is of account before God — lest they should feel confident that it was enough to pacify him, if they brought words and trifles, or leaves only.

The Apostle here lays down with unmistakable definiteness and precision the doctrine that works — what a man has done, the moral tenor of his life — will be the standard by which he will be judged at the last day. There can be no question that this is the consistent doctrine of Scripture. How is this to be reconciled with the main theme of the Epistle, justification by faith?

Who will render — that is, who will make retribution as a righteous Judge; who will give to every man as he deserves. This is a general principle, and it is clear that in this respect God would deal with the Jew as he does with the Gentile. This general principle the apostle is establishing, that he may bring it to bear on the Jew, and to show that he cannot escape simply because he is a Jew.

6.key_tension: Ellicott and Barnes read this as straightforwardly establishing the justice of divine judgment by works; Calvin reads it as stripping false confidence by pointing to what genuine righteousness before God actually requires, as opposed to the hollow performances the self-righteous offer.

7.synthesis:
Those who receive eternal life are described in terms of their sustained orientation: patient continuance in well-doing, seeking glory, honor, and immortality. Calvin notes that perseverance requires not only sustained effort but patience against every obstacle Satan places in the way; and he stresses that what the faithful seek is God's favor, not any merit of their own — the seeking is the evidence, not the ground, of their standing. Ellicott reads the syntactical awkwardness as Paul's earnestness overriding his rhetoric: those who honestly seek this life shall find it, with the stress falling on patient as the defining quality of the seeking.

Barnes draws from the consistent witness of Scripture that none will be saved except those who persevere in holiness — the one who perseveres gives evidence of genuine piety; the one who does not has never truly begun. Wesley briefly and pointedly notes that pure love does not exclude faith, hope, and desire.

voices.html: Patience also is required in the saints, by which they may continue firm, though oppressed with various trials. For Satan suffers them not by a free course to come to the Lord; but he strives by numberless hinderances to impede them, and to turn them aside from the right way. And when he says that the faithful aspire after the divine glory by continuing in good works, he does not mean that they aspire after any thing else but the favor of God.

The stress is upon the words by patient continuance in well doing. From the point of view of rhetoric, no doubt exception might be taken to the tautology; but St. Paul was far too much in earnest to attend carefully to the laws of rhetoric, and it is just this spontaneity which is in great part the secret of his power. Those who honestly seek for this life shall find it.

It means that they who so continue or persevere in good works as to evince that they are disposed to obey the law of God. It does not mean those who perform one single act, but those who so live as to show that this is their character — to obey God. It is the uniform doctrine of the Bible that none will be saved but those who persevere in a life of holiness.

8.synthesis:
Against those who persevere in good-doing, Paul sets the contentious — those who are factious and self-seeking in their relation to God, who disobey the truth and obey unrighteousness instead. Ellicott recovers the word from mistranslation: the underlying Greek denotes one acting in the spirit of a hireling, with factiousness and self-interest, not primarily a quarrelsome disposition toward other people but an orientation of rebellion toward God. Calvin notes the grammatical irregularity — the second half of the contrast is expressed differently from the first — but the theology is clear: these are the disobedient, and upon them fall wrath, fury, tribulation, and anguish.

Wesley draws a sharp connection to Psalm 78:49, the plagues of Egypt, and suggests that the Jews' greater privileges will place them in the foremost rank of those so punished. Barnes names the disposition plainly as rebellion: contending with the Almighty, resisting his claims, refusing submission.

voices.html: That are contentious — an error in the Authorised version through a wrong derivation of the word. Strictly, to those who act in the spirit of a hireling; hence, according to the secondary meaning of the word, to those who act in a spirit of factiousness and self-seeking. It is, in fact, a disposition toward God — rebellious, self-seeking, refusing his obligations.

But to them that are contentious — like thee, O Jew, who thus fightest against God. The character of a false Jew is disobedience, stubbornness, impatience. Indignation and wrath, tribulation and anguish — alluding to Psalm 78:49, intimating that the Jews would in the day of vengeance be more severely punished than even the Egyptians were when God made their plagues so wonderful.

This expression here evidently denotes a disposition towards God, and is of the same signification as rebellious, or as opposing God. They who contend with the Almighty; who resist his claims, who rebel against his laws, and refuse to submit to his requirements, however made known. One striking characteristic of the sinner is, that he contends with God.

9.synthesis:
Tribulation and anguish fall on every human being who does evil — the Jew first and also the Greek. Calvin notes that the Jew takes precedence precisely because they had, in preference to others, both the promises and the threatenings of the law — privilege intensifies accountability. Ellicott observes that the phrase upon every soul of man is more pointed than upon every man: the punishment will be felt in the soul, the seat of moral life, not merely in external circumstance.

Clarke underlines the proportionality: the Jew, possessing greater privileges and having abused greater mercies, stands first; the Gentile, having what God saw as sufficient for his state, faces punishment proportioned to his demerit. Wesley stresses that the Jew's having true religion and receiving Christ's mission first will place him in the foremost rank of those who obey not the truth.

voices.html: He simply places the Jew in opposition to the Gentile. But the Jews take the precedence in this case, for they had, in preference to others, both the promises and the threatenings of the law; as though he had said, This is the universal rule of the divine judgment; it shall begin with the Jews, and it shall include the whole world.
Upon every soul of man — the phrase is not quite the same as upon every man, but more special in character, indicating the part in which the punishment will be felt.

Misery of all descriptions, without the possibility of escape, will this righteous Judge inflict upon every impenitent sinner. The Jew first, as possessing greater privileges, and having abused greater mercies; and also on the Gentile, who, though he had not the same advantages, had what God saw was sufficient for his state; and, having sinned against them, shall have punishment proportioned to his demerit.

Of the Jew first — here we have the first express mention of the Jews in this chapter. And it is introduced with great propriety. Their having been trained up in the true religion, and having had Christ and his apostles first sent to them, will place them in the foremost rank of the criminals that obey not the truth.

10.synthesis:
The symmetry Paul constructs is deliberate: just as tribulation and anguish fall on the Jew first and also the Greek, so glory, honor, and peace flow to every doer of good — the Jew first and also the Greek. The same ordering applies to blessing as to judgment, establishing God's absolute impartiality in both directions. Wesley maps the terms carefully against their opposites: glory against wrath, honor against indignation, and peace — present and eternal — against tribulation and anguish. The parallelism is not merely rhetorical; it establishes that the very logic of divine judgment is symmetrical.

Clarke reads this as a statement of eternal blessedness available to every conscientious person, Jew or Gentile, who lives in obedience to the known will of God. The verse prepares for the great axial principle of v.11 — there is no partiality with God — by demonstrating it in both directions.

voices.html: While the finally impenitent Jew and Gentile shall experience the fullest effects of the righteous indignation of the supreme Judge, every man that worketh good — that lives in a conscientious obedience to the known will of God, whether he be Jew or Gentile — shall have glory, honor, and peace; that is, eternal blessedness.
But glory — just opposite to wrath, from the divine approbation. Honour — opposite to indignation, by the divine appointment; and peace now and for ever, opposed to tribulation and anguish.

11.synthesis:
There is no partiality with God is the axial principle of the chapter's argument. Calvin connects it to his broader movement: the same God who arraigned all mortals as guilty now brings the charge home equally to Jew and Gentile; the privilege of possessing the law is no defense. Ellicott reaches for the historical significance: the great result of the Christian revelation was to break down belief in race-religions — to demolish the middle wall of partition that separated the covenant people from the world.

The principle does not deny that God distributes advantages unequally. Wesley notes it is well consistent with his distributing advantages and opportunities according to his own good pleasure — but those advantages do not alter the standard of judgment. Barnes defines partiality precisely: favoring one party not because his cause is more just, but on account of wealth, rank, or personal relationship. God has no such bias, and the judgment falls accordingly.

voices.html: He has hitherto generally arraigned all mortals as guilty; but now he begins to bring home his accusation to the Jews and to the Gentiles separately. At the same time he teaches us that it is no objection that there is a difference between them, but that they are both without any distinction exposed to eternal death. The Gentiles pretended ignorance as their defense; the Jews gloried in the honor of having the law.

Regard for the external circumstances of a man as opposed to his internal condition — here, especially, regard for the circumstances of birth and race. The great result of the Christian revelation was to break down the belief in race-religions — the middle wall of partition, as St. Paul calls it. The essential equality of Jew and Gentile before God is not affected by their difference of privilege.

For there is no respect of persons with God — he will reward every one according to his works. But this is well consistent with his distributing advantages and opportunities of improvement, according to his own good pleasure.

The word thus rendered means partiality, in pronouncing judgment, in favouring one party or individual more than another, not because his cause is more just, but on account of something personal — on account of his wealth, or rank, or office, or influence. It has special reference to a judge who pronounces judgment contrary to justice from corrupt motives. God has no such bias.

12.synthesis:
Paul now works out the logic of impartial judgment across the two human situations. Gentiles who sinned without the law — that is, without the written revelation given at Sinai — will perish without that law serving as their specific measure; Jews who sinned under the law will be judged by the law they possessed. Ellicott draws out the essential fairness: each is judged by the method proper to his case. Calvin is pointed: the Gentile's ignorance of a written law does not constitute a defense, since condemnation follows from the sin itself, not from the code against which it is measured.

Clarke specifies the dispensations at issue: the Gentile lived under the inferior dispensation of natural conscience; the Jew under positive divine revelation. The result is that neither can claim exemption on the grounds of their respective positions. Wesley observes that Paul speaks as of time past because all time will be past at the day of judgment.

voices.html: In the former part of this section he assails the Gentiles; though no Moses was given them to publish and to ratify a law from the Lord, he yet denies this omission to be a reason why they deserved not the just sentence of death for their sins; as though he had said — that the knowledge of a written law was not necessary for the just condemnation of a sinner.

Jew and Gentile alike will be judged, each by the method proper to his case; the Jew by the written Law against which he has sinned, the Gentile by the unwritten law of conscience against which he too has sinned. The Gentile, who, at the dictates of conscience, acts as if he were subject to law, shall have the full benefit that law can give him. His conscience is to him a law.

They, the Gentiles, who shall be found to have transgressed against the mere light of nature shall not come under the same rule with those, the Jews, who have in addition enjoyed an extraordinary revelation; but they shall be dealt with according to the inferior dispensation under which they lived: while those, the Jews, who have sinned against the law — the positive Divine revelation — shall be judged by that law.

For as many as have sinned — he speaks as of the time past, for all time will be past at the day of judgment. Without the law — without having any written law — shall also perish without the law — without regard had to any outward law; being condemned by the law written in their hearts. The word also shows the agreement of the manner of sinning with the manner of suffering.

13.synthesis:
A central axiom dropped into the argument: the hearers of the law are not righteous before God; the doers will be justified. Calvin reads this as anticipating the Jewish objection that possession and knowledge of the law sufficed; Paul declares the claim groundless — obedience, not possession, is what God requires, since only those who do what the law says will live by it. Ellicott notes that mere existence under the law does not exempt from judgment; the only exemption belongs to those who have actually kept it, which Paul is about to show no one has.

Wesley is insistent that there is no parenthesis beginning here, and that the principle extends to Gentiles as well as Jews. Barnes traces the same axiom through James 1:22 and Matthew 7:21 — those who hear but do not do merely deceive themselves; actual obedience is the only evidence of righteousness before God.

voices.html: This anticipates an objection which the Jews might have adduced. As they had heard that the law was the rule of righteousness, they gloried in the mere knowledge of it: to obviate this mistake, he declares that the hearing of the law or any knowledge of it is of no such consequence that any one should on that account lay claim to righteousness, but that works must be produced.

They must not suppose that the mere fact of their being under the Law will exempt them from this judgment. The only exemption will be that which is given to those who have kept the Law, and not merely had the privilege of hearing it.

For not the hearers of the law are, even now, just before God, but the doers of the law shall be justified — finally acquitted and rewarded: a most sure and important truth, which respects the Gentiles also, though principally the Jews. There is therefore no parenthesis here; for the sixteenth verse also depends on the fifteenth, not on the twelfth.

The same sentiment is implied in James 1:22 and Matthew 7:21. The apostle here doubtless designed to meet an objection of the Jews; to wit, that they had the law, that they manifested great deference for it, that they heard it read with attention, and professed a willingness to yield themselves to it. To meet this, he states a very plain and obvious principle — that this was insufficient to justify them before God unless they rendered actual obedience.

14.synthesis:
The Gentile who by nature does the things the law requires becomes a law to himself — a pivotal claim about natural moral knowledge. Calvin argues that Paul's point is not that all Gentiles fulfill the law but that there is no nation so wholly lost that it does not keep within the limits of some laws; moral conscience is a universal human endowment, however corrupted. Ellicott reads by nature as meaning spontaneously, without the coercion of any external rule — the point is internal prompting rather than external compulsion.

Wesley makes a pointed qualification: even this natural law-keeping is strictly speaking by preventing grace, refusing to ground it in unassisted human capacity. Barnes treats the verse as a hypothetical case Paul introduces to show that the heathen have sufficient moral knowledge to be accountable — not as a factual description of Gentile achievement in obedience.

voices.html: He indeed shows that ignorance is in vain pretended as an excuse by the Gentiles, since they prove by their own deeds that they have some rule of righteousness: for there is no nation so lost to every thing human, that it does not keep within the limits of some laws. Since then the Gentiles have the righteousness of the law, though unwritten, engraven on their minds, the plea of ignorance is taken from them.

By nature — spontaneously; of their own motion; not acting under the coercion of any external rule, but simply by the promptings of their own conscience left to itself. The things of the law — in this one instance the article is used, meaning, however, not the Mosaic law specifically, but what law in its idea implies and requires.

For when the Gentiles — that is, any of them — do by nature — that is, without an outward rule; though this also, strictly speaking, is by preventing grace — the things contained in the law — the ten commandments being only the substance of the law of nature — these, not having the written law, are a law to themselves.

This verse is not to be understood as affirming, as a historical fact, that the Gentiles do keep the law of God. The apostle does not expressly affirm that this was ever done; but he supposes the case, to show the true nature and value of the rites of the Jews, and to show that the heathen have sufficient knowledge of his will to take away every excuse for sin.

14.key_tension: Calvin and Ellicott read vv.14-15 as describing an actual moral phenomenon — the Gentile conscience showing genuine moral knowledge; Barnes treats the case as hypothetical and illustrative; Wesley grounds even natural law-keeping in prevenient grace rather than in unaided human nature.

15.synthesis:
Where the law is not written on tablets of stone, it is written on hearts — and the conscience provides its own two-sided testimony. Ellicott captures the structure: there is a double witness — actions speak externally and conscience speaks internally, the two together providing equivalent evidence to what the written Law provides for the Jew. Wesley reaches for courtroom imagery: conscience functions as plaintiff, defendant, and witness alternately, with thoughts on both sides of the moral ledger accusing or excusing in turn.

Clarke connects this to Paul's earlier point: as the obedient Gentile demonstrates that the great object of the law — to bring people from injustice, cruelty, and falsehood — is accomplished even without the written code, their conscience corroborates their conduct. Barnes stresses that the Gentile's standard, though different in form, will be the same in substance as the written Law on the day of judgment — the will of God is the same whether made known by reason or by revelation.

voices.html: The work of the law — the practical effect or realisation of the law — written in their hearts as the original Law was written upon the tables of stone. Also bearing witness — there is a double witness; their actions speak for them externally, and conscience speaks for them internally. Between themselves — that is, within themselves — their thoughts alternately accusing and excusing.

Which show the work of the law — in acting according to justice, mercy, temperance, and truth, they show that the great object of the law, which was to bring men from injustice, cruelty, intemperance, and falsity, is accomplished so far in them: their conscience also bearing witness — that faculty of the soul where that Divine light dwells and works — shows them that they are right.

Who show — to themselves, to other men, and, in a sense, to God himself — the work of the law written on their hearts.
