Their conscience — there is none of all its faculties which the soul has less in its power than this — bearing witness, and their thoughts, the mean while, accusing or else excusing one another. Among themselves — alternately, like plaintiff and defendant.

The work of the law — the design, purpose, or object which is contemplated by the revealed law; to make known to man his duty, and to enforce the obligation to perform it. The will of God, whether made known by reason or revelation, will be the same in substance. In other words, the standard of judgment for the Gentile, though different in form, coincides with the revealed will of God.

16.synthesis:
Paul closes the bracket opened at v.12: all of this will be disclosed fully on that day when God judges the secrets of men by Jesus Christ. Ellicott insists that this verse cannot refer to the daily process of conscience described in vv.14-15 but to the final judgment specifically — where the Son functions as Mediator of judgment as he is Mediator of salvation. The secrets that shape the real moral quality of actions will then be made manifest, not just the external acts.

Wesley underlines that everything will then be shown to be what it really is; and he adds that Paul's phrase according to my gospel means the gospel too carries the force of law. Clarke reads this as Paul's claim that his apostolic proclamation is itself the statement of the principle of impartial, circumstance-sensitive judgment. Barnes connects the verse back to v.12, treating vv.13-15 as parenthetical, with heathen and Jew arraigned at the same bar, each judged by their own standard.

voices.html: This verse takes up the main thread of the subject. It cannot refer to what immediately precedes, because there the Apostle is referring to the daily process that goes on whenever doubtful actions are submitted to the law of conscience; here he is speaking expressly of the final judgment held by God and not by man. As the Son of God is the Mediator of salvation, so also is He the Mediator of judgment.

And all this shall be farther exemplified and proved in the day when God shall judge the secrets of men by Jesus Christ; which judgment shall be according to my Gospel — according to what I am now laying down before you, relative to the impartiality of God, and his righteous procedure in judging men not according to their opinions or prejudices, but according to the various advantages or disadvantages of their actual situation.

In the day — that is, who show this in the day. Everything will then be shown to be what it really is. In that day will appear the law written in their hearts as it often does in the present life. On secret circumstances depends the real quality of actions, frequently unknown to the actors themselves. According to my gospel — hence it appears that the gospel also is a law.

17.synthesis:
Having established the general principles of impartial judgment, Paul turns with full directness to the Jew — but with rhetorical skill, he first concedes all their claimed privileges before turning those privileges into charges. The catalog is extensive: the name Jew, resting on the law, glorying in God, knowing the divine will, discerning what is excellent. Wesley structures the passage as two sets of five articles, the first about what the Jew boasts in himself, the second about what he claims with respect to others.

Ellicott notes a significant textual matter: the KJV behold should read but if, making this verse the beginning of a conditional whose answering clause falls at v.21. Clarke observes that Paul's entire purpose is to demonstrate that superior knowledge, privileges, and profession serve only to aggravate condemnation when matched against actual conduct — and Barnes notes the pastoral skill of granting all their claims before pressing the charges.

voices.html: The Jews take the precedence in this case, for they had, in preference to others, both the promises and the threatenings of the law. Now Paul concedes to them the knowledge of the divine will, and the approval of things useful; and this they had attained from the doctrine of the law. But there is a twofold approval — one of choice, when we embrace the good we approve; the other of judgment, by which indeed we distinguish good from evil, but by no means strive or desire to follow it.

An interesting case of a corrupt reading which has found its way into the Authorised version. For behold, a decisive consensus of the best MSS. has but if. Adopting but if, the answering clause of the sentence is to be found in the question, Teachest thou not thyself? at Romans 2:21. Turning to the Jew, the Apostle breaks out into indignant and vehement apostrophe.

The apostle now throws off the cover, and openly argues with him in the most plain and nervous manner; asserting that his superior knowledge, privileges, and profession, served only to aggravate his condemnation. And that, in fact, he who, under all his greater advantages, transgressed the law of God, stood condemned by the honest Gentile who, to the best of his knowledge, obeyed it.

By the use of the word behold he calls their attention to it as to an important subject; and with great skill and address, he states their privileges, before he shows them how those privileges might enhance their condemnation. He admits all their claims to pre-eminence in privileges, and then with great faithfulness proceeds to show them how those privileges aggravated their guilt.

18.synthesis:
The Jew knows God's will from the law and can discern what is most excellent — two gifts of privilege that mark him off from the Gentile. Calvin identifies a crucial distinction between two levels of discernment: the approval of judgment, by which one distinguishes good from evil, and the approval of choice, by which one actually embraces and pursues the good. The Jew possesses the first abundantly through the law, but the apostle's charge will be that the second is missing in practice.

Ellicott draws out an ambiguity in the Greek verb rendered approvest: it can mean either to approve (embrace the good) or to discriminate (distinguish good from evil), and probably indicates here the intellectual capacity to make moral distinctions, formed by constant synagogue reading of the law. Clarke stresses the privilege: God's revelation makes the finest moral distinctions possible, showing not merely what is obligatory but what is most excellent in God's sight. Barnes notes this knowledge distinctly marked the Jews from all other nations.

voices.html: He now concedes to them the knowledge of the divine will, and the approval of things useful; and this they had attained from the doctrine of the law. But there is a twofold approval — one of choice, when we embrace the good we approve; the other of judgment, by which indeed we distinguish good from evil, but by no means strive or desire to follow it. Thus the Jews were so learned in the law that they could pass judgment on the conduct of others, but were not careful to regulate their life according to that judgment.

His will — literally, the will — the supreme will. Approvest the things that are more excellent — probably rightly given in the Authorised version, though the marginal rendering is also possible: triest the things that differ, that is, art able to discriminate between good and evil. Being instructed — with reference to the constant reading of the Law in the synagogue.

Knowest his will — hast been favored with a revelation of his own will, immediately from himself. The things that are more excellent — that revelation which God has given of himself makes the nicest distinctions between right and wrong; between vice and virtue; showing how you should walk so as to please God, and, consequently, acquire the most excellent portion that human spirits can have on this side heaven.

And knowest his will — the will or commands of God. This knowledge they obtained from the Scriptures; and of course in this they were distinguished from other nations. The word rendered approvest can mean either to approve or to distinguish. This is probably its meaning here, referring rather to the intellectual process of discerning good from evil, a process formed by the instruction of the law.

19.synthesis:
The Jew's self-confidence reaches its height: he is persuaded he can serve as moral guide to the blind, a light in darkness, a corrector of the foolish, a teacher of children. Calvin notes that this confidence was partly grounded in reality — the Jews did have more light than surrounding nations — but Paul grants it precisely in order to intensify the accusation when practice fails to match the claim. The concession is rhetorical and pointed.

Ellicott cross-references Matthew 15:14, where Jesus describes the Pharisees as blind leaders of the blind — the confident guide turns out to share the blindness he purports to cure. Clarke reads the verse as capturing Jewish self-understanding accurately: they believed the Gentiles were babes and fools by comparison, and that all nations must look to them for the only true knowledge. Barnes notes that Jewish confidence in their religion was not mere hypocrisy but genuine, full conviction — they were not speculative infidels.

voices.html: More is still granted to them; as though they had not only what was sufficient for themselves, but also that by which they could enrich others. He grants, indeed, that they had such abundance of learning, as that others might have been supplied. He grants all this in order to press the accusation the more sharply when he shows their conduct to be wholly inconsistent with it.
A guide of the blind — comp. Matthew 15:14, They be blind leaders of the blind. And if the blind lead the blind — the confident guide turns out to share the very blindness he purports to cure.

And art confident — in consequence of all these religious advantages, ye believe that ye are able to teach others, and to be guides and lights to the bewildered, darkened Gentiles, who may become proselytes to your religion. They believed the Gentiles to be in utter ignorance and moral darkness, from which they alone could deliver them.

This expression denotes the full assurance of the Jew that he was superior in knowledge to all other people. It is a remarkable fact, that the Jews put the fullest confidence in their religion. Though proud, wicked, and hypocritical, yet they were not speculative infidels. It was one of their characteristics through all their history that they had the fullest assurance that God was the Author of their institutions.

20.synthesis:
The catalog of Jewish privilege climaxes with the claim to possess the form of knowledge and truth — the law as the complete embodiment of all that is genuinely worth knowing. Calvin reads form (μόρφωσις) not as empty form opposed to substance but as a sketch, a delineation, a summary — the Jews genuinely claimed to carry in their breasts all the secrets of the law. Ellicott agrees: the word implies substance, not mere appearance — presentation or embodiment of knowledge and truth.

Clarke notes that the Jews considered themselves the sole possessors of the only true knowledge, and that all nations were dependent on them for the system of eternal truth derived from the law. Barnes observes that Paul here is giving the Jews' own self-description, one that was not without foundation — their failure was not in claiming the privilege but in never matching the claim with life.

voices.html: It may be thus explained: because thou hast the form of knowledge, they professed to be the teachers of others, because they seemed to carry in their breasts all the secrets of the law. The word form is put for model, for pattern; the same word occurs in 2 Timothy 3:5 in the form of godliness. It is taken here in a good sense, as meaning a sketch, a delineation, an outline, a representation, or a summary.

The form of knowledge and of the truth — as we might say, the presentation of knowledge and of truth. Here not form as opposed to substance, but as implying substance — presentation, or embodiment of knowledge and truth.

An instructer of the foolish — ye believe the Gentiles to be babes and fools when compared with yourselves; that ye alone possess the only true knowledge; that ye are the only favourites of Heaven; and that all nations must look up to you as possessing the only form of knowledge — the grand scheme and draught of all true science, of every thing that is worthy to be learned: the system of eternal truth, derived from the law.

The word foolish here signifies those who are void of understanding. The expression is figurative, and denotes those who were as ignorant as children — a term they were likely to apply to all the Gentiles. It is evident that the character here given by Paul to the Jews is one which they claimed for themselves, and which was not without foundation. Their failure was that the claim was never matched by life.

21.synthesis:
The rhetorical turn is sharp and devastating: you who teach others, do you teach yourself? The one who preaches against theft has convicted himself when he steals; the moral instructor becomes the moral defendant. Wesley compresses the principle into an epigram: he does not teach himself who does not practise what he teaches. Calvin notes that Paul reverses the usual order here — he addresses sins against neighbor (theft), self (adultery), and God (sacrilege) — the inverse of his treatment of Gentile sin in chapter one.

Ellicott notes that therefore at v.21 is resumptive, referring back to the conditional but if of v.17, the long parenthesis of vv.18-20 now complete and the conditional's consequence now being drawn. Barnes observes that the question form is chosen because it conveys truth with greater rhetorical force — the implied answer is inescapable and well established by the evidence Paul goes on to adduce.

voices.html: It is worthy of notice, that the Apostle, after the Hebrew manner, reverses the order as to the points he mentions; he, as it were, retrogrades, and begins to do so at Romans 2:21. He addresses sins against neighbour, then against self, then against God — the inverse of his treatment of Gentile sin in chapter one, where sins against God come first.

Therefore — see above on Romans 2:17. The word is resumptive, or, as it is technically called, epanaleptic — taking up the thread of the conditional clause, but if thou art called a Jew, after the parenthetical description of Jewish privilege in vv.18-20, and now drawing the consequence.

Thou dost not teach thyself — he does not teach himself who does not practise what he teaches. Dost thou steal, commit adultery, commit sacrilege — sin grievously against thy neighbour, thyself, God. St. Paul had shown the Gentiles first their sins against God, then against themselves, then against their neighbours. He now inverts the order: for sins against God are the most glaring in a heathen, but not in a Jew.

He who is a teacher of others may be expected to be learned himself. They ought to be found to be possessed of superior knowledge; and by this question the apostle impliedly reproves them for their failure. The form of a question is chosen because it conveys the truth with greater force. He puts the question as if it were undeniable that they were grossly inconsistent in conduct.

22.synthesis:
The accusation escalates from theft to adultery and finally to sacrilege — and the last charge is the most pointed, because the Jew who abhorred idolatry as the characteristic Gentile degradation was apparently not above treating idol temples as legitimate plunder. Calvin traces this to a universal category: sacrilege is the profanation of the divine majesty, and whether one plunders a pagan temple or treats God's commands with contempt, the category is the same — a violation of what belongs to God.

Ellicott notes that the charge of robbing pagan temples was commonly brought against Jews in this period (compare Acts 19:37, where the Ephesus town clerk specifically acquits Paul's companions of this charge, implying the accusation was current and credible). Barnes documents that the Jewish Talmud itself accuses certain celebrated rabbis of adultery by name, and defines sacrilege more broadly as devoting to improper purposes what rightly belongs to God.

voices.html: He fitly compares sacrilege to idolatry, as it is a thing of the same kind; for sacrilege is simply a profanation of the Divine Majesty, a sin not unknown to heathen poets. As the Gentiles ascribed the majesty of their gods to idols, they only thought it a sacrilege when any one plundered what was dedicated to their temples. But Paul here points to the general truth: whatever violates what belongs to God falls under the same category.

Commit sacrilege — properly, rob temples, idol temples, with a pointed antithesis to that abhorrence of idols on which the Jew prided himself. They may have thought the idol temples fair plunder. At any rate, it is clear that this charge was commonly brought against them. Comp. Acts 19:37, where the town-clerk of Ephesus specially acquits Paul and his companions of robbing temples, implying the charge was current.

There is no doubt that adultery was a crime very common among the Jews. The Jewish Talmud accuses some of the most celebrated of their rabbies, by name, of this vice. Sacrilege is the robbing of God; withholding from him what is his due, or devoting to other purposes what is owed to him. Thus understood, it stands as the culmination of the charge against those who boast in the law while breaking it.

23.synthesis:
The charge is crystallized: the Jew who boasts in the law dishonors God by breaking it. Calvin locates the particular weight of this accusation in the fact that the Jews avowed God as their lawgiver — their transgression was not merely a moral failure but a contradiction of their highest profession. Ellicott notes the structural choice: whether v.23 is read as a question or as a summary statement, its force is identical — boasting proclaimed conviction of the law's excellence, while breaking it denied that conviction in practice.

Barnes makes a penetrating observation about the epistemology of conduct: actions are the truest test of real opinions. Whatever a person's speculative theology may assert about God's law, his life delivers a verdict that carries more evidential weight than his words. A life of transgression does the law more dishonor than any boast does it honor, and the watching Gentile world draws its own conclusions accordingly.

voices.html: Though every transgressor dishonors God, yet he justly imputes in this respect a special fault to the Jews; for as they avowed God as their Lawgiver, and yet had no care to form their life according to his rule, they clearly proved that the majesty of their God was not so regarded by them, but that they easily despised him.

This verse has been regarded, not as a question, but as a summary answer to the previous questions: You, who make all this boast in the Law, by breaking the Law, dishonour God. There is a certain force in this view. Boasting of the law proclaimed their conviction of its excellence and obligation; breaking it denied it in practice, and the denial carries more weight.

To boast in the law implied their conviction of its excellence and obligation, as a man does not boast of that which he esteems to be of no value. By boasting of the law, they proclaimed their conviction that it was from God. By breaking it, they denied it. And as actions are a true test of men's real opinions, their breaking the law did it more dishonour than their boasting of it did it honour.

24.synthesis:
Paul cites Scripture to confirm what practice has demonstrated: The name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you. The source of the quotation is disputed — Calvin thinks Ezekiel 36:20 is more likely than Isaiah 52:5, since Ezekiel is full of reproofs directed at the people's own conduct; Ellicott finds it in the LXX of Isaiah 52:5 and notes that Paul takes the aptest words available without being careful about their original context. Both agree that the prophetic tradition grounds the charge.

Clarke supplies a remarkably apt rabbinic parallel from Debarim rabba, where a rabbi is condemned for preaching against usury while practicing it — the very pattern Paul is describing. Barnes presses the practical point: Jewish hypocrisy and crime led the pagan world to despise a religion observed to have no effect over its professors, and the reproach terminated ultimately on God himself.

voices.html: I think this quotation is taken from Ezekiel 36:20, rather than from Isaiah 52:5; for in Isaiah there are no reproofs given to the people, but that chapter in Ezekiel is full of reproofs. Some think that it is a proof from the less to the greater: since the Prophet upbraided the Jews of his time that on account of their captivity, the glory of God was ridiculed among the Gentiles, much more will this apply when the cause is the people's own moral failure.

From the LXX version of Isaiah 52:5. The sense of the original is that the name of God is dishonoured by the enslavement and oppression of his people. The Apostle is not careful as to the particular context from which he draws. He knew that he was giving the substance of Scripture, and he takes the aptest words that occur to him at the moment.

In Debarim rabba it is said: The rulers destroy the influence of their own words among the people; and this is done when a rabbin, sitting and teaching in the academy, says, Do not take usury, and himself takes it; do not commit rapine, and himself commits it. That the Jews were exceedingly lax in their morals is thus demonstrated from their own sources.

That is, your conduct is such as to lead the heathen world to blaspheme and reproach both your religion and its Author. By your hypocrisy and crimes the pagan world is led to despise a religion which is observed to have no effect in purifying and restraining its professors; and of course the reproach will terminate on the Author of your religion — that is, the true God.

25.synthesis:
Circumcision, the covenant sign of belonging to Israel, is of genuine value — but only conditionally. Calvin argues that circumcision was always a spiritual symbol: it pointed to the inward cutting away of corruption, and when that spiritual reality was absent the sign had no effect and in fact became a liability. The distinction between sign and reality signed is what the entire argument hinges on. Wesley notes sharply that Paul does not say circumcision justifies — only that it profits — and that profit is thoroughly conditional.

Clarke introduces a striking rabbinic parallel that actually supports Paul's argument: even the rabbis themselves allowed that an apostate or ungodly Israelite must go to hell despite his circumcision. Barnes explains the covenantal background: circumcision was the rite by which membership in Abraham's commonwealth was recognized, a real advantage — but always an advantage conditional on the obedience it signified.

voices.html: He dissipates by anticipation what the Jews might have objected in their defense: since circumcision was a symbol of the Lord's covenant, they seemed not to have gloried in vain; but as they neglected what the sign signified, and regarded only the outward form, he gives this answer — that they had no reason to lay claim to any thing on account of the bare sign. The true character of circumcision was a spiritual promise, which required faith for its reception.

For circumcision verily profiteth — it is a blessing to belong to the Church of God and wear the sign of the covenant, provided the terms of the covenant are complied with. But if thou be a breaker of the law — if thou do not observe the conditions of the covenant, the outward sign is both without meaning and without effect. This was a maxim of the rabbins themselves; for they allowed that an apostate or ungodly Israelite must go to hell, notwithstanding his circumcision.

Circumcision indeed profiteth — he does not say justifies. How far it profited is shown in the third and fourth chapters. Thy circumcision is become uncircumcision — is so already in effect. Thou wilt have no more benefit by it than if thou hadst never received it. The very same observation holds with regard to baptism.

This was the peculiar rite by which the relation to the covenant of Abraham was recognised; or by which the right to all the privileges of a member of the Jewish commonwealth was acknowledged. The apostle was not disposed to deny that they possessed this advantage; but it was an advantage that was conditional on the obedience it signified, not a guarantee of exemption from judgment.

26.synthesis:
If circumcision without law-keeping becomes uncircumcision, the reverse also holds: the uncircumcised Gentile who keeps the law's requirements will have his uncircumcision counted as circumcision before God. Calvin frames this as a logical argument about subordination and ends: circumcision looks to the law, is subordinate to it, and must therefore be inferior to it in value; keeping the law exceeds the value of the sign instituted for its sake. The argument is from purpose — the sign exists for the thing signified, not the reverse.

Clarke reads this as establishing that obedience to the spirit and design of the law, even without the outward rite, has covenantal standing before God. Barnes notes that Paul does not expressly affirm that Gentiles actually achieve this level of obedience, but introduces the case to clarify the true nature and conditional character of Jewish rites — their value was always instrumental, never intrinsic.

voices.html: This is a very strong argument. Every thing is below its end and subordinate to it. Circumcision looks to the law, and must therefore be inferior to it: it is then a greater thing to keep the law than circumcision, which was for its sake instituted. It hence follows, that the uncircumcised, provided he keeps the law, far excels the Jew with his barren and unprofitable circumcision, if he be a transgressor of the law.

Therefore if the uncircumcision keep the righteous requirements of the law — if the Gentile be found to act according to the spirit and design of the law, his acting thus uprightly, according to the light which God has afforded him, will be reckoned to him as if he were circumcised and walked agreeably to the law.
If the uncircumcision — that is, a person uncircumcised — keep the law — walk agreeably to it — shall not his uncircumcision be counted for circumcision — in the sight of God?

The apostle does not expressly affirm that this was ever done; but he supposes the case, to show the true nature and value of the rites of the Jews. Shall not his uncircumcision — shall the fact that he is uncircumcised stand in the way of the acceptance of his services? The implied answer is: no, because the rite's value was always conditional and instrumental.

27.synthesis:
The Gentile who fulfills the law will condemn — put to shame by contrast — the Jew who, despite possessing the written law and the external rite of circumcision, transgresses. The logic is the same that Jesus employs in Matthew 12:41-42, where the Ninevites rise in judgment against the generation that rejected him. Ellicott directs the reader there explicitly: the idea is condemnation by contrast, the obedient Gentile standing as a living refutation of the Jew's claim that privilege guarantees standing.

Calvin specifies the charge precisely: the violation does not come from possessing the outward rite but from persisting in neglect of spiritual worship while maintaining the sign. Barnes resolves the difficult phrase by the letter and circumcision: the preposition marks the condition or circumstance — with all the advantages of the written Law and of circumcision — making the transgression the more inexcusable rather than constituting its cause.

voices.html: He does not mean that they violated the law, because they had the literal circumcision; but because they continued, though they had the outward rite, to neglect the spiritual worship of God, even piety, justice, judgment, and truth, which are the chief matters of the law. The Gentile who fulfills the substance stands as a living refutation of the claim that the sign suffices.

Judge thee — comp. Matthew 12:41-42, The men of Nineveh shall rise in judgment with this generation, and shall condemn it. The idea is that of putting to shame by contrast. By the letter — the preposition here marks the condition or circumstance under which the action is done, and might be paraphrased, with all the advantages of the written Law and of circumcision.

And shall not uncircumcision, which is by nature — the Gentile, who is by birth not obliged to be circumcised — if it fulfill the law — if such a person act according to the spirit and design of the law — judge, condemn thee, who, whilst thou dost enjoy the letter, the written law, and bearest in thy body the proof of circumcision, dost transgress that law?

Yea, the uncircumcision that is by nature — those who are, literally speaking, uncircumcised — fulfilling the law as to the substance of it, shall judge thee — shall condemn thee in that day. Who by the letter and circumcision — who, having the bare, literal, external circumcision — transgressest the law.

28.synthesis:
Paul now draws the implications explicitly: genuine Jewish identity is not a matter of descent, profession, or external sign. Calvin grounds this in the whole tenor of Scripture — the people are everywhere commanded to circumcise their hearts, and that inward circumcision is what God himself promises to perform. Wesley states the logic directly: one who is a Jew in outward show only is not a Jew in the most important sense — not one of God's beloved people, whatever his genealogy or ritual status.

Clarke traces the prophetic background: Jeremiah 4:4, Jeremiah 9:26, and Ezekiel 44:7-9 all define circumcision in its truest sense as a matter of the heart. Barnes frames the point covenantally: the separation of the Jewish people was intended from the beginning to produce a people holy in heart and in life, not merely a people marked externally — a design not generally understood in the time of the apostles, but abundantly declared in the Hebrew Scriptures.

voices.html: The meaning is, that a real Jew is not to be ascertained, either by natural descent, or by profession, or by an external symbol; that the circumcision which constitutes a Jew, does not consist in an outward sign only, but that both are inward. What he subjoins with regard to true circumcision, is taken from various passages of Scripture; for the people are everywhere commanded to circumcise their hearts, and it is what the Lord promises to do.

For he is not a Jew — a genuine member of the Church of God — who has only an outward profession. Neither is that circumcision — circumcision is a rite which represents a spiritual thing, namely, the change and purification of the heart, as may be seen Jeremiah 4:4, Jeremiah 9:26, Ezekiel 44:7, 44:9.

For he is not a Jew — in the most important sense, that is, one of God's beloved people — who is one in outward show only; neither is that the true, acceptable circumcision, which is apparent in the flesh.

He who is merely descended from Abraham, and is circumcised, and externally conforms to the law only, does not possess the true character contemplated by the separation of the Jewish people. Their separation required much more. It contemplated a people holy in heart and in life. This cannot be denied, though it was not generally understood in the time of the apostles.

29.synthesis:
The chapter closes with one of Paul's most compressed theological statements: a Jew is one inwardly, and circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the letter. Calvin explains the grammar of the contrast: the letter is the outward rite without the inward reality; the Spirit is the spiritual design of the rite, which when God's voice penetrates the heart rather than merely sounding at the ears produces genuine transformation and a new identity before God.

Wesley connects the phrase directly to Deuteronomy 30:6, where God promises to circumcise the hearts of his people — the inward Jew is the one in whom that promise is fulfilled. Clarke insists the circumcision of the heart is by the Spirit of God, who is the author of all spiritual affections and holy purposes. The closing phrase — whose praise is not from man but from God — seals the point: the inward Jew seeks no human recognition for covenant standing, since God alone searches the heart and renders the true and final verdict.

voices.html: He calls the outward rite without piety the letter, and the spiritual design of this rite the spirit; for the whole importance of signs and rites depends on what is designed. Where the voice of God sounds, all that he commands, except it be received in sincerity of heart, will remain in the letter — that is, in the dead writing; but when it penetrates into the heart, it becomes spirit, that is, it has life and vigor.

But he is a Jew — a true member of the Church of God — who is one inwardly. And the acceptable circumcision is that of the heart, in the spirit — by the Spirit of God, who is the author of all spiritual affections and holy purposes. Or, every thing here is to be understood spiritually, and not literally; for without holiness none can please God, and without holiness none can see him.

But he is a Jew — one of God's people — who is one inwardly, in the secret recesses of his soul. And the acceptable circumcision is that of the heart, in the spirit — referring to Deuteronomy 30:6, the putting away all inward impurity — and not in the letter. Whose praise is not from men, but from God — the only searcher of the heart.

But he is a Jew — he comes up to the design of the Jewish institution; he manifests truly what it is to be a Jew — who is one inwardly, who has the true spirit and fulfills the design of their being separated as a peculiar people. This passage proves that the design of separating them was not merely to perform certain external rites, but to be a people holy in heart and in life.

1.synthesis: Paul opens the chapter by appealing to a principle his readers already know, whether they are Jews familiar with the Mosaic law or Romans familiar with civil law: law has authority over a person only while that person lives. Death dissolves legal obligation. This is not a legal technicality but the foundational premise for the argument that follows. Calvin notes that Paul had already briefly addressed the abrogation of the law in chapter 6, but now returns to the theme more fully — because the law, while it holds someone separated from Christ and bound to itself, can do nothing but condemn. RWP observes that Paul addresses those who "know law" as a principle, not merely the Mosaic code specifically — the Romans, Jewish or Gentile, would grasp the general principle from their own legal experience. Wesley frames it as Paul's continued effort to wean Jewish believers from their attachment to the Mosaic law, introducing the general principle that will be illustrated by the marriage analogy.

voices.html: Though he had, in a brief manner, sufficiently explained the question respecting the abrogation of the law, yet as it was a difficult one he now shows more at large how the law, with regard to us, is become abrogated; and then sets forth what good is thereby done to us: for while it holds us separated from Christ and bound to itself, it can do nothing but condemn us.

To men that know the law (γινώσκουσιν νόμον). The Romans, whether Jews or Gentiles, knew the principle of law. Law has authority over a person only as long as he lives — a general legal principle, not restricted to the Mosaic code.

The apostle continues the comparison between the former and present state of a believer, and at the same time endeavours to wean the Jewish believers from their fondness for the Mosaic law. The general principle is stated first: law has dominion over a man only as long as he lives.

2.synthesis: The illustration: a married woman is bound by law to her husband while he lives, but when he dies she is released from the law of the husband. Ellicott notes the analogy is not perfectly symmetrical — in Paul's application it is the believer, not the Law, who dies; but the illustration is apt enough to prove the point that death dissolves legal obligation. RWP explains the Greek: ἡ ὕπανδρος γυνή = "the wife under a husband," a late word found only here in the NT. The wife δέδεται, stands bound — perfect passive indicating a state of ongoing legal obligation. Wesley is brief: she is freed from the law which gave the husband a peculiar property in her. Barnes emphasizes that Paul's illustration turns on the general principle that death severs the bond — the specific relationship being secondary to the legal fact it demonstrates.

voices.html: The illustration is not quite exact. The Law is here represented by the husband, but the Apostle does not mean to say that the Law dies to the Christian, but that the Christian dies to the Law. The important thing is the principle that death dissolves legal obligation — that is what the analogy is intended to prove.

She is freed from the law of her husband — from that law which gave him a peculiar property in her. When her husband dies the legal bond is dissolved and she is no longer his by law — she is at liberty to belong to another.

This verse is a specific illustration of the general principle in verse 1, that death dissolves those connexions and relations which make law binding in life. It is a simple illustration from a well-understood legal fact — the marriage bond is dissolved by the death of either party.

3.synthesis: The legal consequence is pressed home: if she takes another man while her husband lives, she will be called an adulteress. But if her husband dies, she is free, and she is not an adulteress if she marries another man. The symmetry is crucial for Paul's argument. RWP explains the verb χρηματίσει: it means to receive a name or designation — she will be publicly designated as an adulteress. Clarke draws out the mutual nature of the marriage bond: either party's death dissolves the engagement equally. Barnes notes that Paul is making the legal principle as clear as possible before the application, which is the real point of the passage: as death dissolves the wife's obligation to the old husband, so the believer's death to the law (through Christ) dissolves the obligation that the law once held.

voices.html: She shall be called (χρηματίσει). Future active — she will receive the public designation of "adulteress." The law does not merely think of her differently; it names her differently. This is the force of the legal illustration: the naming changes when the legal status changes.

The object of the apostle's similitude is to show that each party is equally bound to the other; but that the death of either dissolves the engagement. She would be called an adulteress, not merely in the eyes of society, but by the law itself — which is precisely the point Paul will apply to the believer's former subjection to the Mosaic law.

Paul is making the legal principle as sharp as possible before the application: just as death alone dissolves the wife's obligation and makes remarriage lawful, so only death dissolves the law's claim on the believer — and in Christ, that death has occurred.

4.synthesis: Paul makes the application: believers have died to the law through the body of Christ, so that they may belong to another — to him who was raised from the dead — in order to bear fruit for God. The logic is delicate: it is the believer who dies, not the law, through union with the death of Christ's physical body on the cross. Calvin unpacks the mechanism: Christ, by his cross, triumphed over sin and cancelled the handwriting that held us bound; the law's condemnation is discharged through his death, so the believer who is united with him in that death is no longer under the law's condemnation. RWP notes that ἐθανατώθητε (aorist passive of θανατόω, "to put to death") is strong — "ye were made to die" — more than simply "you died." Wesley brings it to practical application: believers are as free from the Mosaic law as a wife is when her husband has died — and the purpose is positive, not merely negative: to bear fruit for God in newness of life.

voices.html: Christ, by the glorious victory of the cross, first triumphed over sin; and that he might do this, it was necessary that the handwriting, by which we were held bound, should be cancelled. This handwriting was the law — and Christ, by dying, cancelled it. We therefore, who are members of his body, are through his death freed from the law.

Ye also were made to die to the law (ἐθανατώθητε τῷ νόμῳ). First aorist passive — somewhat stronger than simply "ye died." You were put to death with respect to the law. By the body of Christ — by the death of the human body of Christ upon the cross.

Thus ye also — are now as free from the Mosaic law as a wife is when her husband is dead. By the body of Christ — offered up; that is, by the merits of his death. And the freedom is for a purpose: that we might bring forth fruit unto God, not merely escape condemnation.

5.synthesis: The contrast with the old state: when we were "in the flesh" — under the dominion of unregenerate human nature — the sinful passions aroused by the law were at work in our members to bear fruit for death. RWP is careful about the phrase "in the flesh": the flesh is not inherently sinful, but it is subject to sin, and being "in the flesh" describes the condition of being governed by it — equivalent to being "under the law." Wesley puts it plainly: this was the condition before faith in Christ, when believers were "carnally minded" and sin was aroused and intensified by the law's prohibitions. Ellicott adds the paradox: the old alliance was not unproductive — it bore fruit abundantly, but the fruit was death. Clarke specifies that "the motions of sins" (τὰ παθήματα τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν) literally means the passions and sufferings of sins — not mere inclinations but active forces working through bodily members.

voices.html: The new alliance ought not to be unproductive, for the old alliance was not unproductive. Before that mortification of the flesh which proceeds from our relation to the death of Christ, we bore fruit — abundantly — but it was fruit unto death. The contrast is between two kinds of productivity, not between productivity and barrenness.

When ye were in the flesh — carnally minded, in a state of nature; before we believed in Christ. Our sins which were by the law — accidentally occasioned or irritated thereby. Wrought in our members — in all the faculties of the soul and body.
In the flesh (ἐν τῇ σαρκί). The "flesh" is not inherently sinful, but is subject to sin. It is what Paul means by being "under the law" — dominated by the lower nature rather than the Spirit.

6.synthesis: But now we are released from the law, having died to what held us captive, so that we serve in the newness of the Spirit rather than the oldness of the letter. The pivot "but now" (νῦν δέ) echoes the great transition of 3:21. RWP notes that the imperfect κατειχόμεθα ("we were held") recalls the same verb used in 1:18 — the law held us as prisoners. Wesley identifies the two old binding elements: we were held by the law of Moses and our old sinful nature. Calvin emphasizes the positive result: the spirit animates and renews from within, while the letter merely commands from without. Ellicott contributes a textual note: translators followed a false reading here; the correct Greek shows that it is the believer who died (to the law) rather than the law dying — consistent with the marriage analogy. Barnes makes the practical point: freedom from law is not freedom from obedience, but freedom from the law as a system of condemnation and external compulsion.

voices.html: He pursues the argument derived from the opposite effect of things — if the restraint of the law availed so little to bridle the flesh, that it became rather the exciter of sin; then, that we may be freed from this evil, we need to be freed from the law. And note: service in newness of spirit is not from the outward constraint of the letter but from the inward life of the Spirit.

Being dead to that whereby we were held — to our old husband, the law. That we might serve in newness of spirit — in a new, spiritual manner. And not in the oldness of the letter — not in a bare literal, external obedience without inward life.

Under the gospel. This verse states the consequences of the gospel in distinction from the effects of the law. Freedom from law is not freedom from obedience — but from the law as a system of condemnation, of external command without inner power to obey.

7.synthesis: Paul anticipates the devastating objection: if the law excites and intensifies sin (vv. 5-6), does that make the law itself sinful? His answer is again μὴ γένοιτο — by no means. He pivots to personal testimony: he would not have known sin except through the law. The commandment "you shall not covet" revealed covetousness as sin — and in so doing, paradoxically stirred it. Barnes notes that this is an objection a Jew would raise with no slight indignation: "Are you calling the holy law of God sinful?" Calvin explains the resolution: the law is not sin's source but its revealer; the commandment names the sin that was already lurking, and in naming it, gives sin a kind of occasion to assert itself more vigorously. RWP notes a pointed contemporary application: some today oppose all inhibitions because they stimulate violations — this is the error Paul addresses. Wesley reads this section as the apostle's dramatic personation of a man under law who is gradually coming to see his true condition.

voices.html: Since it has been said that we must be freed from the law, in order that we may serve God in newness of spirit, it seemed as though this evil belonged to the law — that it leads us to sin. But as the law is holy and good, it cannot be the cause of sin; it is rather the occasion, by revealing sin and making it more evident.

The objection which is here urged would very naturally rise, and would be urged with no slight indignation. The Jew would ask, "Are you charging the holy law of God with being the cause of sin and death?" Paul's answer is that the law is not sin's source — it is sin's mirror, revealing what was already there.

Is the law sin? (ὁ νόμος ἁμαρτία?) A pertinent query in view of what he had said. Some people today oppose all inhibitions and prohibitions because they stimulate violations. That is half-baked thinking — Paul refutes it here. The law reveals sin; it does not create it.

8.synthesis: Sin, taking occasion through the commandment, produced in Paul every kind of covetousness — for apart from the law, sin lies dead. The law names what is forbidden; the naming gives sin an occasion, a base of operations from which to operate more aggressively. RWP draws out the military metaphor in ἀφορμήν: it means a starting-place or base of operations — sin uses the commandment as an advanced post from which to mount its attacks. Calvin describes this as the clearest possible expression of what he had said earlier: knowledge of sin without the law is buried; the law brings it to light and also, paradoxically, provokes it. Barnes notes that Paul is here describing the psychological phenomenon of prohibition intensifying desire — the commandment forbidding covetousness made covetous desires more vivid and urgent. Wesley identifies "sin" here as inbred corruption — the commandment does not produce it but agitates what was already there.

voices.html: Finding occasion (ἀφορμὴν λαβοῦσα). A starting place from which to rush into acts of sin — sin uses the commandment as a military base of operations. Just so drinking men use the argument of prohibition to justify their excess. Sin exploits the law's very definiteness.

He expresses most clearly the meaning of his former words; for it is the same as though he had said, that the knowledge of sin without the law is buried. It is a general truth, which he presently applies to his own case: without the commandment, the covetousness lay dormant; the commandment woke it.

Paul here depicts the influence of law in exciting to evil desires. Perhaps nowhere has he evinced more consummate knowledge of the human heart. The commandment forbidding covetousness seems to have made covetous desires more vivid and urgent — the very definiteness of the prohibition sharpened the desire to transgress.

9.synthesis: "I was once alive apart from the law, but when the commandment came, sin came alive and I died." This verse is autobiographical but representatively so — Paul speaks for all who pass from a state of naive moral unconsciousness into the full awareness of divine demand. RWP evokes Denney's phrase: "the lost paradise in the infancy of men" — the state before conscience fully woke and moral responsibility arrived. Calvin explains: Paul does not mean he lived literally without law at some period; rather, there was a time when the law's full demand had not yet penetrated his consciousness — and in that state he had a kind of life. Wesley describes it as a condition of "much life, wisdom, virtue, strength" — in his own estimation, before the commandment arrived in its full force. Barnes notes the aorist tense of "sin revived" (ἀνέζησεν) — sin sprang back to vigorous life. The implied sequence is: unconsciousness → commandment arrives → sin becomes alive → the man dies (spiritually).

voices.html: I was alive (ἔζων). Imperfect active — apparently, "the lost paradise in the infancy of men" (Denney), before the conscience awoke and moral responsibility came, "a seeming life" (Shedd). Sin revived (ἀνέζησεν) — sprang back to life, aorist active.

He means to intimate that there had been a time when sin was dead to him or in him. But he is not to be understood as though he had been without law at any time; this word has a peculiar import — there was a time when the law's demands had not yet penetrated his full consciousness, and in that time he had a kind of life.

And I was once alive without the law — without the close application of it. I had much life, wisdom, virtue, strength: so I thought. But when the commandment came — when the law, a part put for the whole, came to my heart with its full force — sin revived, and I died.

10.synthesis: The commandment that was meant for life, he found, led to death. The tragic irony is in the contrast between design and result: the law was given to produce life, but encountered fallen human nature and became the occasion of death. Ellicott preserves the force of the Greek word order: "the very commandment the one for (meant for) life — this I found unto death." Calvin distinguishes two dimensions: the commandment shows a way of life, and it was given so that those who kept it might obtain life. But in practice, it revealed how far short human beings fall, and thus became the instrument of condemnation. Wesley stresses the original design: "doubtless it was originally intended by God as a grand means of preserving and increasing spiritual life." Barnes draws the contrast simply: the law was adapted to give life — had it been obeyed, it would have done so; but the sinful nature encountered it and turned it into an instrument of death.

voices.html: Which was ordained to. — "The very commandment which was for life I found to be for death." The Law was instituted in order that it might give life to those who were under it and who kept it. But in Paul's experience — and in the experience of all sinners — this intended life became actual death.

Two things are stated here — that the commandment shows to us a way of life in the righteousness of God, and that it was given in order that we, by keeping the law of the Lord, might obtain eternal life. But in practice the commandment that was meant for life led him to condemnation — not because the law was at fault, but because he was.

The commandment which was intended for life — doubtless it was originally intended by God as a grand means of preserving and increasing spiritual life, and leading to life everlasting. But in the event it became the occasion of my condemnation and death.

11.synthesis: For sin, seizing an opportunity through the commandment, deceived Paul and through it killed him. The verse repeats the theme of verse 8 but with an additional word: ἐξηπάτησεν, deceived. Sin does not merely use the law as a base of operations; it uses it to deceive. Clarke traces the deception: the commandment forbidding covetousness actually stirred and fed it; sin promised gratification while leading to spiritual death. RWP explains the Greek: ἐξαπατάω = to completely lead astray (ἐξ + a privative + πατέω, to walk) — sin utterly misdirected him. Wesley adds the mechanism of the deception: "while I expected life by the law, sin came upon me unawares and slew all my hopes." Barnes connects this verse explicitly with verse 8, reading v. 11 as a repetition with the addition of the deception motif — the law's clarity about sin's nature does not prevent sin from using it against the person who thought obedience might save him.

voices.html: Beguiled me (ἐξηπάτησέν με). First aorist active — completely made me lose my way (ἐξ + ἀπατάω). Sin used the commandment as cover for a deception: presenting the path of law as a road to life while leading to death.

Sin, deriving strength from the law, threatening death to the transgressor, deceived me — drew me aside to disobedience, promising me gratification and pleasure. It worked death by that which is good, using the very commandment as the instrument of the deception.

Deceived me — while I expected life by the law, sin came upon me unawares and slew all my hopes. I thought the law would give me life if I kept it; instead, sin used the law to expose how completely I had failed to keep it.

12.synthesis: The conclusion Paul draws from the preceding argument: so the law is holy, and the commandment is holy and righteous and good. The three adjectives — holy, righteous, good — are a defense of the law against any slander arising from what Paul has just said. RWP identifies these as God's own qualities reflected in what he gave: holy (like its divine source), righteous (just in its requirements), good (beneficial in its design). Calvin sees the addition of a redundant clause as Paul's rhetorical habit — the emphasis is deliberate. Wesley distills the defense to its essentials: the law is holy as it springs from and partakes of the holy nature of God; righteous as it is just in every requirement; good as it promotes the welfare of those who follow it. Clarke notes Paul may have been conciliating his Jewish readers, whose love for the law he shares even while showing its inability to justify. Barnes: the conclusion clears the law of all blame — the fault lies entirely with human sinfulness, not with the law itself.

voices.html: Holy, and righteous, and good (ἁγία καὶ δικαία καὶ ἀγαθή). The commandment is God's and so holy like him, just in its requirements and good in its intentions. This is the conclusion (wherefore, ὥστε) to the query in verse 7 — the law is entirely vindicated; sin alone is to blame.

The commandment — that is, every branch of the law. Is holy, and just, and good — it springs from, and partakes of, the holy nature of God; it is every way just and right in itself; it is designed wholly for the good of man. The fault that produced death lay entirely in sin, not in the law.

The conclusion clears the law of all blame. The source of the evil is not the law but human sinfulness — the corrupt nature that encounters the law and turns its good purposes to destructive ends. So the law is holy, and just, and good — thoroughly and without qualification.

13.synthesis: A final objection: did that which is good (the law) become death for me? Again Paul denies it — μὴ γένοιτο. It was sin that worked death through the good commandment, so that sin might be shown for what it truly is: sinful beyond measure. Ellicott frames Paul's answer precisely: it is not possible that the holy, good law could simply lead men to ruin — so something else must be the cause. That something is sin, which used the law's very goodness as an instrument. Wesley identifies the purpose: sin worked death by the law so that it might appear in its true colors — "that it might appear sin" — utterly and manifestly sinful. RWP notes the term ὑπερβολή (hyperbole): sin becomes exceeding sinful, sinful beyond measure, sin shown in its true extremity. Clarke frames it as the Jew's final objection, which Paul answers by turning the table: the very extremity of sin's abuse of the law demonstrates both the law's innocence and sin's guilt.

voices.html: Was it possible that the Law, holy and good as it was, could simply lead miserable men to death and ruin? No. It was not the Law that did this. It was sin. Sin used the Law as its instrument — and the fact that sin needed to use something good in order to produce death shows how thoroughgoing and pervasive its corruption is.

Was then that which is good made the cause of evil to me; yea, of death, which is the greatest of evil? Not so. But it was sin, which was made death to me, inasmuch as it wrought death in me even by that which is good — that it might appear sin; that the exceeding sinfulness of sin might be shown.

Hyperbole (ὑπερβολή). Sin becomes exceeding sinful — sinful beyond all measure. This is the purpose: through the commandment, sin stands fully and finally exposed in its true character as utterly and manifestly sinful.

14.synthesis: "We know that the law is spiritual, but I am of the flesh, sold under sin." With this verse Paul enters the most contested passage in the chapter. The contrast is absolute: the law = spiritual (given by the Spirit, of divine character); the speaker = carnal, sold under sin as a slave at auction. Calvin explicitly applies this to a regenerate man in whom the remnants of the flesh war against the law, while the spirit would gladly obey. Barnes concurs: after careful examination, he reads vv. 14-25 as describing the post-conversion state of Paul. Ellicott agrees: "we Christians all know that the Law is spiritual" — the regenerate perspective is the whole framing. Clarke dissents: he reads this as an unregenerate man under the Mosaic law, without the Spirit — sold under sin completely, not partly. Wesley reads it as Paul dramatizing the condition of someone in the process of moving from the legal to the evangelical state — not yet fully delivered. The question divides the tradition, and the synthesis notes it openly.

voices.html: He then sets before us an example in a regenerate man, in whom the remnants of the flesh are wholly contrary to the law of the Lord, while the spirit would gladly obey it. He makes first a comparison between nature and the law: the law being spiritual and man carnal, what agreement can there be between them?

After all the attention I have been able to give to this passage, I regard it as describing the state of a man under the gospel — descriptive of the operations of the mind of Paul subsequent to his conversion. The "we know" of the opening clause shows the perspective is that of a Christian who has come to understand the law's spiritual demands.

But I am carnal, sold under sin — this describes an unregenerate man without the Spirit of God — under the entire dominion of his evil propensities. Sold under sin as a slave in the market, with no power of self-determination, no indwelling of the Spirit to oppose corruption.

14.key_tension: Calvin, Ellicott, and Barnes read vv. 14-25 as the regenerate believer's experience of remaining sin in conflict with the new nature; Clarke reads it as the unregenerate or pre-conversion state of a person under law without the Spirit; Wesley places it in the transitional movement from the legal to the evangelical condition.

15.synthesis: "For I do not understand my own actions. For I do not do what I want, but I do the very thing I hate." The inner dividedness is described with stark honesty. Ellicott's translation is precise: "that which I perform I know not" — not mere ignorance but the absence of conscious, willed direction. The higher part of the person that should preside over action is not actually governing it. Barnes notes that this verse is the heart of the experiential description: the prevailing bent of the regenerate will is toward good, yet evil is performed — a contradiction the person finds inexplicable and distressing. RWP captures the spiritual-psychological insight: "my spiritual perceptions are dulled, blinded by sin" — what is done is not done with clear self-understanding. Clarke reads this as the condition of one who is entirely under sin's dominion, and has no resource to resist it. Wesley sees the will clearly on the side of good — an important evidence that this is not total depravity but a divided state.

voices.html: Rather, that which I perform I know not — I act blindly, without any conscious direction of the will; that higher part of me which should preside over and direct my actions is not actually doing so. The real self is not expressed in what gets done.

The evil which I do — the sin of which I am conscious, and which troubles me. I allow not — I do not approve; I do not wish it; the prevailing bent of my inclinations and desires is against it. This is the language of one who is truly a Christian, and who has really a desire to do right.

I know not (οὐ γινώσκω). "I do not recognize" — in its true nature. My spiritual perceptions are dulled, blinded by sin (2 Cor. 4:4). The dual life pictured here by Paul finds an echo in us all — the struggle between what we will and what we do.

15.key_tension: Calvin, Ellicott, and Barnes read vv. 14-25 as the regenerate believer's experience of remaining sin in conflict with the new nature; Clarke reads it as the unregenerate or pre-conversion state of a person under law without the Spirit; Wesley places it in the transitional movement from the legal to the evangelical condition.

16.synthesis: "Now if I do what I do not want, I agree with the law, that it is good." The logic is subtle: the very fact that I hate what I do is evidence of two things — first, that the law is right (since I agree it is right to hate this); second, that there is a part of me that is not identified with the sin. Wesley is brief and exact: the word "good" here implies all three of the adjectives used in v. 12 — holy, righteous, and good. Ellicott draws the application to believers: the Christian may find in this verse a test of his piety — the very struggle against evil proves that evil is not loved, and therefore that the law which condemns evil is really loved and affirmed. Barnes: this proof from unwilling consent is the argument from moral experience — we know we want the right even when we fail to do it. RWP: "I speak with" (σύμφημι, only here in NT) — to agree in the deepest sense, not merely to assent intellectually.

voices.html: But the fact that I desire to do what is right is itself a witness to the excellence of the Law, which commands that which I desire. Christians may here find a test of their piety: the very struggle with evil shows that it is not loved or approved, and that the law which condemns it is really loved.

It is good — this single word implies all the three that were used before (Romans 7:12): holy, just, and good. My unwilling performance of what I hate proves that I inwardly agree with the law's verdict — the law is right, and I know it.

The very struggle with evil shows that it is not loved. This is the argument from moral experience: we know we want the right even when we fail to do it. And to want the right, even against our performance, is to affirm the law that commands the right.

16.key_tension: Calvin, Ellicott, and Barnes read vv. 14-25 as the regenerate believer's experience of remaining sin in conflict with the new nature; Clarke reads it as the unregenerate or pre-conversion state of a person under law without the Spirit; Wesley places it in the transitional movement from the legal to the evangelical condition.

17.synthesis: "So now it is no longer I who do it, but sin that dwells within me." Paul distinguishes the true self — the willing, reasoning, conscience-shaped person — from the indwelling sin that acts against that person's will. Barnes is careful: this is "evidently figurative language," for it is really the man who sins when evil is committed. But it distinguishes between the dominant desire of the person and the sinful impulse that overrides it. Calvin insists this is not an excuse: Paul is not claiming to be blameless, as those triflers who think naming sin as the agent exempts the person. Ellicott: there is a genuine dualism in the soul — the higher self is not to be identified with the lower enthralled by sin. RWP: "No longer do I do it (the real Εγώ, my better self) — and yet there is responsibility and guilt, for the struggle goes on." Wesley adds the complementary description: sin makes, "as it were, another person" that tyrannizes over the man.

voices.html: This is not the pleading of one excusing himself, as though he was blameless, as the case is with many triflers who think that they have a sufficient defense to cover all their wickedness, when they throw the blame on the flesh. Paul here distinguishes the true self from indwelling sin — not to remove guilt, but to describe accurately the divided condition.

This, then, appears to be the true explanation of the difficulty. There is really a dualism in the soul. I am not to be identified with that lower self which is enthralled by sin. The higher self — the inward man — refuses to be identified with the sinful impulse, even while being dragged by it.

This is evidently figurative language, for it is really the man that sins when evil is committed. But the apostle makes a distinction between sin and that which is properly himself — his prevailing desire, the dominant tendency of his renewed nature — and that corrupt nature from which evil proceeds despite his will.

17.key_tension: Calvin, Ellicott, and Barnes read vv. 14-25 as the regenerate believer's experience of remaining sin in conflict with the new nature; Clarke reads it as the unregenerate or pre-conversion state of a person under law without the Spirit; Wesley places it in the transitional movement from the legal to the evangelical condition.

18.synthesis: "For I know that nothing good dwells in me, that is, in my flesh. For I have the desire to do what is right, but not the ability to carry it out." The self-diagnosis is precise: Paul does not say nothing good dwells in him simpliciter, but in his flesh — the unregenerate part of his nature. RWP notes this is not a complete statement of depravity: he says "not absolutely good" — there is something in him (the renewed mind, the inner man) that wills the good, even if the flesh cannot carry it through. Calvin stresses the depth of the indictment: so far as it regards himself — his own natural resources — he is wholly depraved; but this itself implies that there is something else in him that is not merely himself. Barnes: the desire to do right is present; what is absent is the power of execution — this asymmetry between willing and performing defines the condition. Wesley: in my flesh = the whole man as he is by nature, unregenerate; but the presence of desire proves the person is not in a state of entire natural corruption.

voices.html: He says that no good by nature dwelt in him. Then "in my flesh" means the same as though he had said, "so far as it regards myself." In the first part he arraigns himself as being wholly depraved; yet the very fact that he can distinguish the flesh from the indwelling self implies there is more to him than flesh.

The desire to do right is present; what is absent is the power of execution. This asymmetry between willing and performing defines the condition described in this passage — a condition of real desire for good combined with chronic inability to achieve it by one's own strength.

In me (ἐν ἐμοί). Paul explains this by "in my flesh" (ἐν τῇ σαρκί μου), the unregenerate man "sold under sin" of verse 14. No good thing (οὐκ — ἀγαθόν) — not absolutely good. This is not a complete statement of utter depravity — the will to good is present; what is lacking is the power.

18.key_tension: Calvin, Ellicott, and Barnes read vv. 14-25 as the regenerate believer's experience of remaining sin in conflict with the new nature; Clarke reads it as the unregenerate or pre-conversion state of a person under law without the Spirit; Wesley places it in the transitional movement from the legal to the evangelical condition.

19.synthesis: "For I do not do the good I want, but the evil I do not want is what I keep on doing." The paradox of verse 15 is restated even more sharply — the repeated present tense ("I keep on doing") heightens the chronic, unresolved character of the struggle. Barnes notes this is substantially a repetition of v. 15, and the repetition shows how fully the apostle's mind is absorbed in the subject. RWP points to a dramatic illustration: the extreme case of this practise of evil is seen in those who know and abhor their sin but cannot stop — the drunkard, in his more reflective moments, knows his vice is destroying him. Calvin's reading: the faithful, though rightly influenced, are so conscious of their defects that they fail to do what they approve; this constant falling short is not theoretical but painfully concrete. Clarke sees this as describing the unregenerate man's total incapacity: his will may incline toward good, but without the Spirit he lacks any real power to pursue it.

voices.html: The same view is to be taken of the expression which next follows — the faithful, however rightly they may be influenced, are yet so conscious of their defects that they fail to do what they approve; and, on the contrary, do what they hate. This is not theoretical failure but a chronic, painful reality.

This is substantially a repetition of what is said in verse 15. The repetition shows how full the mind of the apostle was of the subject; and how much he was inclined to dwell on it as the source of deep distress and anxiety. The evil I do not want — I keep on doing.

An extreme case of this practise of evil is seen in the drunkard, who in his more reflective moments knows and abhors his vice, yet continues in it. The will is against it; the performance is otherwise. That is precisely the condition Paul is describing.

19.key_tension: Calvin, Ellicott, and Barnes read vv. 14-25 as the regenerate believer's experience of remaining sin in conflict with the new nature; Clarke reads it as the unregenerate or pre-conversion state of a person under law without the Spirit; Wesley places it in the transitional movement from the legal to the evangelical condition.

20.synthesis: "Now if I do what I do not want, it is no longer I who do it, but sin that dwells within me." The argument of verse 17 is repeated — for the same reason verse 19 repeated verse 15. The repetition is not redundant but rhetorically emphatic: the doubled form of the argument about the divided self reinforces that the analysis is not an excuse but a phenomenological description of the conflict. RWP is direct: "no longer do I do it (the real Εγώ, my better self) — and yet there is responsibility and guilt, for the struggle goes on." Clarke reads this differently: "my will is against it; my reason and conscience condemn it" — but since no power of resistance follows from this moral condemnation, it describes a person utterly unable to help himself, before the Spirit's enabling. Barnes treats v. 20 as another repetition of vv. 16-17, solidifying the conclusion: the prevailing direction of the renewed will is not identified with the evil that overrides it.

voices.html: It is no more I that do it (οὐκέτι ἐγὼ κατεργάζομαι αὐτό). Just as in verse 17 — "no longer do I do it" (the real Εγώ, my better self) — and yet there is responsibility and guilt, for the struggle goes on. The distinction identifies, not excuses.

My will is against it; my reason and conscience condemn it. But sin that dwelleth in me — the principle of sin, which has possessed itself of all my carnal appetites and passions, and rules and reigns there, producing acts of transgression in every part of my conduct.

This verse is also a repetition of what was said in verses 16-17. The prevailing direction of the renewed will is not identified with the evil that overrides it — the person who does unwanted evil affirms by that very fact that the evil is truly unwanted.

20.key_tension: Calvin, Ellicott, and Barnes read vv. 14-25 as the regenerate believer's experience of remaining sin in conflict with the new nature; Clarke reads it as the unregenerate or pre-conversion state of a person under law without the Spirit; Wesley places it in the transitional movement from the legal to the evangelical condition.

21.synthesis: "So I find it to be a law that when I want to do good, evil lies close at hand." Paul now names the pattern he has been describing a "law" — an inward constraining power that operates with the regularity and compulsion of a law. Wesley identifies it as "an inward constraining power flowing from the dictate of corrupt nature." RWP simply notes: this is the law (or principle) already set forth in vv. 18-19 — the pattern of evil's persistent presence whenever good is willed. Calvin sees Paul here assembling a fourfold law: the law of God (the rule of righteousness), the law of the mind (the regenerate conscience), the law of the members (sinful impulse in the body), and the law of sin (the mastery sin exercises). Barnes traces the variety of opinions about "law" in this verse and concludes it means a principle — an observed regularity, not a Mosaic code. The verse describes the mechanics of the conflict, while the next two verses name the combatants.

voices.html: Here Paul supposes a fourfold law. The first is the law of God, which alone is properly so called, the rule of righteousness by which our life is rightly formed. To this he joins the law of the mind, the regenerate conscience that approves the good. Then the law of the members — sinful impulse — and the law of sin, its tyrannizing power. These four are in perpetual conflict.

I find then a law — an inward constraining power, flowing from the dictate of corrupt nature. When I would do good — when my will and reason are strongly bent on doing right — evil is present with me, ready to act, never absent.

There is a law — a principle — whose operation I experience whenever I attempt to do good. It means a principle, an observed regularity: whenever good is willed, evil rises in opposition. The verse describes the mechanics of the inner conflict.

21.key_tension: Calvin, Ellicott, and Barnes read vv. 14-25 as the regenerate believer's experience of remaining sin in conflict with the new nature; Clarke reads it as the unregenerate or pre-conversion state of a person under law without the Spirit; Wesley places it in the transitional movement from the legal to the evangelical condition.

22.synthesis: "For I delight in the law of God, in my inner being." The word is συνήδομαι — not merely consent (as in v. 16) but delight, rejoicing with the law. RWP notes it is found only here in the NT and means "I rejoice with the law of God" — my real self, after the inward man (κατὰ τὸν ἔσω ἄνθρωπον), delights in it. Barnes says the word expresses not only intellectual approbation but "a pleasure in it — a positive happiness in its contemplation." Calvin identifies the inward man with the spirit or renewed mind — as opposed to the flesh or outer man. Clarke, reading this as the experience of the unregenerate, suggests that even an unrenewed person who accepts the Old Testament as divine revelation must acknowledge the purity and excellence of the law — but this acknowledgment falls short of the Spirit's inward working. Wesley sees this verse as the high-water mark of the upward struggle: the closer the approach to the evangelical state, the more evident the delight in God's law becomes.

voices.html: For I delight in (συνήδομαι γάρ). Old verb, here alone in N.T. — "I rejoice with the law of God," my real self "after the inward man" (κατὰ τὸν ἔσω ἄνθρωπον). The delight is the deepest expression of the true self.

The word here (συνήδομαι) occurs nowhere else in the New Testament. It properly means to rejoice with any one, and expresses not only approbation of the understanding, but a pleasure in the law — a positive happiness in its contemplation. It is the fullest possible affirmation of the law by the inner person.

Consent (συνηδομαι) — it is not the same verb as in verse 16; this signifies more than consent, for it includes gratification and delight. The inward man here is the renewed spirit, as opposed to the outer man or flesh — the regenerate part that genuinely loves the law of God.

22.key_tension: Calvin, Ellicott, and Barnes read vv. 14-25 as the regenerate believer's experience of remaining sin in conflict with the new nature; Clarke reads it as the unregenerate or pre-conversion state of a person under law without the Spirit; Wesley places it in the transitional movement from the legal to the evangelical condition.

23.synthesis: "But I see in my members another law waging war against the law of my mind and making me captive to the law of sin that dwells in my members." The image of warfare (ἀντιστρατευόμενον — to carry on a campaign against) names the conflict for what it is: active, military, unceasing. RWP notes this verb is rare (only here in NT) and implies a sustained campaign, not a skirmish. Ellicott describes this opposing law as one that has its chief seat of activity in the bodily members, ready to take advantage of every fleshly impulse. Wesley uses the military metaphor vividly: the law of the mind is the "law of my mind" — the dictate of the mind that delights in God's law — and the opposing force wages war against it from within. Barnes: in my body — the sinful propensities that reside in the corrupt human nature — there is a competing principle that constantly makes war against the renewed mind. The result of the campaign, at this point, is captivity: the prisoner of war is taken.

voices.html: Warring against (ἀντιστρατευόμενον). A rare verb — to carry on a campaign against. Only here in the N.T. A different law from the law of the mind — an opposing principle that wages sustained military war against the renewed conscience and drags the person into captivity.

Another law — a different law. "In my members" — that has its chief seat of activity in the members, the bodily and sensual part of the person. This is the law of sin, which is ready to take advantage of every fleshly impulse and uses it to make war on the higher principle.

But I see another law in my members — in my body; in my flesh; in my corrupt and sinful propensities. Warring against the law of my mind — making war against the renewed principle of my heart and mind. Making me captive — like a prisoner of war dragged away against his will.
