Wesley observes that the glorying is possible because the suffering is not separable from the purpose of God working toward a known end. Robertson notes the same verb καυχώμεθα used here as in verse 2, forming a deliberate parallel between glorying in glory and glorying in tribulations.

voices.excerpt: Tribulation in itself is not a blessing; but when God uses it to produce patience, and patience to produce experience, the saint can glory in the instrument by which God brings him to a richer approbation of his own faithfulness.

The sequence — tribulation, patience, experience, hope — is a genuine climax. Each step is more excellent than the last; the tribulation is the raw material from which God fashions the final jewel of a hope that cannot disappoint.

We glory in tribulations because they are not accidental but providential — not punitive but educational. God shapes the suffering Christian as a craftsman shapes metal, and the Christian who sees this may exult even in the fire.

4.synthesis:
The chain of grace continues: patience produces experience (δοκιμή), a word that denotes the result of testing — approved character, the quality of one who has been tried and found genuine. Calvin notes that this is not a general human virtue but a specifically Christian one, produced by the Spirit through suffering. The word δοκιμή appears also in 2 Corinthians 2:9 and Philippians 2:22, always with the sense of demonstrated trustworthiness.

Ellicott renders it approvedness — the state of having been proved. Barnes explains that the Christian who has endured tribulation with patience has had his faith tested and confirmed; the experience of God's sustaining grace in affliction is itself a kind of capital, a store of proved confidence. The progression is not a mechanical formula but describes what commonly happens in the life of believers who endure — the experience of God's faithfulness deepens and transforms into a hope that is not embarrassed.

voices.excerpt: Experience (δοκιμή) — better rendered 'approvedness' or 'proof.' It is the quality of a coin that has been tested and found genuine — the proved character that tribulation-endured patience leaves in the believer.

James has a similar gradation (Jas. 1:3-4) with different vocabulary, which shows that this is not Paul's invention but the common experience of the saints. Approved character is the harvest that patience cultivates.
Approvedness (δοκιμή) — the result of the test. Not merely that one has been tested, but that the test has produced an approved result. This is the Christian's track record with God.

5.synthesis:
And hope does not put us to shame — the chain of grace that began with tribulation terminates in a hope that will never be found wanting. The verb (καταισχύνει) means to disgrace or disappoint — the hope of the Christian will not turn out to have been misplaced. Paul's reason is the love of God poured out in the heart through the Holy Spirit.

Calvin sees this as the internal seal of assurance: the Spirit convinces the believer of the love of God, and this subjective experience is not delusional but reflects the objective reality of election and atonement. Ellicott notes that the aorist poured out (ἐκκέχυται) refers to Pentecost and to each individual experience of regeneration — a completed act with continuing effects. Barnes stresses that this is not merely feeling loved but having one's heart flooded with the reality of divine love. Wesley connects this to the witness of the Spirit — the love of God shed abroad in the heart is the Spirit's own testimony to the believer's adoption. Robertson notes the intensive compound ἐκκέχυται: the love is poured out in abundance, not merely trickled in.

voices.excerpt: Hope does not put us to shame because it is not grounded in our own resolution but in the love of God, which the Spirit pours into our hearts as an internal testimony that we are indeed objects of the divine favor.

The love of God — his love to us, shed abroad — poured into our hearts as a sweet perfume, filling and overflowing the whole soul with the consciousness of God's fatherly love. This is the Spirit's work.

The Spirit who indwells the believer testifies to the love of God not by whispering words but by flooding the heart with the experience of that love — making it a living, felt reality rather than a theological proposition.

6.synthesis:
For while we were still weak, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly. Paul here grounds the love of God in its supreme historical demonstration. The word weak (ἀσθενής) describes moral impotence — not merely physical frailty but the inability of sinners to help themselves. The phrase at the right time (κατὰ καιρόν) indicates divine appointment: this was not accidental but occurred at the moment God had determined.

Calvin stresses the grace in the object: Christ died not for the strong, the deserving, or the nearly righteous, but for the ungodly — those actively at enmity with God. Ellicott notes that the repetition of while we were yet in verses 6, 8, and 10 is a deliberate rhetorical emphasis — Paul wants to nail down the total unworthiness of the recipients of this love. Barnes observes that the death of Christ for the ungodly is the strongest possible proof that his love is not conditioned on the merit of its object. Wesley sees this as the linchpin of Christian assurance: if God loved us while we were ungodly, we may be confident he will not abandon us now.

voices.excerpt: Christ died for the ungodly — not for those making progress toward goodness, not for the morally ambiguous, but for those who were God's enemies. This is what makes the love extraordinary: its object was wholly undeserving.

At the right time (κατὰ καιρόν) — in the fullness of time, when divine wisdom had appointed. The death of Christ was no tragedy of circumstances but the arrival of a precisely scheduled redemptive event.

While we were yet weak — morally helpless, without power to save ourselves. Paul uses ἀσθενής not in the physical but in the ethical sense — sinners cannot lift themselves out of their condition; they need an external Savior.

7.synthesis:
Paul pauses to illustrate the extraordinary character of Christ's death by comparison with human heroism. For scarcely for a righteous man will one die, though perhaps for a good man someone would even dare to die. The distinction between righteous (δίκαιος) and good (ἀγαθός) is subtle but real. Calvin reads righteous as one who fulfills legal obligations — respectable but not warmly beloved — while good denotes one who is also kind, generous, beneficent, arousing affection as well as respect.

Ellicott accepts this distinction and notes that human courage may stretch to dying for a generous benefactor; but even this is rare. Barnes stresses the comparative: if human love is so rarely powerful enough to produce self-sacrifice even for a beloved friend, how infinitely more remarkable is Christ's death for those who were enemies. The rhetorical move sets the reader up for verse 8's climax. Robertson notes the rhetorical restraint: Paul says perhaps (τάχα) even for the good man — not asserting it but acknowledging its possibility as the extreme of human heroism.

voices.excerpt: The righteous man may be respected but not warmly loved; scarcely would one die for mere respectability. For a good man — one who has shown kindness and generosity — someone might dare it. But Christ died for neither category.

Human devotion can rise to sacrifice for a beloved benefactor; but this is the maximum of natural love. Paul cites this maximum precisely to show how infinitely Christ's love exceeds it — for Christ died for enemies.
The argument is from the less to the greater: if even extraordinary human love scarcely reaches self-sacrifice for the worthy, the love that sacrifices for the unworthy is beyond all human measure.

8.synthesis:
But God demonstrates his own love toward us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us. This is the climax of the argument that began in verse 6. The verb demonstrates (συνίστησιν) means to commend, to make stand out, to prove by exhibition — God has given an objective historical proof of his love. The pronoun his own (ἑαυτοῦ) is emphatic: not a general love, but the specific love of God himself.

Calvin notes that Paul does not say while we were seeking him or while we were improving but while we were sinners — still actively in rebellion. The demonstration is therefore complete and irrefutable; God did not wait for us to become loveable before loving us. Ellicott observes that the death of Christ is the divine proof, exhibited in history, that cannot be erased. Barnes connects this to assurance: if God proved his love at its most costly point while we were his enemies, we may be confident it will not fail now that we are his friends. Wesley sees this as the foundation of full salvation — the love established here is the ground of everything that follows in the Christian life.

voices.excerpt: God demonstrates — the word means more than declares; it is the act of putting evidence before the court. The cross is the exhibit; the sinners for whom Christ died are the proof that this love is not conditional on worthiness.

His own love — the pronoun is emphatic, distinguishing the divine love from the pale human analogies of verses 7. God's love is sui generis; it stooped to die for enemies while human love scarcely extends to benefactors.

While we were yet sinners — the condition Paul names is chosen to maximize the grace. Not merely imperfect, not merely struggling, but sinners in the full sense: those whose will was set against God. Into this condition Christ entered in love.

9.synthesis:
Much more then, having now been justified by his blood, we shall be saved from the wrath through him. Paul draws a how much more argument (a fortiori): if the harder thing has been accomplished — the justification of sinners at the cost of Christ's blood — the easier consequence must certainly follow. Salvation from the coming wrath is the easier consequence because the guilty party has already been dealt with.

Calvin explains that justification is the great removal of the legal obstacle; once that is accomplished, preservation is assured. Ellicott notes that wrath here is eschatological — the final judgment — not merely present divine displeasure. Barnes stresses the security this provides: if the greater work is done (reconciliation at the price of the Son's blood), the lesser continuation of that reconciliation cannot be in doubt. Wesley sees this as one of the strongest texts for final perseverance — not presumption, but logical inference from what God has already done. Robertson notes the contrast between blood (the historical death of Christ as the instrument of justification) and through him (his ongoing heavenly intercession as the means of continued preservation).

voices.excerpt: The argument is from the greater to the lesser: if God gave his Son to justify us — the costliest possible act — he will not withhold the lesser gift of preserving us from final judgment. The reasoning is irresistible.

Saved from the wrath — the eschatological wrath of the final day, not merely temporal affliction. The justified believer may stand before that day without condemnation because the basis of condemnation has been removed.

Much more then — Paul's favorite form of argument (a fortiori). The greater act, justification by the blood of Christ, makes the lesser act, preservation from final wrath, not merely probable but certain.

10.synthesis:
The argument of verse 9 is repeated in stronger terms: if while we were enemies we were reconciled to God through the death of his Son, much more, having been reconciled, we shall be saved by his life. The word enemies (ἐχθροί) is the strongest possible term — not merely strangers but those actively hostile to God. The reconciliation occurred in this condition.

Calvin observes the contrast between death and life: the death of Christ accomplished the reconciliation; the ongoing life of the risen Christ (his intercession, his continuing priesthood) secures the application. Ellicott distinguishes: by his death = the ground of reconciliation; by his life = the power of preservation. Barnes notes that the living Christ who intercedes (cf. Romans 8:34, Hebrews 7:25) is the continuing guarantee that what the cross accomplished will not be lost. Wesley sees the risen life of Christ as the inexhaustible fountain from which sanctification and final salvation flow. Robertson notes that his life refers specifically to the resurrection life — Christ risen, ascended, and interceding.

voices.excerpt: The death secured reconciliation when we were enemies; the life secures its maintenance now that we are friends. The living intercession of Christ is the seal upon what the cross accomplished — it cannot be broken.
Saved by his life — the resurrection life of the exalted Christ, by which he intercedes and applies what his death procured. The dying Christ reconciled; the living Christ preserves to the end.
If God reconciled enemies by the costliest possible act, he will certainly save friends by the continued intercession of the risen Son. The logic of grace demands it; the nature of God requires it.

11.synthesis:
And not only so, but we also rejoice in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received the reconciliation. The third not only so in this chapter marks another step beyond what has already been established. Not only are we at peace with God, not only are we secure against final wrath — we actually rejoice in God himself. God is not merely our Savior from something but our positive joy and delight.

Calvin notes that this glorying in God is the summit of Christian experience: it is not merely relief from judgment but the positive enjoyment of the divine nature. Ellicott observes that the Greek verb (καυχώμεθα) is the same used in verses 2 and 3 — the glorying in hope, in tribulations, and now in God himself is one continuous and escalating exultation. Barnes explains that the reconciliation (ἡ καταλλαγή, with the article — the reconciliation, the definite redemptive event) has been definitively received; it is not a process still in progress but a gift already accepted. Wesley sees this as the culminating privilege: the reconciled person does not merely fear God less but loves and delights in God more.

voices.excerpt: Glorying in God — this is the apex of Christian life. Not merely relief from condemnation, not merely hope of heaven, but the positive delight in God himself as our Father, our portion, our supreme good.
The same verb (καυχώμεθα) used in vv. 2 and 3 here reaches its highest object — not hope, not tribulations, but God himself. The whole chapter is a single ascending chord of Christian exultation.

We have now received the reconciliation — the aorist marks its completeness. It is not offered for our consideration but received into our possession. And having received it, we rejoice in the Giver rather than merely in the gift.

12.synthesis:
Paul now embarks on the Adam-Christ typology that forms the theological spine of the chapter, but he does not complete his initial comparison. The sentence breaks off in what grammarians call an anacoluthon — Paul begins "just as through one man sin entered the world..." but never completes the "so also" half until verses 15–19. Robertson, Ellicott, and Calvin all note this broken syntax.

The crux is the phrase eph' hō (ἐφ' ᾧ), translated "because" or "in whom." The interpretive question: did death spread to all because all individually sinned, or because all were somehow implicated in Adam's sin? Calvin, Barnes, and Robertson read eph' hō as "because," but understand "all sinned" to include the federal/imputed dimension — all sinned in Adam, and the proof that this imputed guilt is real is that all die. Clarke reads eph' hō as referring to individual transgression without imputation — each person dies because of their own personal sin. Ellicott acknowledges both readings as exegetically viable. The anacoluthon itself is part of the argument: Paul is struggling to find language adequate to express the solidarity between humanity and Adam, and the sentence structure breaks under the weight.

voices.excerpt: He now begins to enlarge on the same doctrine by comparing with it what is of an opposite character — the comparison of Adam and Christ. But the sentence does not complete itself; Paul is feeling after the mystery of our union with Adam.

Paul begins a comparison between the effects of Adam's sin and the effects of the redemptive work of Christ, but he does not complete it in the same form because of the parenthetical matter in vv. 13-17 — a true anacoluthon.

Death passed upon all men for that (eph' hō) all have sinned — meaning that each individual became a sinner and by that individual sin incurred mortality. The text does not require us to posit the imputation of Adam's guilt to his posterity.

Eph' hō is most naturally rendered 'because,' making 'all sinned' the reason death spread universally. Whether this refers to personal sin, imputed sin, or seminal participation in Adam's act is the question that has divided interpreters from antiquity.

12.key_tension: eph' hō crux: Calvin/Barnes/RWP read federal/imputation implications into 'all sinned in Adam'; Clarke reads individual personal transgression; the anacoluthon (broken sentence) is noted by RWP, Ellicott, and Calvin as reflecting Paul's struggle to express Adamic solidarity.

13.synthesis:
Before completing the Adam-Christ comparison, Paul inserts a parenthesis (vv. 13-14) to defend the claim that sin and death operated universally even before the Mosaic law. For until the law, sin was in the world; but sin is not charged (ἐλλογεῖται) when there is no law. The argument is: if people died between Adam and Moses — and they did — there must have been a ground of death other than violation of the specific Mosaic code.

Calvin explains that sin without a specific positive command is not formally charged in the legal accounting sense — yet people still died. This proves that death's dominion rested on a cause prior to and independent of the Mosaic law, namely the Adamic transgression. Ellicott notes that ἐλλογεῖται is a commercial term for entering an item in an account — sin is not formally entered to one's account without a specific law to transgress. Barnes sees the period from Adam to Moses as Paul's test case: death reigned there without Mosaic law, proving that Adam's sin was the regnant cause. Wesley explains that this does not mean God overlooked sin in the pre-Mosaic era; he punished it, but on grounds other than violation of the specific Sinaitic code.

voices.excerpt: Sin is not charged (imputed) in the formal accounting sense when there is no specific commandment to violate. Yet death reigned before Moses — which proves that death's universal dominion rested on Adam's sin, not on individual violations of the Mosaic code.

Ἐλλογεῖται — a commercial term, to enter to one's account. Sin without specific positive law is not formally debited; yet the wages of sin (death) were still being paid from Adam to Moses. Therefore another account was open — Adam's.

The period from Adam to Moses is Paul's decisive test case. Death reigned there universally, though the Mosaic law had not yet been given. This can only be explained by the transmission of Adam's guilt — the primary cause of universal death.

14.synthesis:
Yet death reigned from Adam until Moses, even over those who had not sinned in the likeness of Adam's transgression, who is a type of the one to come. The parenthesis of verse 13 reaches its conclusion: death reigned universally, even over infants and those who had committed no specific act like Adam's explicit command-violation. This universal reign of death is the proof that all humanity was affected by Adam's act.

Calvin explains that those who sinned in ways unlike Adam's transgression (no explicit positive prohibition) nonetheless died — demonstrating that the condemnation flows from Adam's federal headship, not from individual acts of defiance. Ellicott notes that the contrast is between Adam's specific transgression of an explicit divine command and the sins of the intervening period, which, while real, were not of the same formally-charged character. Barnes connects this to verse 13: the very fact that even those dying without formal Mosaic transgression shows that Adam's guilt was the inherited ground of death. The verse ends with the key typological assertion: Adam is a type of the one to come — the first man is a figure, an analogy, a shadow of Christ, the second man whose obedience reverses what Adam's disobedience unleashed.

voices.excerpt: Death reigned even over those who did not sin as Adam sinned — by violating an explicit positive command. This shows that the dominion of death rests not on individual formal transgression but on the inherited guilt of Adam's representative act.

Adam is a type (τύπος) of the one to come — a figure, a prefigurement, an analogy. The parallel between Adam and Christ is not perfect; Paul is about to show (vv. 15-19) that the gift far exceeds the trespass in every respect.

The reign of death over infants — who had committed no personal act — is Paul's most pointed proof. They died, though they had not sinned after the similitude of Adam's transgression; therefore Adam's sin was the cause of their death.

15.synthesis:
Paul now completes the Adam-Christ comparison but immediately qualifies it with a how much more: but the free gift is not like the trespass. The comparison is real but not symmetrical — the gift exceeds the trespass in multiple dimensions, which Paul elaborates through verses 15-17.

The first contrast: by the trespass of one the many died; but much more did the grace of God and the gift by the grace of the one man Jesus Christ abound to the many. Calvin notes the deliberate asymmetry: the fall was an act of one man affecting many, but the redemption is an act of the God-man whose grace infinitely exceeds what was lost. Ellicott explains that the many in both halves does not mean the same group — the first many is all humanity affected by Adam; the second many is all who receive the gift (Paul does not assert universalism here). Barnes notes the much more: grace is more powerful than sin, life is more potent than death, the second Adam accomplishes more than the first undid. Wesley sees in this the boundlessness of divine grace — it was not circumscribed to undo only what Adam did, but overflowed to accomplish far more.

voices.excerpt: The free gift is not like the trespass — not in kind (grace is not sin's mere cancellation) and not in degree (grace immeasurably exceeds the trespass in its power). God does not merely repair the damage; he transforms the situation entirely.
Much more did the grace abound — the 'much more' (πολλῷ μᾶλλον) is theological, not merely rhetorical. Grace is intrinsically more powerful than sin, life than death, restoration than ruin.

The gift abounded to the many — Paul's 'many' in the second clause does not require universal salvation; it means 'the many who receive the gift,' just as the first 'many' means 'all who fell in Adam.' The parallelism of scope is deliberate.

16.synthesis:
And the gift is not like that which came through the one who sinned. Paul identifies a second dimension of asymmetry. The judgment which arose from one trespass brought condemnation; but the free gift arising from many trespasses brought justification. The contrast is stark: one sin → condemnation; many sins → justification. The gift not only reverses Adam's single act but deals with the entire accumulated mass of human transgression.

Calvin notes that this is a greater work: it is one thing to undo one trespass, another to deal with the multitude of sins that have accumulated since Adam. Grace proves its superiority precisely in this — it is not merely an equal and opposite force to Adam's fall but something qualitatively more comprehensive. Ellicott observes that the judgment (κρίμα) came from one transgression, but the grace (χάρισμα) came through many trespasses — the accumulation of sin was the occasion for the greater manifestation of grace (cf. 5:20). Barnes sees this as confirming that justification is not merely clearing the Adamic charge but pronouncing the believer righteous in the face of all their sins. Wesley draws from this the confidence of complete pardon: not one sin is excepted from the scope of the gift.

voices.excerpt: One offense brought condemnation; the gift came through many offenses to bring justification. This shows the gift to be the greater: it did not merely cancel one debt but the entire ledger of human transgression accumulated since the fall.

From one trespass — condemnation. From many trespasses — justification. The asymmetry is deliberate: grace is not Adam's equal but Adam's superior, dealing not merely with his original sin but with all that flowed from it.

The free gift (χάρισμα) arose out of many trespasses unto justification — the very accumulation of sins became the field in which grace proved its superior power. The more sins, the more grace needed; the more grace manifested.

17.synthesis:
The third dimension of asymmetry: for if by the trespass of the one, death reigned through the one, much more those who receive the abundance of grace and of the gift of righteousness will reign in life through the one, Jesus Christ. The contrast is between the reign of death through Adam and the reign of life through Christ — but the reigning parties have changed. In Adam, death reigned as king over humanity; in Christ, the recipients of grace themselves reign in life.

Calvin notes this is more than a mere reversal: those who were subjects of death's dominion become co-regents in life. Ellicott observes that the gift of righteousness (δικαιοσύνης) is the gift of a right standing before God, not merely moral improvement — it is forensic before it is transformative. Barnes stresses the abundance of grace (τῆς περισσείας τῆς χάριτος): not a minimal provision just adequate to undo the damage, but an overflow. Wesley sees in reign in life not merely survival but active dominion — the Christian is not a refugee from death but a victor over it. Robertson notes that the future tense will reign includes both present experience of life in Christ and the eschatological consummation of resurrection glory.

voices.excerpt: Death reigned; now life reigns — but more than that, those who receive the gift themselves reign in life. They are not merely freed from death's dominion; they are elevated to a kingly dignity far surpassing what Adam lost.

The gift of righteousness — not righteousness as a moral quality alone, but as a forensic status, a gift imputed to the believer. Out of this status flows both present life in Christ and the future reign of glory.

The abundance of grace — not a bare sufficiency but a superabundance. God did not provide the minimum necessary to undo Adam's damage; he poured out grace in excess, so that life's reign exceeds death's in every way.

18.synthesis:
Paul now resumes the main comparison he began in verse 12 and states it in its simplest form: so then, as through one trespass there was condemnation to all men, even so through one act of righteousness there was justification of life to all men. The symmetry is complete: the one act of Adam brought condemnation to all; the one act of Christ brings justification to all.

All commentators are careful to qualify all men in the second clause. Calvin insists that Paul is comparing scope, not asserting that every human being is actually saved — the parallel is between the universal scope of condemnation through Adam (all humanity) and the universal scope of the provision through Christ (all humanity, potentially). Barnes explains that justification of life is offered to all and sufficient for all, but becomes effective only for those who receive it by faith. Ellicott distinguishes between the objective provision (universal in scope) and the subjective application (conditioned on union with Christ through faith). Wesley notes that the offer is as wide as the condemnation — no one is excluded from the scope of Christ's atoning act. No commentator reads this as teaching universalism; the context of chapters 1-4 and the condition of faith are assumed throughout.

voices.excerpt: Paul is comparing the scope of two representative acts, not asserting the salvation of every individual. As Adam's trespass extended in its effects to all humanity, so Christ's righteous act extends in its provision — but not all receive it.

Justification of life to all men — the provision is coextensive with the condemnation: all who were under Adam's curse are within the scope of Christ's remedying act. But the application requires the union of faith.

The free gift came upon all men — the provision is as wide as the fall. No human being is excluded from the scope of Christ's atoning work. Whether all receive it is another question; the offer is without restriction.

19.synthesis:
For as through the one man's disobedience the many were constituted sinners, even so through the obedience of the one the many will be constituted righteous. This verse restates verse 18 with a change of vocabulary that makes the forensic character explicit. The verb constituted (κατεστάθησαν) means to appoint, to place in a status — not merely to be regarded as, but to be established in a legal standing.

Calvin notes that the verb proves the imputation character of the parallel: just as sinners were constituted in a sinful status through Adam's representative act (not merely because they personally imitated him), so believers are constituted righteous through Christ's representative obedience (not merely because they personally kept the law). Ellicott observes that the verb carries forensic weight — it is the language of juridical declaration, not merely of moral influence. Barnes stresses that this is the heart of the imputed righteousness doctrine: Christ's obedience is the ground upon which believers are legally constituted righteous. Wesley accepts the parallel but emphasizes that the constituted righteousness becomes real and personal through the Spirit's application. Robertson notes that obedience here encompasses Christ's entire life of righteousness, not only his death.

voices.excerpt: Constituted (κατεστάθησαν) — placed in a status. Just as we were placed in Adam's guilt by his representative act, so we are placed in Christ's righteousness by his representative obedience. This is the forensic heart of the chapter.

The verb 'constitute' is judicial — to appoint to a status, to establish in a standing before the law. It confirms that the parallel between Adam and Christ operates at the level of representative legal status, not merely moral influence.

Will be constituted righteous — the future includes both the present reality (they are being constituted righteous as they believe) and the eschatological completion at the last day. The obedience of Christ is the ground; faith is the condition.

20.synthesis:
And the law entered alongside so that the trespass might increase; but where sin increased, grace abounded all the more. A potentially shocking statement: the Mosaic law entered (a verb suggesting it came in beside the main flow of redemptive history, not as the main river itself) with the purpose of increasing the trespass. This does not mean God intended to make people more sinful, but that the law by defining and specifying sin made the total account of transgression more visible and legally chargeable.

Calvin explains that the law added formal transgression to already-existing sin — when you know what is forbidden and do it anyway, your culpability is greater. Ellicott notes the remarkable purpose clause: that the trespass might increase. The law was designed to bring sin to its full expression so that the remedy could be applied at the point of maximum need. Barnes observes that this is preparatory to verse 21: the greater the sin's expression, the more dramatically grace is seen to triumph over it. Wesley stresses the much more (ὑπερεπερίσσευσεν, a compound that suggests superabundance): wherever sin reached its maximum expression, grace not merely matched it but overwhelmed it. Robertson notes the unusual verb ὑπερεπερίσσευσεν — unique in the New Testament, a double compound suggesting grace's excess above and beyond the excess of sin.

voices.excerpt: The law entered so that the trespass might increase — not to make people more sinful, but to make their sin more formally charged. Where there is a specific prohibition and it is violated, the guilt is greater and the need for grace more apparent.

Where sin increased, grace superabounded — the verb (ὑπερεπερίσσευσεν) is a double compound found nowhere else in the NT. Paul coins it to express that grace does not merely equal sin's excess but exceeds it by a further excess.

The law's preparatory role was to bring sin to its fullest formal expression so that when grace came, it would be seen to have triumphed at the point of maximum resistance — making the victory all the more glorious.

21.synthesis:
Paul reaches the final resting-point of the chapter: so that, as sin reigned in death, even so grace might reign through righteousness to eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord. The two kingdoms are named: sin reigned through the mechanism of death; grace reigns through the mechanism of righteousness, leading to the destination of eternal life.

Calvin notes the personification of grace as a king — not merely God's attitude but an active, ruling power. The instrument of grace's reign is righteousness (δικαιοσύνης) — the same righteousness of God that was declared in chapters 3-4, now seen to be the very scepter with which grace exercises dominion. Ellicott observes the movement: sin reigned in death (death being its domain and outcome); grace reigns through righteousness (righteousness being its instrument) to eternal life (life being its destination). Barnes sees in this verse the grand summary of chapters 1-5: the entire argument about sin's universality, justification by faith, and the Adam-Christ parallel has been heading here — the reign of grace through righteousness to eternal life. Wesley finds here both present experience (grace reigns now in the believer) and eschatological hope (eternal life is the final destination of this reign).

voices.excerpt: Grace reigns through righteousness — righteousness is the instrument of grace's kingship. Grace does not reign by suspending justice but by satisfying it; not by ignoring sin but by dealing with it through the righteousness of Christ.

The final antithesis: sin reigned in death — grace reigns through righteousness to eternal life. The destinations define the kingdoms: death belongs to sin's domain; eternal life belongs to grace's domain.

Through Jesus Christ our Lord — the chapter ends where it began: in Christ. He is the mediator of peace (v. 1), the ground of access (v. 2), the demonstration of love (v. 8), and now the channel of the reigning grace that leads to eternal life.

1.synthesis:
Chapter 6 opens with an antinomian objection that Paul treats as the logical consequence of the doctrine just expounded — if grace abounds where sin increases (5:20), why not sin more to get more grace? What shall we say then? Are we to continue in sin so that grace may increase? Paul does not attribute this objection to a real opponent but raises it himself as the most dangerous misapplication of the gospel of grace.

Calvin observes that the objection proves how completely Paul's doctrine of free justification is being rightly understood — only a doctrine of pure grace without merit would generate this specific objection. If there were any merit involved, the objection could not arise. Ellicott notes that Paul has been so emphatic about grace abounding over sin that the logic seems to point toward antinomianism. Barnes explains that this is not a strawman but the actual inference some were drawing — if grace covers sin, and more sin means more grace, why restrain sin? Wesley sees this as the occupational hazard of genuine evangelical preaching: when grace is preached fully, antinomianism is always the first misapplication. Robertson notes that Paul's answer in verse 2 is not an argument but a horror.

voices.excerpt: The objection proves the doctrine is rightly understood — only a gospel of pure grace without any admixture of human merit could generate the inference that one should sin more to get more grace. The question is wrong but the premise is right.
The objection follows necessarily from 5:20 if misread. Paul does not blame the objector for the inference; he blames the misunderstanding of what grace does to the one who receives it.

This is not an abstract philosophical question but a practical temptation: if grace is greater than sin, and God's glory is shown in forgiving sin, why not sin freely? Paul answers not with philosophy but with the reality of what union with Christ means.

2.synthesis:
By no means! How shall we who died to sin still live in it? Paul's answer is not a moral argument but an ontological one — the question misunderstands what has happened to the believer. The one who has received grace has died to sin. You cannot live in what you have died to.

Calvin explains that dying to sin is not a goal to strive toward but a fact accomplished — the believer's relationship to the power of sin has been definitively altered by union with the crucified Christ. This is not moral improvement but a change of legal and vital status. Ellicott notes the aorist died (ἀπεθάνομεν): a completed past event, not an ongoing aspiration. Barnes observes the logical force: if we are dead to sin, then living in sin is a contradiction in terms — as impossible as a dead man conducting his affairs. Wesley stresses that this is the foundation of sanctification: not a new law but a new nature, not a command to die to sin but the announcement that believers have died to sin. The imperative of holiness rests on the indicative of what has already occurred in Christ.

voices.excerpt: By no means! — not an argument but a recoil of conscience. How shall we who died to sin live in it? The question answers itself: to live in what you have died to is a contradiction in terms, not merely a moral failure.

We died to sin (ἀπεθάνομεν) — aorist, completed action. The death to sin is not an aspiration but a past event, accomplished in our union with Christ's death. To live in sin is therefore not merely wrong but impossible in principle.

The power of sin over the justified believer has been broken by the death of Christ, with which the believer is identified. To continue in sin would require coming back to life in the domain you have left — which contradicts the nature of death itself.

3.synthesis:
Or do you not know that all of us who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death? Paul appeals to baptism as the public confession and symbol of the union with Christ's death that the believer has experienced. The phrase baptized into Christ Jesus uses the preposition into (εἰς) indicating incorporation and identification.

The key exegetical question is whether Paul treats baptism as the cause of the believer's union with Christ's death or as its symbol and public proclamation. Robertson argues explicitly that Paul was not a sacramentarian: the union with Christ was achieved before the baptism; baptism is the outward profession of an inward spiritual relation already attained. Calvin uses baptism as the proof and confirmation of the union — the sign is not empty but attests the reality. Clarke and Barnes emphasize the immersion imagery — going under water pictures burial; coming up pictures resurrection — without resolving the sacramental question. Ellicott notes that into his death is the striking phrase: not merely into Christ in general but specifically into the death of Christ, the death-event that broke sin's power.

voices.excerpt: The translation 'into' makes Paul say that the union with Christ was brought to pass by means of baptism, which is not his idea, for Paul was not a sacramentarian. Baptism is the public proclamation of one's inward spiritual relation to Christ attained before the baptism.

Baptism is not empty — it is the sign that attests and confirms our union with the death of Christ. Paul appeals to it here not as the instrument of union but as the acknowledged symbol of what has happened to those who have received Christ.

Baptized into his death — the preposition carries us into the death-event itself. Whatever view one takes of baptismal efficacy, Paul here identifies the baptized Christian's identity with the specific death of Christ as the pivot of the argument.

3.key_tension: Whether baptism is the cause or the public symbol of union with Christ's death: Robertson explicitly denies sacramentarianism (union precedes baptism); Calvin uses baptism as confirming sign; Clarke/Barnes emphasize immersion imagery without resolving the question.

4.synthesis:
Therefore we were buried with him through baptism into death, so that as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, so we too might walk in newness of life. The baptism imagery is extended: immersion pictures burial, emergence pictures resurrection. The burial confirms the completeness of the death — you bury what is thoroughly dead.

Clarke explains the immersion connection explicitly: going under water represents burial with Christ; rising from the water represents resurrection. Barnes accepts this imagery. Calvin uses the baptismal burial to argue the real death of the old self — we are not merely reformed but buried, our old identity interred. Ellicott notes the purpose clause: burial with Christ has an end goal — that we might walk in newness of life. The resurrection life is not merely a future hope but a present reality to be walked in. Robertson again notes that Paul does not say baptism accomplished the burial — the burial was accomplished by death to sin; baptism depicts it publicly. Wesley emphasizes the newness of life: not merely resuming old life on better terms but an entirely fresh creation, a life qualitatively different from what preceded conversion.

voices.excerpt: It is probable that the apostle here alludes to the mode of administering baptism by immersion — the person being plunged under the water as into a grave, and then rising out of it as from the dead, representing the death and resurrection of Christ.

The burial confirms the completeness of the death. We do not bury the living; we bury what has fully died. To be buried with Christ through baptism is to have the old self declared thoroughly and irrevocably dead.

So that we too might walk in newness of life — the resurrection life of Christ becomes the pattern and the power for the believer's present conduct. It is not merely a future inheritance but a present walk, a daily mode of being.

4.key_tension: The immersion-as-burial imagery is accepted by Clarke, Barnes, and Ellicott; the sacramental question (whether baptism accomplishes or depicts the burial) remains: Robertson insists Paul depicts what happened before baptism; Calvin uses it as confirming sign.

5.synthesis:
For if we have become united with him in the likeness of his death, we shall certainly also be in the likeness of his resurrection. The word united (σύμφυτοι) is botanical — grown together, organically joined, like a graft that has taken. The union is not contractual but organic. Calvin stresses the certainty of the second consequence: if the first union is real (and it is), the second must follow — we cannot be united to Christ's death without being united to his resurrection.

Ellicott notes the future tense shall be (ἐσόμεθα): the resurrection union is partly present experience and partly eschatological — the risen life is already operative but not yet fully manifested. Barnes explains that the logic is one of organic necessity: what is grafted into the death cannot be separated from the resurrection because Christ's death and resurrection are one redemptive event. Wesley stresses the practical implication: the believer's present life is already resurrection life in embryo — not yet glorified but already fundamentally other than the old death-dominated existence. Robertson notes that σύμφυτοι is a unique word in the NT, expressing the most intimate possible union.

voices.excerpt: United (σύμφυτοι) — grown together organically, as a graft into the vine. This is not external association but internal union. If united to the death, the resurrection is certain — we cannot be detached from one half of an indivisible event.

The future 'shall be united' points forward to the final resurrection — but the logic is that the union with Christ's death is already the pledge and guarantee of this future union. The botanic metaphor (grown together) implies organic necessity.

Σύμφυτοι — literally 'grown together,' a botanical metaphor. We have been ingrafted into Christ's death; the life that rises from that death is not separable from us any more than the life of a vine is separable from the grafted branch.

6.synthesis:
Knowing this, that our old man was crucified with him, so that the body of sin might be done away with, so that we would no longer be slaves to sin. The old man (ὁ παλαιὸς ἄνθρωπος) is the pre-conversion self, the entire person as constituted under Adam's headship and sin's dominion. Crucified with him means this old identity has been dealt a death-blow by the cross.

Calvin explains that the old man is not merely a set of sinful habits but the whole nature inherited from Adam — everything we are from the womb as children of the first man. Robertson calls the body of sin the body marked by sin — not the physical body as inherently evil but the entire human organism as sin has taken it captive. Ellicott notes the purpose chain: crucifixion of the old man → destruction of the sin-body → liberation from slavery to sin. The sequence is logical and theological, not merely moral. Barnes observes that done away with (καταργηθῇ) does not mean annihilated but rendered inoperative, put out of business. Wesley stresses that this is already accomplished — the basis of the exhortation in verses 11-13 is this past reality, not an ongoing aspiration.

voices.excerpt: The old man — he means by this the whole nature which we bring from the womb, as children of the first Adam. It is not one department of the self but the entire self as constituted under sin's dominion that was crucified with Christ.

The body of sin — the body as the instrument of sin, the body marked by sin, not the body as inherently evil (Paul is no Platonist) but as sin has commandeered it for its purposes. This took place not at baptism, but only pictured there.

Done away with (καταργηθῇ) — rendered inoperative, put out of commission. The sin-body is not annihilated but deprived of its power to compel; the believer is no longer under its coercion as a slave under a master.

7.synthesis:
For he who has died has been freed from sin. A brief, almost aphoristic statement — but it carries the legal weight of the entire argument. The word freed (δεδικαίωται) is actually the verb for justified — the one who has died has been justified from sin. Death cancels the claims of the old master.

Calvin notes that Paul uses the language of justification here in connection with death — not merely psychological freedom but legal acquittal. The death of the old man in union with Christ's death settles the legal account; sin's claim is discharged. Ellicott observes that this is a legal principle Paul may be drawing from the rabbinic tradition — death satisfies all obligations; a person cannot be prosecuted after death. Barnes explains the application: the believer who has died with Christ is no longer under sin's jurisdiction, just as a dead slave is no longer under the jurisdiction of the master. Wesley sees this as the foundation of the believer's assurance: the past tense (has been freed) declares a completed status, not a hoped-for future achievement. Robertson notes the passive has been justified — freed by another's action, not by the believer's own performance.

voices.excerpt: He who has died has been justified from sin — Paul uses the language of legal acquittal. Death cancels the old debt; the one who died with Christ has had sin's entire claim against him discharged by that death.

Δεδικαίωται — justified, acquitted. Death is the full and final payment. The believer who has died with Christ has satisfied, in Christ, every claim sin had upon him. Sin can no longer summon him to answer.

The principle is legal: death dissolves all obligations to a former master. The slave who dies is free from the master's authority. The believer who died with Christ is free from sin's authority — not by moral effort but by the legal fact of death.

8.synthesis:
Now if we died with Christ, we believe that we shall also live with him. Paul draws the positive consequence of the death-union: life-union. The same logic that applied to the resurrection in verse 5 applies here — the union is indivisible. Death with Christ is not an end but a door; the other side of the door is life with Christ.

Calvin notes the deliberate pairing: if the death-union is real (and it is), then the life-union is certain. This is not an open question but a guaranteed consequence. Ellicott observes that we believe that we shall also live — the faith is not merely in future resurrection (though that is included) but in a life with Christ that is already operative in some sense. Barnes explains that the risen life of Christ is the pattern, the power, and the content of the believer's new life — we will live with him, not merely in the same manner as him. Wesley stresses the assurance implied: the death is certain (we died with Christ); therefore the life is certain (we shall live with him). Robertson notes that the future tense again spans present spiritual life and future bodily resurrection.

voices.excerpt: If we died with Christ — this is not conditional in the sense of uncertain; it is the acknowledged premise. Therefore we shall live with him — this follows with logical necessity. The union is with an indivisible event: death and resurrection.

We shall live with him — not merely a future resurrection hope but a present reality of life in union with the risen Christ. The life we now live in the flesh we live by faith in the Son of God who loved us and gave himself for us.

We believe that we shall also live with him — the confidence is grounded in the nature of the union itself. You cannot be united to the death without being united to the life; the resurrection is the other side of the cross.

9.synthesis:
Knowing that Christ, having been raised from the dead, is never to die again; death no longer is master over him. The resurrection of Christ is qualitatively different from the resuscitations of Lazarus or the widow's son — those returned to mortal life and died again. Christ's resurrection is a passage into an indestructible life from which death has been permanently expelled.

Calvin stresses the once-for-all character: the death of Christ was a single event in time, now past; the life of the risen Christ is eternal and unrepeatable. The dominion that death once exercised over the incarnate Christ — in the garden, on the cross — has been permanently terminated. Ellicott notes that death no longer is master (θάνατος αὐτοῦ οὐκέτι κυριεύει) uses the same verb as sin's mastery in verse 14 — the parallel is deliberate, connecting death's defeat to sin's defeat. Barnes observes that this fact grounds the assurance of verse 8: the life that Christ now lives is indestructible, and since our life is in him, it partakes of the same indestructibility. Wesley draws the practical conclusion: the resurrection of Christ is not merely a past miracle but the present guarantee of the believer's life in him. Robertson notes that death no longer is master is the decisive declaration of the new order — the old regime of death over Christ has been abolished forever.

voices.excerpt: Death no longer has dominion over him — the resurrection of Christ is not a return to mortal life (as with Lazarus) but a passage into an eternal, indestructible life from which death has been permanently and finally expelled.

Death no longer is master (κυριεύει) — the same verb as 'sin shall not have dominion over you' in v.14. The defeat of death and the defeat of sin are parallel realities in the new order inaugurated by the resurrection.

Christ is never to die again — the resurrection life is qualitatively other than mortal life. This matters for the argument: if Christ's life were mortal, our union with it would offer only temporary security. Since it is eternal, so is our union.

10.synthesis:
For the death that he died, he died to sin once for all; but the life that he lives, he lives to God. The two states of Christ are here defined: the death was to sin — exhausting its claims, satisfying its demands — done once for all (ἐφάπαξ). The life is lived entirely to God — freed from any involvement with sin's sphere.

Calvin notes that once for all is crucial: Paul is guarding against any notion that Christ's death-dealing with sin was incomplete or needs repetition. Ellicott explains he died to sin as meaning Christ died while bearing sin's penalty, in the realm of sin — the cross was his engagement with the entire domain of sin and death on our behalf, a single engagement now forever past. Barnes observes the contrast between the finite past event (he died) and the eternal present state (he lives) — the death was one definitive act; the life is unending. Wesley stresses that he lives to God is the pattern for the believer's new life: freed from sin's sphere, living entirely in the orbit of God. Robertson notes that ἐφάπαξ (once for all) is the same word used in Hebrews 7:27; 9:12; 10:10 for the finality of the atonement.

voices.excerpt: Once for all (ἐφάπαξ) — the finality of the death to sin is absolute. Christ's engagement with sin on the cross was a single, complete, unrepeatable event. It needs no supplement; it admits of no repetition; it accomplished everything.

He died to sin — bearing its penalty, entering its domain, exhausting its claims. This was a single engagement. He lives to God — in the resurrection life, he is entirely in God's sphere, entirely free from any entanglement with sin's world.

Once for all (ἐφάπαξ) — the same decisive word used in Hebrews for the finality of the atonement. Paul uses it to guarantee the completeness of what Christ's death accomplished — and therefore the completeness of what the believer's death-with-Christ accomplished.

11.synthesis:
Even so consider yourselves to be dead to sin, but alive to God in Christ Jesus. The first imperative of the chapter — and significantly, it is not a command to do something but to reckon (λογίζεσθε) something. The believers are to count as true about themselves what is already true in Christ: dead to sin, alive to God.

Calvin explains that this reckoning is not self-deception or wishful thinking but the application of objective truth to subjective experience — believers are to live as if dead to sin because they are dead to sin in Christ. Ellicott notes that λογίζεσθε is the same accounting verb used in chapter 4 for the imputation of righteousness — Paul is using financial metaphor consistently. Barnes observes that the reckoning precedes and grounds the moral exhortation that follows in verses 12-13 — you can only be commanded to not let sin reign once you understand that you are dead to it. Wesley stresses the in Christ Jesus: the death and life Paul speaks of have no existence apart from vital union with Christ. Robertson notes that the present imperative suggests ongoing, habitual reckoning — not a single calculation but a continuous posture of faith.

voices.excerpt: Reckon yourselves — the word is the same as in chapter 4 for imputation. Believers are to count what is true in Christ as true of themselves: dead to sin, alive to God. This reckoning is not pretense but faith grasping the reality of union with Christ.

Λογίζεσθε — present imperative, continuous action: keep reckoning, habitually count this as true about yourselves. The accounting metaphor bridges chapters 4 and 6: what is credited to faith is also credited as the believer's status before God.

Alive to God in Christ Jesus — the positive side of the reckoning. We are not merely dead to sin (a negative) but alive to God (a positive). The new life has a specific orientation: it is directed toward God, lived in his service and fellowship.

12.synthesis:
Therefore do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its lusts. The first direct ethical command follows from the reckoning of verse 11. The believer who reckons themselves dead to sin and alive to God must not allow sin to resume its reign. The word reign (βασιλευέτω) is important: sin is being cast as a would-be usurper attempting to re-establish a dominion that has been legally terminated.

Calvin notes the logic: sin's legal dominion has been broken (chapters 3-6 thus far), but sin remains as a force that can still solicit obedience. The imperative commands resistance to that solicitation. Ellicott distinguishes the two aspects: sin cannot legally reign because the believer is justified and has died to sin; but sin can still try to influence behavior, and the believer must not permit this influence to operate. Barnes observes that mortal body is used deliberately — Paul acknowledges that the body remains subject to mortality and physical temptation while the spirit is already quickened. Wesley stresses that the exhortation is possible — it is not telling believers to do the impossible, but to resist what can be resisted because the power of sin over them has been broken. Robertson notes that the imperative is present — ongoing resistance is commanded, not a single act of resolve.

voices.excerpt: Sin's legal dominion has been broken, but sin still solicits. The command is to refuse that solicitation — not because sin cannot approach, but because its authority to command has been terminated and the believer need not obey.

Do not let sin reign — the present imperative: do not allow it to resume a reign that has been deposed. Sin is cast as a usurper attempting to reclaim a throne from which it has been lawfully removed by the death of Christ.

In your mortal body — the body is still mortal, still subject to temptation, still feeling the pull of the passions. But the believer is not to obey those passions; the body's impulses are not commands now that sin no longer has authority.

13.synthesis:
And do not go on presenting the members of your body to sin as instruments of unrighteousness; but present yourselves to God as those alive from the dead, and your members as instruments of righteousness to God. The practical application becomes concrete: the body's members — hands, eyes, tongue, mind — are weapons (ὅπλα). The question is whose arsenal they serve.

Calvin notes that Paul does not command the mortification of the body itself but its redeployment — the same members that served sin are to be enlisted in the service of righteousness. Ellicott explains the double command: negative (stop presenting members to sin) and positive (present them to God). Barnes observes that the positive is the greater emphasis: as those alive from the dead — the believer's posture is that of one risen, looking back on the old life as definitively past. Wesley stresses the word present (παραστήσατε): a deliberate, conscious act of offering — not a passive yielding but an active and decisive consecration. Robertson notes that the aorist imperative present yourselves suggests a decisive act — a once-and-for-all commitment of the self to God, followed by the continuing practical choices described in verses 12 and 13a.

voices.excerpt: The members are weapons — they can serve one kingdom or the other. Paul commands not the destruction of the members but their transfer: from sin's service to God's. The same tongue that cursed is to bless; the same hands that stole are to give.

Present yourselves to God as those alive from the dead — the posture of the believer is resurrection posture: looking back on the old death as finished, looking forward to a life entirely oriented toward God.

Instruments of righteousness — the Greek word (ὅπλα) means weapons or tools. Paul is thinking militarily: the members of the body are armaments, and the question is which army they serve. The answer: God's army, not sin's.

14.synthesis:
For sin shall not be master over you, for you are not under law but under grace. Paul gives the ground of assurance for the commands of verses 12-13: the very reason the commands are obeyed-able is that the believer is under a new regime. The old regime (law) activated the sinful impulse; the new regime (grace) provides both the will and the power to resist.

Calvin explains that being under law means being in the regime where law specifies what is required but provides no power to fulfill it, and where every failure is a legally-chargeable transgression. Being under grace means being in the regime where the law's demands have been met by Christ, its condemnation absorbed, and the Spirit given to enable new obedience. Ellicott notes that the promise sin shall not be master is given in the future tense as a certainty, not a possibility — it is grounded in the nature of the new regime, not in the believer's moral effort. Barnes observes that the power of sin is connected to the law: the law provokes transgression (5:20; 7:7-11) and makes sin formally chargeable. Under grace, the legal dominion of sin is broken. Wesley stresses that this does not mean the believer under grace is lawless — it means they are freed from the law as a system of condemnation and enabled to fulfill its intent.

voices.excerpt: Under law — the regime where sin's dominion is strongest, because the law specifies what is forbidden and provides no power to refrain, while making every failure a formal charge. Under grace — the regime where sin's dominion is broken and the Spirit enables what the law demanded.

Sin shall not be master over you — a promise, not a command. The future indicative expresses certainty: this will not happen to those who are under grace, because the power of the new regime is greater than the power of sin in the old.

Not under law but under grace — the law intensifies sin's power by specifying it and making it legally chargeable. Grace removes both the condemnation and, through the Spirit, the dominance. The believer is free from both the guilt and the mastery of sin.

15.synthesis:
The second antinomian objection: What then? Shall we sin because we are not under law but under grace? This is a subtly different objection from verse 1. Verse 1 asked whether we should sin so that grace increases (abusing 5:20); verse 15 asks whether being free from law's jurisdiction gives license to sin. Both receive the same horrified denial: By no means!

Calvin notes that Paul's response here shifts from the ontological argument of verses 2-11 (you have died to sin) to the relational argument of verses 16-23 (you are now someone's slave — whose?). Ellicott observes that the objection is a real one: if we are not under law, does that mean sin is irrelevant? Paul's answer is that freedom from law is not freedom from God; it is transfer from one master to another. Barnes explains that the grace that frees from the law's condemnation does not free from moral obligation — it creates a new and deeper moral obligation of love and gratitude. Wesley stresses that the question itself exposes the misunderstanding: grace is not permissive indulgence but a new ownership, and the new owner is God himself.

voices.excerpt: A second objection, subtler than the first: if we are not under law, is there no prohibition on sin? Paul's answer moves from the ontological (you are dead to sin) to the relational (you belong to a new master who requires righteousness).

By no means! — the identical response to v. 1. The repetition signals that both objections arise from the same misunderstanding: that grace, by removing law's condemnation, also removes any obligation to righteousness. Paul denies this with equal force.

Freedom from the law is not freedom from God. The believer freed from condemnation is not freed from obligation — the obligation has deepened, because it now rests not on legal compulsion but on love, gratitude, and new ownership.

16.synthesis:
Do you not know that when you present yourselves to someone as slaves for obedience, you are slaves of the one whom you obey, either of sin resulting in death, or of obedience resulting in righteousness? Paul introduces the slavery metaphor that will govern verses 16-23. The argument is pragmatic: whoever you obey, you have effectively made your master. There is no neutral ground.

Calvin notes that Paul is making a logical and experiential point: moral obedience creates a relationship of ownership. If you obey sin, sin is your master and its wage is death. If you obey God, God is your master and the outcome is righteousness. Ellicott observes that Paul does not say sin or law but sin or obedience — the alternative to slavery to sin is slavery to obedience itself, which is then defined as slavery to God. Barnes stresses the practical force: the question of which master you serve is answered empirically — what do you actually obey? Wesley notes that both forms of slavery are real: sin is a real master who pays real wages (death); God is a real master whose service produces real righteousness. Robertson observes that Paul is not using the metaphor to demean believers but to make the ownership relation vivid — and then immediately (v. 17) to celebrate what the new ownership means.

voices.excerpt: Whoever you obey owns you — this is the logic of moral slavery. There is no neutral free agency; obedience constitutes servitude. The only question is which master's service you are in, and the two available masters lead to opposite destinations.

Sin resulting in death; obedience resulting in righteousness — the outcomes define the masters. Sin's wages are always death; obedience's fruit is always righteousness. You can know which master you serve by what you are producing.

The slavery metaphor is not demeaning but clarifying: it makes the ownership relation plain and eliminates the illusion of neutral autonomy. Every human being is serving some master; the gospel changes which master, not whether one exists.

17.synthesis:
But thanks be to God that though you were slaves of sin, you became obedient from the heart to that form of teaching to which you were committed. Paul breaks into thanksgiving — this verse is a doxology embedded in a theological argument. The Roman believers were formerly slaves of sin, but they have become obedient. The obedience is qualified: from the heart, not merely external compliance.

Calvin notes the phrase form of teaching (τύπον διδαχῆς): not an abstract system but a specific pattern of instruction — the apostolic gospel delivered to the Roman church, into which they were poured like molten metal into a mold. Ellicott explains the metaphor: the gospel mold shapes the believer's character, so that their obedience takes the shape of the teaching. Barnes observes that from the heart is the distinguishing mark of genuine conversion: not outward compliance under compulsion but inward transformation producing genuine willingness. Wesley stresses that this thanksgiving is proof that the slavery to obedience Paul describes is not oppressive but is the natural outflow of a heart that has been changed by the Spirit. Robertson notes the unusual construction you were committed to the form of teaching (rather than the teaching being committed to you) — Paul is saying the believers were delivered over to the teaching as material to be shaped.

voices.excerpt: You were delivered into the mold of teaching — the metaphor is of molten metal poured into a cast. The gospel is the form; the believer is the material. As the metal takes the shape of the mold, so the heart takes the shape of the gospel's pattern.

Obedient from the heart — not the obedience of fear or compulsion but of genuine inward transformation. This is the mark that distinguishes slavery to God from slavery to sin: the former is willing, joyful, and transformative; the latter is coerced and destructive.

Thanks be to God — Paul cannot describe the Romans' liberation from sin without breaking into doxology. The transfer from sin's service to God's is entirely a work of grace, and thanksgiving is the only appropriate response to a gift of this magnitude.

18.synthesis:
And having been freed from sin, you became slaves of righteousness. The liberation from sin is not liberation into independence but into a new servitude. Paul does not present the Christian life as autonomous self-determination but as a transfer of ownership: freed from sin, bound to righteousness.

Calvin notes that this is a foundational principle: human beings are not designed for autonomy but for service. The question is not whether to serve but whom. Freed from sin, the believer is immediately enrolled in the service of righteousness — not by compulsion but by the nature of what grace has done. Ellicott observes that freed from sin and slaves of righteousness are simultaneous events, not sequential — the liberation is into servitude, not into a neutral interval. Barnes stresses that this servitude to righteousness is the opposite of oppressive: it is the fulfillment of human nature as God designed it, the service that brings life rather than death. Wesley draws the practical implication: the believer who understands this will not experience the commands of righteousness as burdensome constraints but as the proper expression of their new nature. Robertson notes the divine passive having been freed — the liberation was God's act, not the believer's self-emancipation.

voices.excerpt: Freed from sin, enslaved to righteousness — Paul does not promise autonomous freedom. Human beings are made to serve; the gospel does not eliminate servitude but transfers us from a master who destroys to a master who gives life.

The two events are simultaneous: the moment of liberation from sin is the moment of enrollment in righteousness's service. There is no neutral ground between the two kingdoms; the exit from one is the entrance to the other.

Slaves of righteousness — the word is strong, but the service it describes is the fulfillment of human dignity, not its degradation. To serve righteousness is to serve God; and to serve God is the end for which humanity was created.

19.synthesis:
I am speaking in human terms because of the weakness of your flesh. For just as you presented your members as slaves to impurity and to lawlessness, resulting in further lawlessness, so now present your members as slaves to righteousness, resulting in sanctification. Paul apologizes for the slavery metaphor — it is a concession to human understanding, not the ideal description of the Christian's relationship to God.

Calvin notes that the relationship of the believer to God is more accurately described as adoption and love than slavery, but the slavery image is pedagogically useful precisely because the Romans understood it. Ellicott observes that the parallel is exact: just as they formerly presented members to impurity (which deepened impurity), so they are to present members to righteousness (which produces sanctification). The progression is important — sin produces more sin (a downward spiral); righteousness produces sanctification (an upward transformation). Barnes stresses the deliberate symmetry: Paul uses the former life as the exact template for the new life, asking only that the same energy and completeness of service be redirected to God. Wesley sees in this the principle of sanctification: progressive growth in holiness through the deliberate, repeated offering of all the members to God's service.

voices.excerpt: I speak in human terms — the slavery metaphor is a pedagogical concession. The true relationship of the believer to God is adoption and love, not servitude; but the concrete image of slavery helps those who have experienced both regimes to grasp the transfer.

Resulting in further lawlessness; resulting in sanctification — the two progressions are the key. Sin, when fed, deepens; righteousness, when practiced, grows. The spirals move in opposite directions, and the direction is set by the initial offering.

Just as you presented your members as slaves to impurity — the same energy, the same wholehearted presentation is now to be directed to righteousness. Paul asks not for more than they gave sin, but for the same completeness directed to God.

20.synthesis:
For when you were slaves of sin, you were free in regard to righteousness. A sardonic observation: in the former life, righteousness had no claim on them — they were genuinely free from its demands (in the sense of having no connection to it, no allegiance to it). The freedom they had was freedom from righteousness, which is another way of describing their total slavery to sin.

Calvin notes the biting irony: the only freedom you had was freedom from the very thing that would have given life. You were completely unrestricted in sin and completely uninvolved in righteousness. Ellicott explains that free from righteousness does not mean righteous freedom but the absence of righteousness's claim — sin monopolized the entire life, leaving no room for any obligation to the good. Barnes observes that this clarifies why the freedom-language is insufficient: the old freedom was not liberty but bondage — the freedom of a slave who is only free from his master's enemy. Wesley draws the practical diagnostic: if anyone wishes to know whether they are in the old regime or the new, they should ask what they are free from and what they are enslaved to. Robertson notes that Paul is completing a symmetry: as believers are now free from sin (v. 22), the unconverted were free from righteousness — the two freedoms define the two regimes.

voices.excerpt: Free in regard to righteousness — a devastating irony. Your great freedom was freedom from the only thing that could have given you life. You were completely unrestricted in sin and completely alienated from righteousness.

Free from righteousness — not morally free but simply disconnected from it; righteousness had no purchase on you. Sin had monopolized your service entirely, leaving no allegiance available for righteousness to claim.

The symmetry is exact: formerly free from righteousness, enslaved to sin; now free from sin, enslaved to righteousness. Paul is making clear that the change of regime is total — there is no partial transfer, no foot in both kingdoms.

21.synthesis:
Therefore what fruit were you having at that time from the things of which you are now ashamed? For the end of those things is death. Paul asks the converts to assess the former life's productivity: what did sin produce? The answer is shame — they are now ashamed of the things that once seemed desirable. And beyond shame, there is a further assessment: the end (τέλος) of those things is death.

Calvin notes the double verdict: shame now (subjective recognition of the worthlessness and degradation of the former life) and death at the last (objective outcome). The shame is evidence of genuine conversion — you cannot be ashamed of what you still value. Ellicott observes that the fruit question exposes the deception of sin: it promised something desirable but produced only what causes shame when seen clearly. Barnes stresses that end here means final destination or outcome, not termination — the direction of sin's travel was always toward death, even when it was not visible. Wesley sees in the shame itself a grace: it is the Spirit's testimony to the conscience that what was done was wrong, and that proof is evidence of the new nature. Robertson notes that the two verdicts — shame and death — are both necessary: shame alone might produce mere regret; death alone might produce fear; together they produce repentance and the flight from sin.

voices.excerpt: What fruit? The answer is shame — which proves that conversion has occurred. You cannot be ashamed of what you still cherish. The shame the converts feel toward their former sins is the Spirit's testimony that they now belong to a different kingdom.

The end of those things is death — not merely that death terminates the sinful life, but that death was always the destination toward which sin was carrying its servants. Sin is a road with a known terminus; the converts have turned off that road.

For the end of those things is death — the final outcome, the direction of travel, the wages ultimately paid. Sin seemed to promise life and pleasure; it was transporting its servants toward death all the while. The gospel reveals what the former life was actually doing.

22.synthesis:
But now having been freed from sin and enslaved to God, you have your fruit resulting in sanctification, and the outcome, eternal life. The positive counterpart to verse 21. The question what fruit? is now answered from the new life's perspective: the fruit is sanctification (ἁγιασμόν), and the outcome (τέλος) is eternal life.

Calvin notes the contrast at every point: formerly slaves of sin, now freed; formerly free from righteousness, now enslaved to God; formerly producing shame, now producing sanctification; formerly heading to death, now heading to eternal life. The contrast is total and the change is comprehensive. Ellicott observes that sanctification here is both the fruit in process (progressive growth in holiness) and the character of the end (eternal life as the perfect holiness of the new creation). Barnes stresses that sanctification is described as fruit — it is the natural product of the new life, not an additional achievement. Wesley sees here the great summary of what grace has done: liberation, ownership, productivity, and destination — all four dimensions of the Christian life are transformed. Robertson notes the parallel structure with verse 21: the two verses form a complete contrast, the old life and the new life mapped against each other in every dimension.

voices.excerpt: Freed from sin, enslaved to God — the liberation is not into autonomy but into a different and better ownership. And this new ownership produces sanctification as naturally as a good vine produces grapes: it is fruit, not effort.

The outcome, eternal life — τέλος in both verses: sin's τέλος is death; obedience's τέλος is eternal life. The destination has changed, and the change begins now in sanctification and is completed in glory.

Fruit resulting in sanctification — holiness is not the condition of salvation but the fruit of it; not what earns eternal life but what is produced by the life already given. The new ownership creates what the old regime could never manufacture.

23.synthesis:
For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord. The chapter closes with one of the most memorable antitheses in the New Testament.
