Clarke's paraphrase is evocative: the person who draws joy from the hope of future glory, who endures present suffering remembering it is for Christ's sake, and who prays persistently — this is the profile of the Christian formed by the gospel Paul has been expounding.

voices.html: Three things are connected here and seem to belong together: the person who is sustained by hope will be able to endure tribulation, and the one who endures will naturally persist in prayer. Hope enables patience; patience feeds prayer; prayer renews hope. The three form a single spiritual economy, each sustaining the others.

"Patient in tribulation" — this virtue was specially needed in the troublous times through which the Church was passing. The same connection between affliction and patience appears in Romans 5:3-5. Christian patience is not passive endurance but an active, hope-sustained commitment to hold one's ground in suffering.

The rejoicing comes first — in hope — and the hope is what makes patience possible. The two are not independent but mutually reinforcing: the hope produces the joy that enables the patience, and the patience exercised in tribulation strengthens the hope. Prayer sustains both by maintaining the connection to the God in whom the hope rests.

13.synthesis: "Contributing to the needs of the saints; pursuing hospitality." Two more marks of love: generosity toward the broader community of believers and active hospitality toward strangers. The verb κοινωνοῦντες (contributing, sharing) implies active participation in the needs of others — not merely occasional giving but ongoing solidarity. Wesley connects this to the larger argument: communicating to the saints, whether Jews or Gentiles, relates back to the appeals of chapters 9-11 for unity between these two parts of the one people of God. Calvin notes the unusual word for hospitality: φιλοξενίαν (love of strangers, from ξένος, stranger) — not merely tolerating guests but actively pursuing them. Ellicott adds that hospitality was specially prized in the early church because the infrastructure for traveling believers was underdeveloped, and opening one's home to fellow Christians on journey was a concrete act of solidarity. Barnes: the word "pursuing" (διώκοντες) — the same word used for pursuing righteousness — suggests that hospitality requires active effort, not passive willingness; it must be hunted down as an opportunity.

voices.html: Hospitality was specially prized in the early church because travelling Christians had little infrastructure — inns were associated with immorality, and opening one's home was a concrete act of solidarity and care. "Pursuing" (διώκοντες) — the verb implies active effort, not merely passive welcome when occasions arise.

The unusual word for hospitality: φιλοξενίαν — love of strangers (from ξένος, stranger). Paul calls for not merely tolerating guests but actively pursuing them as opportunities for love. The active pursuit transforms hospitality from a social grace into a form of ministry.

"Pursuing" (διώκοντες) — the same word used elsewhere for pursuing righteousness — suggests that hospitality requires active effort. It must be hunted down as an opportunity, not waited for as an occasion. The Christian who only opens their door when asked has not yet learned the love Paul describes here.

14.synthesis: "Bless those who persecute you; bless and do not curse." RWP identifies this as "like Matthew 5:44 in spirit, not a quotation, but a reminiscence of the words of Jesus" — Luke 6:28 is even closer in wording. Paul here brings the teaching of Jesus into his ethical instruction without formal citation, suggesting the command was so well known it needed only allusion. Ellicott notes the historical context: it was probably around the time Paul wrote this letter that the compositions underlying our Gospels were beginning to circulate — the words of Jesus were alive in the oral tradition even before the written Gospels. Barnes calls this one of the most severe and difficult duties of Christianity: to bless only, however long or aggravated the injury. Clarke is specific about the linguistic content: εὐλογεῖτε (bless) means to give good words; καταρᾶσθε (curse) means to utter curses — Paul commands the first and forbids the second unconditionally, whatever the provocation. Wesley simply notes: "Bless them, pray for them, and on no account curse them, whatever the provocation may be. Have the loving, forgiving mind that was in Christ."

voices.html: Like Matt 5:44 in spirit — not a quotation, but a reminiscence of the words of Jesus. "And curse not" (καὶ μὴ καταρᾶσθε). Present middle imperative with μή. The negative addition gives emphasis — the command is not merely to bless but explicitly and actively not to curse, however naturally the latter might arise from provocation.

Bless, and curse not — bless only; or continue to bless, however long or aggravated the injury. Do not be provoked to anger or to cursing by any injury, persecution, or reviling. This is one of the most severe and difficult duties of Christianity — and one of the clearest marks of a mind transformed by the mercy of God.

Εὐλογεῖτε — give good words; pray for them. Καταρᾶσθε — utter curses against them. Paul commands the first and forbids the second unconditionally, whatever the provocation. The contrast is not between positive and neutral response, but between active blessing and active cursing — only the first is permitted.

15.synthesis: "Rejoice with those who rejoice; weep with those who weep." One of the simplest and most searching of Paul's practical commands. No theological apparatus is required — the command is the direct implication of genuine love (v. 9) and the body metaphor (vv. 4-5). Calvin in the broader context notes this belongs to the network of duties toward those within the community. Ellicott emphasizes the order: Paul names rejoicing first and weeping second — perhaps because rejoicing with another's joy requires more self-transcendence than sympathizing with their sorrow, which comes more naturally to fallen human beings. Barnes observes that the command covers both extremes of human experience: there is to be no envy when another prospers and no coldness when another suffers. RWP: both participles are present tense, indicating ongoing responsive dispositions rather than one-time acts. Clarke brings the Pauline warmth: "Show that you feel for them whatever they feel." Wesley connects it directly to the prior command about persecutors: even enemies are to be loved — how much more those within the community of faith.

voices.html: Paul names rejoicing first — perhaps because rejoicing with another's joy requires more self-transcendence than weeping with their sorrow. It is harder to be genuinely glad at another's blessing than to be sorrowful at another's grief, since the first requires overcoming envy and the second only requires compassion.

The command covers both extremes of human experience: no envy when another prospers, no coldness when another suffers. The two prohibitions are not symmetric in difficulty — rejoicing with the prosperous is rarer than weeping with the bereaved, which is why Paul names it first. Both require the genuine love commanded in v. 9.

Show that you feel for them whatever they feel. Enter into their circumstances, share their joys and sorrows as if they were your own — because in the body of Christ, they are. The member that suffers makes all suffer; the member that is honored makes all rejoice (cf. 1 Cor 12:26).

16.synthesis: "Be of the same mind toward one another. Do not be proud but associate with the lowly. Do not be wise in your own estimation." Three interlocking commands against the pride that fragments communities. The first (τὸ αὐτὸ εἰς ἀλλήλους φρονοῦντες) calls for mutual harmony — not uniformity of opinion but unity of spirit and orientation. The second (μὴ τὰ ὑψηλὰ φρονοῦντες) — "not thinking high things" — is the concrete application of the humility of v. 3. Ellicott: "In every Christian community there should be that harmony which proceeds from a common object, common hopes, common desires." RWP adds: "set not your mind on high things" (μὴ τὰ ὑψηλὰ φρονοῦντες) — not pursuing positions, persons, or pleasures associated with social elevation. The third command — "do not be wise in your own sight" — echoes Proverbs 3:7 ("Do not be wise in your own eyes"). Calvin reads the first clause as calling believers to feel alike toward each other, with no variation based on social status. Clarke adds the pastoral color: "Do not court the rich nor the powerful; do not pass by the poor man to pay your respects to the great."

voices.html: In every Christian community there should be the harmony that proceeds from a common object, common hopes, and common desires. "Set not your mind on high things" — do not be drawn toward the socially elevated at the expense of the lowly. Condescend to men of low estate — let yourselves be carried along by the stream of humble service.

The first clause calls believers to feel alike toward one another — with no variation in warmth or respect based on social status. The mutual regard of Christians must not be calibrated to worldly rank. The body's unity requires that each member is as honored in the community's regard as their place in Christ demands, regardless of their place in society.

Be not ambitious; affect nothing above your station. Do not court the rich nor the powerful; do not pass by the poor man to pay your respects to the great. The church that calibrates its warmth to the social rank of its members has ceased to be the body of Christ and become merely another social institution.

17.synthesis: "Repay no one evil for evil. Have regard for good things in the sight of all men." The prohibition of retaliation and the positive command of transparent integrity. RWP notes that the prohibition is "directly opposite to the law of retaliation of the Pharisees as in Matthew 5:39; 1 Thessalonians 5:15; 1 Corinthians 13:5." The positive command — "take thought beforehand" (προνοουμένοι) — calls for premeditated integrity: not merely avoiding dishonest acts but planning in advance to be seen as honest before all men. Ellicott reads the second clause as a call to avoid enterprises of a doubtful character that might bring the entire Christian community into ill repute (cf. Matthew 5:14-16; 2 Corinthians 8:21). Barnes notes that this is probably one of the most difficult precepts of Christianity: the natural human instinct runs so strongly toward retaliation that the non-retaliating disposition requires a sustained work of grace. Clarke adds the specific target: "Do not be litigious. Beware of too nice a sense of your own honor; intolerable pride is at the bottom of this." Calvin reads the second clause as providing good faith in advance — arranging what is honorable so that no one can bring a charge of dishonesty.

voices.html: Render to no man (μηδεὶν ἀποδιδόντες). "Giving back to no man." Independent participle again. Evil for evil (κακὸν ἀντὶ κακοῦ) — directly opposite to the law of retaliation (Matt 5:39; 1 Thess 5:15). Take thought of (προνοούμενοι) — taking thought beforehand. Old word in N.T. only here and 2 Cor 8:21; Phil 4:8.

"Provide things honest in the sight of all men" — let your purposes be such that all men shall recognise their complete integrity. Do not engage in enterprises of a doubtful character that might bring not only yourselves but the entire Christian body into ill repute. The reputation of the whole community is at stake in each member's conduct.

Probably one of the most difficult precepts of Christianity: the natural instinct toward retaliation runs so strongly that the non-retaliating disposition requires sustained grace. The positive command of premeditated integrity — planning in advance to be seen as honest before all — transforms the prohibition into a program of transparent living.

18.synthesis: "If possible, so far as it depends on you, be at peace with all men." The two qualifications — "if possible" (εἰ δυνατόν) and "so far as it depends on you" (τὸ ἐξ ὑμῶν) — are honest acknowledgments that peace is not always achievable from one side alone. Ellicott notes these qualifications are Paul's realism: peace requires two parties, and the Christian can only control their own side of the relationship. Barnes specifies the scope: the duty of peaceableness is universal — toward all people, including those outside the community of faith. The qualifications do not reduce the obligation but define its reach: one cannot force peace on a party determined to be hostile, but one can refuse to contribute to hostility from one's own side. Calvin reads the qualifications as Paul's pastoral concession: there are situations in which maintaining peace would require compromising truth or righteousness — in such cases the obligation to peace yields. Wesley is simply practical: "As much as in you lies" — do your part; leave the rest to God and the other party.

voices.html: "If possible" and "so far as it depends on you" — both qualifications acknowledge that peace requires two parties and the Christian can only control their own side. The Christian's obligation is to exhaust every possibility of peace from their own side — the other party's response lies outside the Christian's control and therefore outside the Christian's obligation.

The qualifications are Paul's pastoral concession: there are situations in which maintaining peace would require compromising truth or righteousness. In such cases the obligation to peace yields. But these cases are the exception — the general rule stands, and its demands are extensive: be at peace with all people so far as it is in your power.

The duty of peaceableness is universal — toward all people, including those outside the faith community. The qualifications define the scope without reducing the obligation: one cannot force peace on a party determined to be hostile, but one can refuse to contribute to hostility from one's own side. That refusal is what the verse commands.

19.synthesis: "Beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave room for the wrath of God, for it is written, 'Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord.'" The opening address "beloved" (ἀγαπητοί) both softens and intensifies the command: it reminds the reader that they are loved by the one who commands them to forgo revenge. Ellicott argues that "the wrath" (τῇ ὀργῇ — note the article) refers to God's wrath, not a general anger: "Stand aside as a mere spectator, and let the wrath of God have free course to accomplish itself as He shall think well." RWP confirms: the article points to the wrath of God — Deut 32:35. Calvin's reading: the evil of revenge is more grievous than the kindness-withholding of v. 17, both arising from inordinate self-love. Barnes specifies what is being prohibited: to avenge is to take satisfaction for an injury by inflicting punishment — this prerogative belongs exclusively to God and to the civil magistrate acting in God's name (cf. 13:4). Wesley adds the nuance: "Perhaps it might more properly be rendered, 'leave room for wrath' — that is, the wrath of God, to whom vengeance properly belongs." The quotation from Deuteronomy 32:35 grounds the command in God's own assertion of his exclusive right to repay.

voices.html: "Give place unto wrath" — best understood as "the wrath of God" (the Greek article points to something definite). Stand aside yourself as a mere spectator and let the wrath of God have free course to accomplish itself as he shall think well. The alternative to personal revenge is not passivity but trust in the God who has reserved vengeance to himself.

But give place unto wrath (ἀλλὰ δότε τόπον τῇ ὀργῇ). Second aorist active imperative of δίδωμι. "Give room for the (note article as pointing to God's) wrath" — Deut 32:35. For it is written — the quotation grounds the command in God's own declaration of his exclusive right to repay.

To avenge is to take satisfaction for an injury by inflicting punishment — this prerogative belongs exclusively to God and to the civil magistrate acting in God's name (cf. 13:4). The prohibition leaves no gap: there is no legitimate sphere for personal revenge. The one who refrains from revenge is not leaving the wrong unpunished — they are committing it to the one who sees perfectly and judges perfectly.

20.synthesis: "To the contrary, 'if your enemy is hungry, feed him; if he is thirsty, give him something to drink; for by so doing you will heap burning coals on his head.'" (Proverbs 25:21-22) The active good to be done to the enemy is drawn from Israel's own wisdom tradition. The phrase "heap coals of fire" has generated debate. Ellicott reads it as taking the best and most summary "vengeance" upon the enemy — but a strictly metaphorical and redemptive vengeance: "awakening in him a burning sense of shame and contrition." Wesley uses a poetic illustration: like the artist who melts sullen lead by heaping coals on it, so acts of kindness melt the enemy's resistance. RWP identifies the quotation from Proverbs 25:21-22 (LXX). Barnes explains the two main readings: (a) the coals are the burning shame the enemy feels when treated with kindness they don't deserve; (b) the coals are the divine fire that judges them if they persist in hostility. Calvin reads the verse in terms of "indirect retaliation" overcome: when we do good to those who have done wrong to us, we show the invincible firmness of our mind. Most commentators favor the "shame-into-repentance" reading as more consistent with the tone of vv. 9-21.

voices.html: "Heap coals of fire" — in a strictly metaphorical sense: "Thou shalt take the best and most summary vengeance upon him" by awakening in him a burning sense of shame and contrition. There may be the underlying idea of melting resistance — as fire melts metal, so persistent kindness melts hostility into repentance.

"Heap coals of fire upon his head — that part which is most sensible." A poetic image: as artists melt the sullen ore of lead by heaping coals of fire upon it, so persistent kindness melts the enemy's resistance. Feed him, give him drink, do good — and the very acts of love become the instrument of transformation.

Two main readings: (a) the coals are the burning shame the enemy feels when treated with kindness they don't deserve — this shame may lead to repentance; (b) the coals are the divine fire that judges them if they persist in hostility. Most commentators favor (a) as more consistent with the chapter's tone of active love toward enemies and the call to overcome evil with good.

20.key_tension: Commentators divide between reading "coals of fire" as the shame-leading-to-repentance that enemy-kindness produces (Ellicott, Wesley — the melting-into-repentance reading) and the divine judgment that accumulates on a persisting enemy (Barnes notes this second option). The first reading is more consistent with the context of active love; the second fits the Deuteronomy 32 backdrop of divine vengeance in v. 19.

21.synthesis: "Do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good." The capstone sentence of chapter 12 and of the entire practical section on enemy love. Calvin reads it as confirmation: in this contest with perverseness, if we try to retaliate it, we confess that we are overcome by it; if we return good for evil, we show the invincible firmness of our minds. RWP supplies the Latin aphorism: "Vincit malos pertinax bonitas — persistent goodness conquers the wicked." The verbs are present tense: stop being conquered by evil (μὴ νικῶ ὑπὸ τοῦ κακοῦ) and keep on conquering the evil in the good (ἀλλὰ νίκα ἐν τῷ ἀγαθῷ τὸ κακόν). Wesley is brief and practical: "And if you see no present fruit, yet persevere." Barnes draws the principle into focus: the Christian who retaliates has allowed evil to dictate the terms of the encounter; the Christian who returns good has established the terms of the encounter on the gospel's own ground. Ellicott: "The infliction of vengeance is not a sign of strength, but of weakness. To repress the desire for revenge is to gain a victory over self, which is nobler in itself and more effectual in result."

voices.html: Be not overcome of evil (μὴ νικῶ ὑπὸ τοῦ κακοῦ). Present passive imperative — "stop being conquered by the evil." But overcome evil with good (ἀλλὰ νίκα ἐν τῷ ἀγαθῷ τὸ κακόν) — keep on conquering the evil in the good. Vincit malos pertinax bonitas — persistent goodness conquers the wicked.

This sentence is laid down as a confirmation: our contest is altogether with perverseness. If we try to retaliate it, we confess that we are overcome by it. If we return good for evil, by that very deed we show the invincible firmness of our mind. True conquest in this conflict is not suppression of the enemy but transformation of the terms of battle.

The infliction of vengeance is not a sign of strength but of weakness. To repress the desire for revenge is to gain a victory over self, which is not only nobler in itself but also much more effectual. It will disarm the enemy and may win them over — vengeance tends to harden, while persistent goodness tends to melt.

1.synthesis: "Let every soul be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God." Paul now turns to the duty Christians owe to civil government. RWP notes the Hebraism: πᾶσα ψυχή (every soul) = every person. The governing authorities (ἐξουσίαις ὑπερεχούσαις) are described as God-ordained: the theological grounding is not the goodness of any particular government but the sovereignty of God who orders human institutions. Wesley notes the verse is "an admonition peculiarly needful for the Jews" — who had strong traditions of resistance to Gentile rule. Calvin explains the two levels of authority: God as the source of all authority, and earthly magistrates as its secondary holders. Barnes frames the difficulty: this is an extremely important and difficult subject, and one might be surprised to find such political teaching in a doctrinal letter — but Paul's point is precisely that doctrinal truth and social responsibility are not separable. Ellicott notes that Paul, writing to the church in the capital of the empire, had particular reason to address civil obedience explicitly — and that the passage also served, in effect, as a public apology for the Christian religion.

voices.html: Every soul (πᾶσα ψυχή) — a Hebraism for every person (as in Acts 2:43). To the higher powers (ἐξουσίαις ὑπερεχούσαις) — abstract for concrete. "Except by God" (εἰ μὴ ὑπὸ θεοῦ) — the divine appointment of civil authority is the ground of the obligation to submit to it, not the character of the rulers themselves.

Let every soul be subject to the supreme powers — an admonition peculiarly needful for the Jews, who carried strong traditions of resistance to Gentile rule. Power, in the singular, is used here for those who exercise authority — the person or institution through which the authority is channeled. The source of all authority is God; the secondary holders are accountable to him.

Paul discusses the duty Christians owe to civil government — an important and difficult subject. The doctrinal teaching of the epistle is not separable from social responsibility. Christianity is not designed to break in upon the proper order of society but to confirm it, while transforming its members from within. The obligation is grounded in God's own institution of civil authority, not in any claim about the ruler's personal virtue.

2.synthesis: "Therefore whoever resists the authority resists what God has appointed, and those who resist will incur judgment." The logic follows from v. 1: if governing authorities are God-ordained, resistance to them is resistance to God's ordering of human affairs. Calvin in the broader context distinguishes two types of resistance: the ordinary subject's resistance to a superior's commands (which is forbidden here) and the lesser magistrate's corporate check on a tyrant (which is discussed in the Institutes but not in this passage). Barnes notes the dual reference of "judgment" (κρίμα): the civil punishment of the earthly authority and, possibly, the divine judgment that falls on those who disorder the social fabric God has ordained. Ellicott adds the qualification that emerges from the entire NT: the authority of the state is always relative to God's authority; where the state commands what God forbids (as in Acts 5:29), obedience to God takes precedence. RWP is succinct: the one who resists (ἀντιτασσόμενος) has ranged themselves against (ἀντί) the arrangement (τάσσω) of God. Clarke supplies the pastoral nuance: even a bad government is better than anarchy, which is why resistance must be exceptional and principled, not casual.

voices.html: The authority of the state is always relative to God's authority — where the state commands what God forbids, obedience to God takes precedence (Acts 5:29). But Paul's concern here is not with the exceptional case but with the general pattern: the Christian is to be known as a law-abiding member of the community, not as a habitual resister of social order.

"Judgment" (κρίμα) — both the civil punishment of the earthly authority and the divine judgment on those who disorder the social fabric God has ordained. The two judgments reinforce each other in the normal case: God's design for social order uses the civil authority's discipline as one of its instruments.

Even a bad government is better than anarchy — which is why resistance must be exceptional and principled, not casual. The person who resists the civil authority has taken upon themselves the role that belongs to God: the ordering of human affairs. That presumption, even when directed against a genuine injustice, carries its own grave dangers.

3.synthesis: "For rulers are not a terror to good works, but to evil. Do you want to have no fear of authority? Do what is good and you will have praise from it." Paul describes the proper function of civil government — not as it is in every instance, but as it is designed to be. Ellicott notes the idealized character of this description: Paul is describing the purpose and function of civil authority, not making an empirical claim about every ruler. Calvin in the broader commentary explains that magistrates may learn their vocation from this verse: they are not to rule for their own interest but for the public good; they are not endued with unbridled power but with power restricted to the wellbeing of their subjects. Barnes adds that Paul's instruction is prudential as well as theological: the law-abiding Christian has nothing to fear from civil authority in the normal course of events — it is those who do evil who fear the sword. Wesley simply identifies the authority's intent: to maintain the peace and order of society, rewarding good conduct and punishing evil. Clarke notes that "praise" (ἔπαινον) from the civil authority takes the form of protection and security — the reward for lawful living is not commendation but the enjoyment of the goods that civil order provides.

voices.html: Paul is describing the purpose and function of civil authority, not making an empirical claim about every ruler. Rulers are instituted as a terror to evil and an encouragement to good — that is their design. Where a particular ruler inverts this design, the principle is not invalidated; rather, that ruler has failed the standard by which all magistrates are assessed.

Magistrates may learn their vocation from this verse: they are not to rule for their own interest but for the public good; they are not endued with unbridled power but with power restricted to the wellbeing of their subjects. In short, they are responsible to God and to men in the exercise of their power. The authority is real; the accountability is equally real.

Paul's instruction is prudential as well as theological: the law-abiding person has nothing to fear from civil authority in the normal course — it is those who do evil who fear the sword. The Christian who does good can expect the authority's protection; the one who does evil can expect its punishment. This functional description motivates compliance without depending on the virtue of any particular ruler.

4.synthesis: "For it is God's servant for your good. But if you do wrong, be afraid, for he does not bear the sword in vain. For he is the servant of God, an avenger who carries out God's wrath on the wrongdoer." The magistrate is twice called "God's servant" (θεοῦ διάκονος) — in the general sense of one appointed by God to a role, not implying personal piety. RWP: "Of course even Nero was God's minister 'to thee for good.' That is the ideal, the goal." The sword (μάχαιραν) is not the dagger worn as ornament but the instrument of genuine authority — Ellicott notes that "to bear the sword" was a recognized phrase for the power of life and death in magistracy. Calvin draws the theological point: the authority to execute capital punishment derives from God himself, and this is how God's wrath against wickedness is administered through human institutions. Barnes specifies that this verse has been foundational to Christian thinking about capital punishment: the civil authority wields the sword as an avenger of God's wrath, not as an agent of private revenge. Wesley is concise: "The sword — the instrument of capital punishment, which God authorizes him to inflict."

voices.html: A minister of God (θεοῦ διάκονος). General sense of διάκονος. Of course even Nero was God's minister "to thee for good" — that is the ideal, the goal. Beareth (φορεῖ) — present active indicative of φορέω, old frequentative form of φέρω. The sword is not worn as ornament but borne as functional instrument of authority.

"The sword" — not the dagger worn by Roman emperors, but the actual sword in the strict sense. "To bear the sword" was a recognized Greek phrase expressing the power of the magistrates. It was carried before them in processions and on important occasions. The authority to use it is derived from God; its exercise is legitimate when directed against genuine wrongdoing.

The authority to execute capital punishment derives from God himself — this is how God's wrath against wickedness is administered through human institutions. The magistrate who punishes the murderer is not acting on private impulse but as the agent of God's own retributive justice. The theology of civil punishment is inseparable from the theology of divine justice.

5.synthesis: "Therefore one must be in subjection, not only to avoid God's wrath but also for the sake of conscience." The motivation for civil obedience is doubled: the fear of punishment (external) and the witness of conscience (internal). Calvin notes that Paul is here removing hypocrisy from the equation: a compliance that is purely fear-based is merely strategic, not genuinely moral. Barnes explains the distinction: subjection from fear is prudential; subjection from conscience is ethical — the Christian is called to the latter. The addition of "for conscience's sake" elevates civil obedience from a mere calculation of consequences to a matter of genuine moral duty — obeying not because disobedience is dangerous but because the authority is God-given and conscience recognizes that fact. Ellicott reads this as the mature Christian position: one who has fully understood that the magistrate is God's servant will obey not merely under compulsion but as an act of integrity before God. Clarke adds that conscience, properly understood, is "the moral sense given by God" — and this moral sense, when functioning correctly, recognizes the legitimacy of properly constituted civil authority. Wesley: both motivations are valid; both are named.

voices.html: Paul removes hypocrisy from the equation: compliance that is purely fear-based is merely strategic, not genuinely moral. The addition of "for conscience's sake" elevates civil obedience from prudential calculation to genuine moral duty — obeying not because disobedience is dangerous but because conscience recognizes the God-given legitimacy of the authority.

"For conscience's sake" — the mature Christian position. One who has fully understood that the magistrate is God's servant will obey not merely under compulsion but as an act of integrity before God. The external fear and the internal conscience point in the same direction — but only the latter is genuine moral obedience.

Subjection from fear is prudential; subjection from conscience is ethical. Paul calls for both — but the "conscience" motivation is the primary one. It is possible to comply without truly obeying if the compliance is only strategic. The Christian's obedience to civil authority is meant to be a genuine moral act, not a calculated avoidance of punishment.

6.synthesis: "For because of this you also pay taxes, for the authorities are ministers of God, attending to this very thing." Paul grounds the specific obligation of tax-paying in the general theological argument he has been making: taxes are the practical expression of the subjection owed to God-ordained authorities. The word for "ministers" here (λειτουργοί) is a different word from v. 4 (διάκονος) — it was used of public servants who performed official religious or civic duties, often at their own expense. Calvin in the broader context reads the payment of taxes as an act of acknowledging the legitimate authority of the civil order — not merely a pragmatic transaction. Barnes notes the tone: the verse reads as a statement of fact ("you do pay taxes") as much as an exhortation, grounding the obligation in the practice Paul's readers already acknowledge. RWP notes the term λειτουργοί: "public servants" — used in the LXX of religious officials and here of civic ones, suggesting the dignity Paul attributes to the magistrate's function. Clarke: their function is continuous ("attending to this very thing" — present participle, ongoing), requiring ongoing financial support.

voices.html: Ministers (λειτουργοί). Public servants — used in the LXX of religious officials and here of civic ones. The dignity of the term suggests that Paul regards the magistrate's function as a genuine service to the community under God. Attending continually (προσκαρτεροῦντες) — the ongoing character of the service grounds the ongoing obligation of tax support.

The verse reads as a statement of fact as much as an exhortation: "you do pay taxes" — grounding the obligation in the practice Paul's readers already acknowledge. The payment of taxes is the specific, tangible expression of the general subjection commanded in vv. 1-5. To honor the principle while evading the practice would be a contradiction.

"Attending to this very thing" — a present participle indicating continuous, ongoing service. The authorities are permanently engaged in maintaining the civil order — a full-time responsibility that cannot be sustained without public financial support. The tax is the price of the order from which all benefit; refusing it while enjoying the order is dishonest.

7.synthesis: "Pay to all what is owed to them: taxes to whom taxes are owed, revenue to whom revenue is owed, respect to whom respect is owed, honor to whom honor is owed." The fourfold list — taxes (φόρους), revenue/customs (τέλη), fear/respect (φόβον), honor (τιμήν) — covers the full range of obligations owed to civil authorities. RWP identifies the two types of financial obligation: φόροι were personal or property taxes levied directly; τέλη were customs levied on goods exported or imported, collected by different officers. Ellicott distinguishes fear and honor: one class of officers could claim respect for their official position even without personal merit (φόβον); another class of persons — perhaps the emperor or senior officials — was owed the deeper respect of honor (τιμή). Calvin reads this verse as a comprehensive summary of the obligations of subjects: they are to hold magistrates in esteem and honor, to obey their edicts, laws, and judgments, and to pay tributes and customs. Barnes notes the simplicity and practicality of the command: pay debts, not only financial but the debts of respect and honor — Christianity does not exempt believers from the social duties they owe to those in authority.

voices.html: Dues (ὀφειλάς) — debts. The fourfold list: φόρος = direct taxes on persons or property; τέλος = customs on goods. Then: φόβον = fear/respect for official position; τιμήν = deeper honor for persons of supreme rank. Paying debts needs emphasis even today, including for ministers.

"Fear... honour" — there would be one class of officers who could claim respect for their official position even without special merit (fear); another, perhaps the emperor or senior officials, owed a deeper honor. The distinction is not arbitrary — it reflects the graduated nature of civil authority and the appropriate graduated response of the Christian subject.

This verse comprehensively summarizes the obligations of subjects toward magistrates: they are to hold them in esteem and honor; to obey their edicts, laws, and judgments; to pay tributes and customs. By the word honor Paul means the reverence owed to those in authority — not merely outward compliance but the inward recognition that their office is legitimate and God-given.

8.synthesis: "Owe no one anything, except to love one another, for the one who loves another has fulfilled the law." Paul pivots from civil to interpersonal obligations with a memorable paradox: love is a debt that can never be fully discharged. RWP: "This debt can never be paid off, but we should keep the interest paid up." Ellicott observes the transition of thought: when you have paid all your other debts — taxes, customs, reverence, honor — there will still be one debt unpaid and unpayable: the debt of love. Calvin reads the transition as Paul having completed his instruction on magistrates and now passing to general duties — and love as the fulfillment of the law covers everything that follows. Wesley identifies love as the one eternal obligation: "An eternal debt, which can never be sufficiently discharged; but yet if this be rightly performed, it discharges all the rest." Barnes specifies: "owe no man anything" does not prohibit contracts or debts of business — it prohibits the attitude of permanent indebtedness toward other people that love precisely is. Clarke makes the practical application: pay all civil and commercial debts in full; the only debt that properly and permanently remains is the debt of love — which, unlike financial debt, grows rather than diminishes when paid.

voices.html: Save to love one another (εἰ μὴ τὸ ἀλλήλους ἀγαπᾶν). "Except the loving one another." This articular infinitive is in the accusative case, the object of ὀφείλετε. This debt can never be paid off, but we should keep the interest paid up. His neighbour (τὸν ἕτερον) — "the other" — extended to any person in need.

To love one another — an eternal debt, which can never be sufficiently discharged. But yet if this be rightly performed, it discharges all the rest. "He that loveth another hath fulfilled the whole law toward his neighbour" — love is not one duty among others but the animating principle from which all other duties flow and by which they are measured.

When you have paid all your other debts — taxes, customs, reverence, honor — there will still be one debt unpaid and unpayable: the debt of love. "Owe no man anything" refers to the cancellable, finite debts of commerce and civility. Love alone is a debt that increases rather than diminishes with payment, and that no accumulation of payments can extinguish.

9.synthesis: "For the commandments, 'You shall not commit adultery, You shall not murder, You shall not steal, You shall not covet,' and any other commandment, are summed up in this word: 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself.'" Paul cites the second table of the Decalogue to show that love is their common root and summary. RWP notes the order of commandments here matches Luke 18:20 and James 2:11, differing from the Hebrew order of Exodus 20. Calvin observes that Paul omits the command to honor parents — not because it is less important but because his focus is on duties toward neighbors, and the parent-child relationship has a character of its own. Wesley reads the summary as total: "If there be any other commandment toward our neighbour... it is summed up in this" — love is not one commandment among others but the one that contains all the others. Barnes: the verse states the moral logic behind v. 8 — the reason love fulfills the law is that the entire moral law toward the neighbor is an elaboration of the single principle of love. Ellicott notes that the summary citation is from Leviticus 19:18, and that Jesus had already identified it as the second great commandment (Matthew 22:39).

voices.html: The commandments cited are from the second table of the Decalogue — those governing human relationships. The summary from Leviticus 19:18 was already identified by Jesus as the second great commandment (Matt 22:39). Paul here shows that all the specific commandments toward the neighbor are specifications of the single principle of love.

"If there be any other commandment toward our neighbour... it is summed up in this." Love is not one commandment among others but the one that contains all the others. To love the neighbor as oneself is to will for them everything that obedience to any specific commandment secures — their life, their fidelity, their property, their honor.

This verse states the moral logic behind v. 8 — love fulfills the law because the entire moral law toward the neighbor is an elaboration of the single principle of love. The specific commandments name particular applications; love names the root from which all the applications grow. Root and applications are inseparable: the root produces the applications; the applications express the root.

10.synthesis: "Love does no wrong to a neighbor; therefore love is the fulfilling of the law." The negative formulation — love does no wrong (κακὸν οὐκ ἐργάζεται) — is as significant as a positive one. RWP: "The filling up or complement of the law" (πλήρωμα νόμου) — love not only keeps the law but fills it up, realizing its full intention. Ellicott: "The form of the Greek word implies not only that love helps a man to fulfil the law, but that in the fact of the presence of love in his heart the law is actually fulfilled." Calvin demonstrates by effect: the person endued with true love will never entertain the thought of injuring others. The whole law forbids doing harm to the neighbor — love's presence makes that entire prohibition unnecessary because love never wills that harm. Barnes draws the social implication: if the law of love were engraven on every heart and practised in every life, what a change would immediately follow — not merely fewer violations but a transformed social order. Wesley adds the positive complement: "The same love which restrains from all evil, incites us to all good." Clarke: love is the fulfillment of the law not as an alternative to keeping its commands but as the inner spring from which all genuine law-keeping flows.

voices.html: "The fulfilling of the law" — the form of the Greek word implies not only that love helps a man to fulfil the law, but that in the fact of the presence of love in his heart the law is actually fulfilled. Love does not merely produce law-keeping as a result; love IS the law's inner reality, the substance of which the specific commandments are the outer form.

He demonstrates by the effect: the person endued with true love will never entertain the thought of injuring others. The whole law forbids doing harm to the neighbor — love's presence makes that entire prohibition unnecessary, because love by its nature never wills the harm that the law forbids. Where love reigns, the law's commands are superfluous in the best sense.

"If the law of love were engraven on every heart and practised in every life, what a change would immediately follow" — not merely fewer violations of specific commandments but a transformed social order. Love is the fulfilling of the law because it does not merely comply with the law's demands but inhabits the reality toward which the law points.

11.synthesis: "And do this, knowing the time, that it is already the hour for you to wake from sleep. For now salvation is nearer to us than when we first believed." The eschatological urgency now sharpens the ethical appeal: we do all this knowing what time it is. RWP explains: the nominative absolute or accusative of general reference (καὶ τοῦτο) is a common idiom for "and that too" — confirming and intensifying what precedes. Wesley: "And do this — fulfil the law of love in all the instances above mentioned. Knowing the season — full of grace, but hasting away." Ellicott: the word for "time" (καιρός) means the critical moment, the loaded hour — not mere clock-time but the significant, eschatologically charged present. The image of waking from sleep appears repeatedly in early Christian eschatological exhortation (1 Thessalonians 5:6-8; Ephesians 5:14). Barnes draws out the imagery: salvation is closer now than at conversion — with every passing moment, the distance to final redemption decreases and the urgency of holy living increases. Calvin reads "salvation" here as the completion of salvation, the consummation that the whole life of faith is moving toward.

voices.html: "The time" (καιρός) — not mere clock-time but the critical, eschatologically loaded moment. There is all the more urgent motive for the law of love because the time is itself urgent. The word for "time" here is different from that in the next clause — this first one means the character of the moment, the second merely the duration.

And do this — fulfil the law of love in all the instances above mentioned. Knowing the season — full of grace, but hasting away. This life, a night; the resurrection, the day; the gospel shining on the heart, the dawn. How beautifully is the metaphor carried on — and how searching the urgency it creates for present faithfulness.

Salvation is closer now than at conversion — with every passing moment the distance to final redemption decreases and the urgency of holy living increases. Calvin reads "salvation" as the completion of salvation, the consummation the whole life of faith is moving toward. The narrowing gap between present and consummation is the eschatological pressure that makes the ethical demands of the chapter urgent rather than optional.

12.synthesis: "The night is far spent; the day is at hand. So then let us cast off the works of darkness and put on the armor of light." The eschatological imagery moves from waking (v. 11) to dawn: the night of the present age is advanced toward its end; the day of Christ's return is near. RWP: "Is far spent" (προέκοψεν, first aorist active of προκόπτω — to cut forward, to advance). "Is at hand" (ἤγγικεν, perfect active — has drawn near and remains near). Ellicott draws out the strange metaphor: it is curious that here, as in 1 Thessalonians 5:8, the metaphor of night and day should suggest the metaphor of armor — the imagery is eschatological (night/day) but its practical application is military (armor/weapons). Calvin reads "the night" as the present state of imperfection and darkness in which believers still live, and "the day" as the full revelation of Christ in glory. Barnes specifies: the works of darkness are described in v. 13 — rioting, drunkenness, lust, strife — and the armor of light is Christ himself (v. 14). Clarke applies this to the specific historical moment: if the text refers to the heathen state of the Romans, the night of paganism is nearly over; if to the church's condition, the approaching return of Christ is the daylight that motivates present holiness.

voices.html: Is far spent (προέκοψεν). First aorist active — to cut forward, to advance, to make progress. Is at hand (ἤγγικεν). Perfect active — has drawn near and remains near. Vivid picture for day-break. Let us cast off (ἀποθώμεθα) — second aorist middle subjunctive of ἀποτίθημι, "to put off" as garments.

"The night... the day" — the time during which the Messiah is absent from his people is compared to night; he is the sun whose coming converts it to day. The metaphor of night and day suggesting armor (as also in 1 Thess 5:8) may seem strange — but the armor of light is what the arriving day requires and enables: full, battle-ready equipment for the Christian life.

The works of darkness are detailed in v. 13 — rioting, drunkenness, lust, strife. The armor of light is Christ himself, as v. 14 will identify. The eschatological metaphor does practical work: the approaching day is not merely a fact to be contemplated but a motive that changes how one lives in the remaining night.

13.synthesis: "Let us walk properly as in the day, not in revelry and drunkenness, not in sexual immorality and debauchery, not in quarreling and jealousy." Paul lists three pairs of vices to be put off: excess in eating and drinking, sexual impurity, and social strife. RWP notes that Paul is fond of the "walk" metaphor (περιπατέω), using it 33 times. "Honestly" (εὐσχημόνως — decorously, becomingly, gracefully) is the positive standard, defined by what one would do if all actions were seen. Ellicott notes that this verse, catching the eye of Augustine, had a great effect in leading to his baptism and transformation. Calvin reads the three pairs as increasingly grievous: self-indulgence, then carnal lust, then envy and contention — each pair names a vice that even worldly people recognize as shameful in the open. Barnes gives the social context: as the day makes all actions visible, so Christian life is to be conducted as though fully seen — not merely avoiding what would shame one in public but actually living as if constantly in the light. Clarke defines "rioting" (κώμοις, originally festive processions) and "drunkenness" (μέθαις) as the cluster of behaviors associated with pagan festival culture that the Roman church was called to abandon.

voices.html: Decorously, becomingly, as men do when their actions are seen. It is interesting to know that this verse, catching the eye of Augustine, had a great effect in leading to his baptism and change of life. The positive standard — walking as in the day — requires not merely the avoidance of darkness but the active cultivation of behavior fitting for God's light.

He mentions three kinds of vices and gives each two names. If these have in them so much filthiness that even carnal people are ashamed to commit them before the eyes of men, how much more ought they to shame us, who walk as in the midst of the day under the eye of God and of his angels!

As the day makes all actions visible, so Christian life is to be conducted as though fully seen — not merely avoiding what would shame one in public but actually living as if constantly in the light of God's presence. The metaphor of day-time behavior is not just an analogy for moral visibility; it is a description of the eschatological reality in which the Christian already lives.

14.synthesis: "But put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh, to gratify its desires." The two imperatives — put on Christ, make no provision for the flesh — complete the armor of light metaphor of v. 12 and name the practical content of the transformation of v. 12:2. Ellicott: "So invest and identify yourselves with the spirit of Christ as to reproduce it in your outward walk and conduct." Wesley: "Put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ — herein is contained the whole of our salvation. It is a strong and beautiful expression for the most intimate union with him, and being clothed with all the graces which were in him." Calvin explains the metaphor: to put on Christ means to be on every side fortified by his power, wisdom, and righteousness — as a garment envelopes the whole person. The negative command — make no provision (πρόνοιαν μὴ ποιεῖσθε) for the flesh — is not asceticism but the refusal to plan for gratification of sinful desires. RWP: "Provision (πρόνοιαν) — old word for forethought. For the flesh (τῆς σαρκός) — objective genitive. Do not plan in advance for the flesh's desires, do not give them a running start." Barnes notes that the putting on of Christ implies both the imputed righteousness of justification and the imparted righteousness of sanctification — the whole of salvation compressed into a single metaphor.

voices.html: "Put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ" — a continuation of the armor metaphor of v. 12. So invest and identify yourselves with the spirit of Christ as to reproduce it in your outward walk and conduct. Make not provision for the flesh — take no thought for the flesh so as to supply a stimulus to its desires. The two commands define the Christian life between them.

Put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ — herein is contained the whole of our salvation. It is a strong and beautiful expression for the most intimate union with him, and being clothed with all the graces which were in him. The apostle does not say, put on purity and sobriety, peacefulness and benevolence — he says, put on Christ, and all these follow as necessary consequences.

Provision (πρόνοιαν) — old word for forethought (from προνόος). In N.T. only here and Acts 24:2. For the flesh (τῆς σαρκός) — objective genitive. Do not plan in advance for the flesh's desires; do not give them a running start by thinking ahead about how to satisfy them.

1.synthesis: Paul opens Romans 14 by addressing the single most destabilizing conflict in the Roman church: whether Gentile believers, free from the Mosaic food laws, should insist on their liberty while Jewish converts retained inherited scruples about clean and unclean meats. The command is clear — 'receive' the weak one, a warm word of active welcome rather than grudging toleration. Ellicott illumines 'weak in the faith' as describing a believer whose mind lacks the integrating power of Christ as sole master-motive; when faith is not yet strong enough to govern conscience by a single great allegiance, petty details fill the space. Calvin stresses that the strong bear the obligation of accommodation, since it is precisely their progress in understanding that creates the asymmetry. Wesley captures the pastoral posture compactly: receive the weak 'with all love and courtesy into Christian fellowship.' The critical restraint Paul adds — 'not to doubtful disputations' — forbids the stronger believer from using the welcome as an occasion to debate and convert the weaker to his own position.

voices.html: They who have made the most progress in Christian doctrine should accommodate themselves to the more ignorant, and employ their own strength to sustain their weakness; for among the people of God there are some weaker than others, and who, except they are treated with great tenderness and kindness, will be discouraged, and become at length alienated from religion.

Weak in the faith — there may be a sincere desire to lead a religious life, and yet the mind is taken up with petty details, each of which is painfully judged by itself, and not by reference to a central principle. The opposite of this is to be 'weak in the faith.'
Him that is weak — Through needless scruples. Receive — With all love and courtesy into Christian fellowship. But not to doubtful disputations — About questionable points.

The converts to Christianity were from both Jews and Gentiles. There were many Jews in Rome; and it is probable that no small part of the church was composed of them. The New Testament everywhere shows that they were disposed to bind the Gentile converts to their own customs, and to insist on the observance of the peculiar laws of Moses.

2.synthesis: Paul illustrates the division between strong and weak by the most tangible marker: what each eats. The 'strong' believer — typically the Gentile convert — is confident that the Mosaic distinctions between clean and unclean meats have no binding claim on the Christian, and so eats freely. The 'weak' believer — typically a Jewish convert — has not yet shed the ingrained reverence for the dietary laws, and so retreats to herbs alone to avoid any taint of forbidden meat. Clarke helpfully notes that Jewish converts living in a Gentile city could not always verify whether meat had been properly slaughtered, and so abstained altogether as the safer conscience. Calvin underscores that Paul's phrasing is descriptive, not prescriptive: he is not commanding the more restricted diet but simply noting the fact of the division. Ellicott focuses on the psychological root: the strong person's 'confidence' is faith large enough to prevent the conscience from becoming uneasy over such distinctions — the difference is not merely custom but the reach of faith into daily life.

voices.html: Both sentences are declarative, announcing a fact respecting two parties: the one believed he might eat all things — the other, who is weak, abstains from things forbidden by the law, and chooses to eat herbs rather than be defiled by contact with what the law prohibits.
Believeth that he may — rather, hath confidence to eat all things. His faith is strong enough to prevent his conscience from becoming uneasy.

Another, who is weak, eateth herbs — Certain Jews, lately converted to the Christian faith, and having as yet little knowledge of its doctrines, believe the Mosaic law relative to clean and unclean meats to be still in force; and therefore, when they are in a Gentile country, for fear of being defiled, avoid flesh entirely, and live on vegetables.

The apostle admits that the Jewish convert was weak — not fully informed of the liberty which the gospel gives; while the strong, whether Gentile or enlightened Jewish convert, was not restrained by any scruples about the lawfulness of certain meats.

3.synthesis: Paul addresses the characteristic failure of each party with surgical economy. The strong — who eat freely — are prone to despise the scrupulous as petty, superstitious, or spiritually immature; Calvin observes that this contempt comes naturally to those who regard such scruples as beneath notice. The weak — who abstain — are prone to judge and condemn the free-eaters as irreligious or careless about holiness; Ellicott notes drily that 'human nature alters very little' in these matters. The silencing argument is the same for both: 'God hath received him.' This is not an appeal to the man's own virtue but to the divine verdict already rendered. Ellicott points out that 'received' (προσελάβετο) means admitted him into God's church at his conversion; the act of divine welcome has already settled a question that neither party is competent to reopen. Wesley draws the application sharp: that welcome places the received person among God's children, and neither contempt nor condemnation from a human judge is appropriate toward a member of the divine household.

voices.html: He wisely and suitably meets the faults of both parties. They who were strong had this fault — that they despised those as superstitious who were scrupulous about insignificant things; these, on the other hand, were hardly able to refrain from rash judgments, so as not to condemn what they did not follow.

The strong despise the weak; the weak judge the strong. In the one case there is contempt for what is thought to be narrowness and pedantry. In the other case censorious judgments are passed on what is regarded as levity and irreligion. Human nature alters very little. God hath received him — admitted him into His Church when he was baptised.
Both being sincere and upright, and acting in the fear of God, are received as heirs of eternal life, without any difference on account of these religious scruples or prejudices.

Despise him that eateth not — As over-scrupulous or superstitious. Judge him that eateth — As profane, or taking undue liberties. For God hath received him — Into the number of his children, notwithstanding this.

4.synthesis: The rebuke in verse 4 extends the servant-master image to its full weight. In the Greek and Roman world, a master's servant was not subject to the discipline of a neighbor's household — interfering with another man's servants was both a social breach and a legal overreach. Calvin applies this precisely: to judge God's servant by your own convictions about meats is exactly this kind of presumption, treating your preferences as law for someone else's household. The question 'who art thou?' is not rhetorical softening but a genuine challenge to assumed standing. Ellicott notes it is directed especially at the weak believer who, carrying over the Jewish tradition of religious authority, assumed a mandate to pronounce on others. The verse does not leave the condemned servant's fate in uncertainty: 'he shall be holden up.' Clarke stresses that God's ability to make even the scrupulous believer stand is the real point — sincere uprightness before God secures the verdict, regardless of what either party thinks about the other's practice.

voices.html: As you would act presumptuously among men were you to bring another man's servant under your own rules, and try all his acts by the rule of your own will; so you assume too much, if you condemn anything in God's servant, because it does not please you; for it belongs not to you to prescribe to him what to do and what not to do.

Who art thou? — This is addressed to the weak. The Apostle indignantly challenges his right to judge. That right belongs to another tribunal, before which the conduct of the stronger Christian will not be condemned but approved and upheld. Holden up — Made to stand — by God himself.

Who has ever given thee the right to condemn the servant of another man? To his own master he standeth or falleth. He is sincere and upright, and God, who is able to make him stand, will uphold him; and so teach him that he shall not essentially err.

The doctrine of this epistle is uniformly that the Jew had no such privilege, but that in regard to salvation he was on the same level with the Gentile. This is a principle of common sense and common propriety: no man has the right to sit in judgment on another man's servant in the matters that belong to his master's household.

5.synthesis: From food, Paul moves to days: the same structure of Jewish scruple and Gentile freedom repeats itself in the observance of festivals. Calvin observes that the converted Jew, raised from infancy with a reverence for Passover, Pentecost, Sabbath, and the rest, could not simply set aside those observances once converted to Christ; the associations were too deep and the law's authority too long established. The Gentile convert, with no such formation, observed no such days and saw no reason to begin. Paul's resolution is not to adjudicate which practice is correct but to locate the authority where it belongs: in each believer's own conscience. The phrase 'fully persuaded in his own mind' (πληροφορείσθω ἐν τῷ ἰδίῳ νοΐ) is the hinge. Wesley makes the application direct: a person should be fully persuaded that a thing is lawful before doing it. Ellicott notes from Galatians and Colossians that day-observance has no absolute apostolic sanction but may have genuine religious expediency — which is a sufficient basis for those who practice it in good conscience.

voices.html: The Jews, who had been brought up from their childhood in the doctrine of the law, would not lay aside that reverence for days which they had entertained from the beginning, and to which they had been accustomed. The Gentile convert, with no such formation, observed no such days and saw no reason to begin.

For the observance of days and seasons, compare Galatians 4:10; Colossians 2:16. From these passages, taken together, it is clear that the observance of special days has no absolute sanction, but is purely a question of religious expediency. That, however, is sufficient ground on which to rest it.

The converted Jew still thought these festivals of moral obligation; the Gentile Christian not having been bred up in this way had no such prejudices. As those who were the instruments of bringing him to the knowledge of God gave him no such instructions, he did not think himself obliged to observe them.
One day above another — As new moons, and other Jewish festivals. Let every man be fully persuaded — That a thing is lawful, before he does it.

6.synthesis: The logic of verse 6 is unexpected and generous: Paul pronounces both the day-keeper and the day-ignorer, both the meat-eater and the herb-only abstainer, acceptable to the Lord — provided both act from genuine conscience toward God and seal the act with thanksgiving. Clarke calls this 'a beautiful apology for mistaken sincerity,' recognizing that Paul does not endorse the theological position of the day-keeping Jew but does honor the intention behind it. Calvin draws the same distinction carefully: the notion that the Mosaic observances remain binding is, in his view, a superstition; but the sincere intention to honor God by observing them can be received by God. The act of giving thanks is the mark of sincerity on both sides. Wesley notes that both the observer and non-observer of days act 'out of a principle of conscience toward God' — the motive, not the menu or the calendar, is what the Lord sees. Ellicott adds that saying grace at meals consecrates the meal to God, making even the act of eating meat a religious act offered in worship.

voices.html: It is necessary to distinguish between the notion which any one may have entertained as to the observance of days, and the observance itself to which he felt himself bound. The notion was indeed superstitious, but the sincere intention to honor God by observing them can be received; for Paul commends only the upright intention, and not the error.
For he giveth God thanks — By the saying of grace at meat, the meal, whatever it may be, is consecrated to God, and he who partakes of it shows that he does so in no irreverent spirit.

A beautiful apology for mistaken sincerity and injudicious reformation. Do not condemn the man for what is indifferent in itself: if he keep these festivals, his purpose is to honor God by the religious observance of them. On the other hand, he who cannot observe them in honor of God does not observe them at all.

Regardeth it to the Lord — That is, out of a principle of conscience toward God. To the Lord he doth not regard it — He also acts from a principle of conscience. He that eateth not — Flesh. Giveth God thanks — For his herbs.

7.synthesis: Verse 7 draws back from the debate about food and days to articulate the controlling principle behind both. No Christian lives to himself or dies to himself — the language of total belonging to another. Clarke notes that Greek usage applied 'living to oneself' specifically to acting on one's own judgment, following one's own preferences as law; Paul's categorical denial of this for the Christian means that every act, however mundane, is referred to a higher will. Calvin presses the argument from whole to part: if the whole of life belongs to God's glory, then it follows that each particular choice — what to eat, which day to observe — is also governed by his claim. This has immediate relevance for the strong believer who wants to assert liberty: the freedom he claims is not self-ownership but stewardship. Wesley is crisp: the Christian is 'not at his own disposal; doeth not his own will.' Barnes notes that no Christian lives to gratify his own inclinations or appetites, making it the very principle distinguishing Christian life from merely natural life.

voices.html: He confirms the former verse by an argument derived from the whole to a part — that it is no matter of wonder that particular acts of our life should be referred to the Lord's will, since life itself ought to be wholly spent to his glory; for then only is the life of a Christian rightly formed, when it has for its object the will of God.

The Greek writers use the phrase ἑαυτῳ ζῃν to signify acting according to one's own judgment, following one's own opinion. Christians must act in all things according to the mind and will of God, and not follow their own wills. God is our master; we must live to him, as we live under his notice and by his bounty.
None of us — Christians, in the things we do. Liveth to himself — Is at his own disposal; doeth his own will.

No Christian lives to gratify his own inclinations or appetites. He makes it his great aim to do the will of God; to subordinate all his desires to his law and gospel; and though one should eat flesh and another abstain, both may be doing the will of God in their respective choices.

8.synthesis: Verse 8 unpacks the claim of verse 7 into its full scope. Whether the Christian lives or dies, he belongs to the Lord — not merely in moments of religious exercise but in the totality of existence. Calvin explains 'living to the Lord' as conforming to Christ's will and designing all things to his glory, not simply performing outward acts of devotion. The verb 'live' here describes the quality and orientation of life, not merely its continuation. Barnes notes that this is the distinguishing mark of the Christian among all people: other men live to gratify themselves, to pursue their own projects and pleasures; the Christian has a different center, doing those things 'which the Lord requires.' The declaration 'we are the Lord's' lands with finality — it is not a conditional membership but a statement of ownership established by Christ's own act, as verse 9 will clarify. For the debate about meats and days, the implication is plain: neither the eating nor the abstaining is the point in itself; the question is whether the act is offered to the Lord.

voices.html: This does not mean only that we perform outward acts of devotion, but that we conform to his will and pleasure, and design all things to his glory. Nor are we only to live to the Lord, but also to die; that is, our death as well as our life is to be referred to his will — he has full authority over our life and our death.

Other men live to gratify themselves; the Christian to do those things which the Lord requires. By the Lord the apostle evidently intends the Lord Jesus, and the truth taught here is that it is the leading and grand purpose of the Christian to do honour to the Saviour — this constitutes his peculiar character.

9.synthesis: The 'for' of verse 9 grounds the total claim of verses 7–8 in a historical event with a stated purpose. Christ died and rose 'to this end' — not incidentally, not merely as an act of love, but with sovereign intent — that he might be Lord of both dead and living. Calvin stresses the purchase: through death and resurrection, Christ acquired authority over us that death itself cannot dissolve; he who lived and died for us has received our whole existence as his peculiar property. Clarke extends the scope: Christ's dominion extends not only over the living but over separate spirits in the intermediate state — all throughout eternity belong to his authority. Barnes notes that Paul does not present this as the only design of Christ's death, but as a main purpose distinctly in view, confirming what precedes: the reason we live and die to the Lord (v. 8) is that the Lord purchased us by doing so himself. Ellicott identifies a textual note: the best manuscripts substitute 'and lived' for the longer 'and rose, and revived,' making resurrection the single enthroning act.

voices.html: In order to prove that we ought to live and to die to the Lord, he shows how rightly Christ claims this power over us, since he has obtained it by so great a price; for by undergoing death for our salvation, he has acquired authority over us which cannot be destroyed by death, and by rising again, he has received our whole life as his peculiar property.

It was through the resurrection that Christ was finally enthroned at His Father's right hand, and that universal dominion was given to Him. The best MSS. substitute simply 'and lived' for the received 'and rose, and revived.'

Christ's power extends equally over both worlds: separate, as well as embodied spirits, are under his authority; and he it is who is to raise even the dead to life: and thus all throughout eternity shall live under his dominion.

The apostle does not say that this was the only design of his death, but that it was a main purpose, or an object which he had distinctly in view — confirming what he had said that in all circumstances we are the Lord's.

10.synthesis: With the universal lordship of Christ established, Paul draws the judicial conclusion: no Christian has standing to judge another, because only Christ is Lord and Judge of all. Calvin identifies this as the specific power in view — not mere moral guidance but the divine authority to pronounce on a person's standing before God, authority received by Christ from the Father and not delegated to any believer. To exercise that authority is to usurp what belongs to Christ alone. Wesley notes that Paul here turns specifically to address the stronger believer ('why dost thou set at nought thy brother?') after addressing the weak in earlier verses, so both parties receive the rebuke. Barnes frames the argument in equity: if all must stand before the same tribunal on the same footing, what grounds remain for one believer to sit in judgment over another? Ellicott flags an important textual variant — the best manuscripts read 'judgment seat of God' rather than 'of Christ,' uniting the divine persons in the single judgment Paul is invoking. The word 'brother' is itself the rebuke: this is someone God has already received.

voices.html: He concludes that it is an unreasonable boldness in any one to assume the power to judge his brother, since by taking such a liberty he robs Christ the Lord of the power which he alone has received from the Father. By the term brother he checks this lust for judging; for the Lord has established among us the right of fraternal relationship.
Judgment seat of Christ — The true reading is of God.

The superstitious are prone to judge, and those who are not superstitious are prone to despise. Both must appear before God's bar: the Christian Jew must answer for his judging; the Christian Gentile for his contempt. It is God's bar, not ours.
Or why dost thou despise thy brother — Hitherto the apostle has addressed the weak brother: now he speaks to the stronger.

Since we are all subjects and servants alike, and must all stand at the same tribunal, what right have we to sit in judgment on others? God has recognised the weak brother as his friend, and he should be regarded by thee as a brother in the same family.

11.synthesis: Paul supports the coming universal judgment with Isaiah 45:23, a text in which Jehovah swears by his own life that every knee will bow and every tongue confess. Barnes draws the significant implication: since in Isaiah the speaker is unambiguously Jehovah (the divine name is used repeatedly and exclusively), Paul's application of the text to Christ's judgment seat in verse 10 constitutes a strong incidental proof of Christ's divinity — one Paul does not argue for but simply presupposes in the quotation. Calvin reads the purpose of the citation differently from mere proof: Paul quotes Isaiah not primarily to establish that a judgment exists (Christians already knew this) but to induce the humility appropriate to those who must stand there. 'Every knee shall bow' levels all distinctions; the scrupulous Jewish convert and the free Gentile believer will both bow in the same posture before the same Lord. Wesley notes that the oath 'As I live' is proper to God alone — no creature possesses 'life infinite and independent' — and that the full scope of the confession awaits its accomplishment at the final judgment.

voices.html: He seems to have quoted this testimony of the Prophet not so much to prove what he had said of the judgment-seat of Christ, which was not doubted among Christians, as to show that judgment ought to be looked for by all with the greatest humility and lowliness of mind; for all flesh must be humbled while expecting that judgment.

As I live — An oath proper to him, because he only possesseth life infinite and independent. It is Christ who is here termed both Lord and God; as it is he to whom we live, and to whom we die. Every tongue shall confess to God — Shall own him as their rightful Lord; which shall then only be accomplished in its full extent.

In Isaiah there can be no doubt that it refers to Jehovah. The speaker expressly calls himself Jehovah, the name appropriate to God alone. In the place before us, the words are applied by Paul expressly to Christ — a strong incidental proof that the apostle regarded the Lord Jesus as Divine.

12.synthesis: The judicial argument of verses 10–11 reaches its practical conclusion: since every one of us must give account of himself to God, no one has the leisure to prosecute a judgment against a neighbor. Calvin notes the elegant verbal pivot Paul has performed: he has forbidden 'judging' in the sense of condemning others (v. 10), and now reminds us that we must ourselves be judged — the same verb, two opposite uses, both landing on the same person. The irony is that those most eager to sit in judgment of their brethren are also those who must give the full account. Clarke applies the conclusion with a practical urgency: let each person take heed to be prepared 'to give up his accounts with joy' — not merely resigned to judgment but glad to face it. Barnes extends the principle beyond the immediate context: while the debate is about the Jewish-Gentile tension over meats and days, the truth that every word, work, and purpose will be exposed and weighed at God's unerring tribunal is universal and reaches every believer in every era.

voices.html: From the various significations of the word to judge, he has aptly drawn two different meanings: in the first place he forbids us to judge, that is, to condemn; in the second place he bids us to judge, that is, to exercise judgment so as not to give offense. It is not lawful for us to usurp the office of judging, who must ourselves submit to be judged.

Every one of us shall give account of himself — We shall not, at the bar of God, be obliged to account for the conduct of each other — each shall give account of himself: and let him take heed that he be prepared to give up his accounts with joy.

In the fearful arraignment of that day, every work and purpose shall be brought forth, and tried by the unerring standard of justice. This is a truth revealed elsewhere, that all men shall give account of their conduct to God — it is here applied to settle the quarrel between believers over meats and days.

13.synthesis: Paul pivots the very word 'judge' that has characterized the weak believer's failure into a positive command: stop judging one another, and instead exercise judgment about how to avoid becoming an obstacle in a brother's path. Ellicott notes that this turning of the word forms the connecting link between the two halves of the chapter; the faculty for judgment, redirected from condemning others to examining one's own conduct, becomes a virtue rather than a sin. Barnes observes that Paul is meeting the judgmental temperament on its own ground: those who cannot resist pronouncing opinions should at least direct their judgment toward something useful — determining how not to cause harm.
